I opened from the button with Q3s, which is a standard steal attempt. However, Elon in the Big Blind has responded with a significant 3-bet that effectively puts him all-in (he has only 100 chips remaining). To call the 1250, I need approximately 40% equity against his range (1250 / 3150). Against a standard 15-16BB shove range from the Big Blind, Q3s typically has about 34-37% equity, as it is dominated by many Aces, better Queens, and any pocket pair. Given that I have a massive chip lead over Elon and there is no need to take a high-variance, negative-EV call with a marginal hand, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in UTG position with 2h 5d, which is a very weak hand. In a 4-handed game, this is an easy fold from first position as it has poor playability and almost no equity against a standard opening or calling range from the other players.

T2o is the worst starting hand in poker with virtually no equity. While position on the button is normally favorable for opening, this hand is too weak to play regardless of position. I'm only facing 100 more to call, but I'd be playing out of position postflop with the worst hand possible against two opponents (Elon in SB with 1950, and Minni in BB with 15425). Even though I'm on the button, the risk-reward here doesn't justify playing such a weak holding. My stack is healthy at 5200, so there's no need to gamble lightly. I'll wait for superior opportunities and preserve my chips for better spots. This is a straightforward fold.

J♣3♣ is a suited hand with straight and flush potential. In the small blind facing folds from UTG and Button, I can complete the bet for just 50 more chips (total 100 into the pot) into a 150-chip pot, getting over 3:1 odds directly plus implied odds. With 19.5BB stack, speculative hands play well heads-up versus the big blind, especially as opponents have been active in pots. Limping preserves fold equity post-flop and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal raising hand. Folding is too tight in this spot; shoving is overkill for this equity.

I have A♠Q♦ in the big blind heads-up against Elon (SB) who just called. This is a strong hand in heads-up play and I want to take the initiative rather than check and play out of position. Elon is short-stacked (~19bb) so a raise both builds the pot when I'm likely ahead and applies pressure to his wide calling/shoving range. I choose a sizing of 400 total (≈4bb) — big enough to discourage speculative calls and extract value from worse Ax/Kx hands, but small relative to my deep stack. If Elon shoves I will call with AQs; if he folds I pick up the 200 pot. Therefore I raise to 400.

Facing a raise to 400 total from Minni in the BB after my SB limp with J♣3♣. Pot is 500, to call 300 more (15.8% of my 19BB stack). While J3s is suited with straight/flush potential and offers ~35-40% equity against a typical BB isolating range (wide due to aggression seen in history), I am OOP postflop with a short stack. Calling commits a significant portion of my stack into a bloated pot where I will struggle to realize equity on paired boards or without hitting strong draws. Implied odds are limited by my stack size and villain's deep stack, but postflop play is awkward. Raising or shoving is overplaying a marginal hand. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in a tournament setting with 150 hands remaining and active opponents. Discipline > speculative calls OOP short.

Q6o is a weak hand that should not be played from UTG (first position), especially against an aggressive field. From the hand history, I can see Minni (small blind) and Flash (big blind) are both aggressive and competitive players. With a 5200 chip stack, I need to preserve chips and play premium hands from early position. The six kicker makes this queen particularly vulnerable, and the risk/reward is unfavorable. Folding is the standard play here - I'll preserve my stack for better opportunities and positions.

Folded to me on the button preflop with 4s5h offsuit. This is a classic steal spot. While the hand is speculative, position on the blinds, 19BB stack, and pot odds make raising profitable due to fold equity. Minni and Flash are deep-stacked but have folded blinds before. Standard raise to 3x BB (300 total) to apply pressure. Folding is too tight; limping is passive and invites multiway pots with a marginal hand.

I’m in the small blind with Qc8c — a playable suited queen. Elon (button) opened to 300 but he’s very short (≈1,600) and Flash (BB) is deep. Q8s is worth defending vs a 3bb button open: I get good pot odds to call and can realize equity with flush/straight potential. 3-betting light here risks an immediate shove from the short stack or creating a bigger multiway confrontation with Flash; I prefer to call and play a flop out of position while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll call the extra 250 (300 total committed).

I am in the Big Blind with Js 2s. Elon (Button) has raised to 3x the big blind, and Minni (Small Blind) has called. While I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to close the action, J2s is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and is easily dominated. Furthermore, Elon is playing a short stack (1600 total), meaning he is likely to commit his remaining chips on the flop, making it difficult to realize equity with a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Flop: As Qs 3h. I hold Qc8c — second pair with a weak 8-kicker and no meaningful backdoor draw. Elon is very short (~1,600) after his preflop open, so any shove from him is likely heavy on Ax and some better Qx that dominate me. If he jams, I'd need roughly 41% equity to justify a call (≈1,600 to win ≈3,900), and second pair is unlikely to have that vs a shove range weighted toward Aces and KQ-type hands. I want to keep the pot small, avoid being forced to call a short-stack shove with a marginal holding, and allow cheap turn/river realization or induce bluffs. Plan: check and reassess — call small c-bets, fold to large polarizing overbets or call/jam only when odds and reads justify it.

Preflop, I raised as a steal from the button with 4s5h, a standard play given position and fold equity against blinds. Minni defended from the SB with a flat call, which suggests a wide range including suited connectors, Ax, Qx, or speculative hands. The flop As Qs 3h is dry but drawy with spade flush potential and no strong made hands beyond top pair. I completely whiffed with no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 2 or 6 unlikely), but backdoor spade and heart draws provide minimal equity and fold equity. Minni's check indicates weakness—likely no Ax or Qx strong enough to bet out, or trapping, but her history shows aggression when strong. With position, 700 pot, and my 1600 stack (~16BB effective vs her deep stack), a continuation bet applies max pressure. Half-pot sizing (350) maximizes fold equity while preserving stack for turns/rivers if called. High likelihood she folds float calls or weak draws Ax. Checking gives her free card on dangerous board and kills my stealing narrative.

I have Qc8c on As Qs 3h — second pair with a weak 8-kicker and only a remote backdoor club possibility. Elon is very short (~1.25k) and has c-bet ~350 into a ~700 pot (half-pot). Folding would be too nitty since I beat a lot of Kx/Jx and bluffs; raising is unattractive because it either commits me or induces an immediate shove from the short stack. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and gives me the right odds to see a turn (350 to win ~1,400 ≈ 20% required). If Elon jams his remaining stack on a later street I’ll re-evaluate but will often fold unless I improve or the shove looks light. Plan: call the 350, see the turn, and fold to large polarized action unless I pick up a better hand.

Board As Qs 3h Jd; I hold Qc8c — second pair with a weak 8-kicker and only a remote club backdoor. Elon is very short (~1,250) and has shown aggression (open preflop, small c-bet 350 on the flop). Betting here risks inducing an immediate shove from his short stack or bloating a pot I’m usually behind in (many Ax and better Qx still in his range). Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity cheaply, and gives me the option to fold to a polarized shove where I likely don’t have the required equity (~32% to call an all-in for ~1,250 into a 1,400 pot). If he barrels a small bet I can call to catch up or fold to clear shove; if he checks back I get a free river to improve. Therefore I check.

Preflop steal raise with 4s5h got called by Minni from SB, which widened her range to include Ax, Qx, suited connectors, draws, or floats. Flop AsQs3h missed me completely but I c-bet for fold equity and story consistency, sizing small into growing pot. She check-called, suggesting some showdown value like weak Ax, Qx, or spade draw. Turn Jd is a blankish card that completes some broadway straights (KT) or gutshots but doesn't hit my nothing hand—no pair, no flush draw (backdoor spades irrelevant now), no straight draw. Pot 1400 with my remaining 1250 (~12.5BB effective). Minni checks again, which could be weakness or trap, but her history shows she's sticky, calling large bets multi-street with marginal hands (e.g., Hand 5,15). Shoving as bluff risks my stack against her deep stack and likely portion of range that calls (top pair+, draws with equity). Pot odds for her call are favorable (~2:1), and she beats my bluff 100%. Checking preserves stack for better spots, takes free river card (hoping for brick and her check-back), or allows fold to her river bet. No value bet possible, low fold equity vs calling station. Pot control and survival in tournament with short stack prioritizes checking here over spew.

I have Qc8c on A♠ Q♠ 3♥ J♦ A♥ — second pair with a very weak kicker and no real backdoor. Pot is 1,400 and Elon is very short (~1,250). His line (open pre, a small c-bet on the flop, then check on the turn) can contain Ax (Ad/Ac => trips on this river), Qx (including QJ two‑pair), and a mix of bluffs. The river Ace significantly weakens my hand: many hands that beat me (trip A, two pair, better Qx) are now plausible. Betting here for thin value is unlikely to get called by worse very often and will frequently induce a shove from the short stack, which I would need roughly 32% equity to call (1250/(1400+2*1250) ≈ 32%) — I don’t have that equity. I prefer to keep the pot small and go to showdown. Plan: check and show down; I’ll only continue vs a small river bet depending on sizing/reads and will fold to a large shove.

River Ah pairs the board's ace, creating a paired board (As Ah) that improves Ax hands and potentially some straight draws (e.g., KTs for broadway), but it's a complex card that doesn't help my complete air (4s5h: no pair, no flush, no straight). Preflop steal got defended wide by sticky Minni (flat-call SB), flop check-call suggests Ax, Qx, pairs, or draws. Both checked turn Jd (straight completer), preserving pot at 1400. With ~9BB left (1250), deep-stacked Minni is a calling station per history (multi-street calls with marginal hands like Hand 5,15,26). Bluff-betting commits stack with poor fold equity—her range includes too many Ax (now trips or two-pair possible), Qx (TPTK), 10x (straights), and she calls light. Pot odds favor her calls (~2:1+), and she beats bluffs 100%. Checking maximizes EV: free showdown (unlikely to win but avoids spew), preserves short stack for better spots in tournament (34/150 hands, goal maximize stack), and consistent with turn check for pot control/story of missed draw. No value bets possible. Fold equity too low vs her style.

Short-stacked at 12.5BB UTG in a 4-handed game with 54s, a speculative suited connector but too weak from early position. It has straight and flush potential but low equity against calling ranges from deep-stacked opponents likely to defend wide. Limping is poor with short stack; min-raising commits too much without fold equity. Shoving risks being called by better hands like Ax, pairs, or better suited connectors, with poor implied odds post-flop due to stack size. Folding preserves stack for superior shoving spots like premium pairs, broadway, or stronger suited aces/connectors later, maximizing EV over remaining 115 hands.

I’m on the button with Q♦9♣ in a 4-handed game — a good spot to open. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; with position and fold equity I want to take the initiative rather than just limp. Standard sizing at this table has been ~3x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 300 to steal the blinds/isolate the BB and play postflop in position. Claude (BB) is shorter (≈51bb) and often calls; Flash (SB) is deep and can 3‑bet. Plan: if I get single‑raised call (i.e., called) I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and use position; if I face a sizable 3‑bet or an all‑in shove from BB I’ll fold Q9o (not strong enough to call large 3‑bets/all‑ins). Raise to 300 is the balanced, +EV play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5d, a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a standard play for an aggressive player, but my hand lacks the strength or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

K3o (King-Three offsuit) is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind. While Minni is clearly an aggressive button raiser, I'm out of position with a hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. With my medium-short stack of 5100, I should be selective about which spots I take. K3o doesn't meet the threshold for a profitable call against an aggressive opponent from an out-of-position spot. Minni has shown consistent button raising patterns and post-flop aggression, so I'd likely be facing further pressure if I call and miss. This is a standard fold scenario - I'll preserve chips for better opportunities.

8♥3♥ UTG in a 4‑hand game is a very weak hand. Out of position against three opponents, it has poor showdown equity and limited playability even though it’s suited. Calling 100 (limp) to see a flop hands me a low‑equity situation and inviting raises/3‑bets; open‑raising to 200 as a steal is too loose from UTG and risks a shove from the short‑stacked BB (Elon, 1,150) or a strong defense from the SB (Claude, 5,050) or Button (Flash). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with 9-5 offsuit. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, particularly since Elon is very short-stacked in the big blind (1,150 chips), this hand is quite weak. Elon is likely looking for a spot to shove any reasonable hand, and Claude is also fairly short. I will fold this hand and look for a better spot to utilize my chip lead or leverage against the shorter stacks.

Q7o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant getting involved, even against a short stack. While Elon's desperation is noted, I should maintain discipline with marginal hands. Losing the 50 SB is acceptable rather than potentially getting into an unfavorable spot. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings.

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. Ace-Six offsuit (A6o) is typically at the bottom of a standard opening range in this position. However, with Elon sitting on a very short stack of 1,250 (12.5 BB) in the Small Blind, he is likely looking for any reasonable hand to shove. Raising to 300 (3 BB) forces the issue. If Elon shoves, the pot odds will likely dictate a call, but A6o doesn't perform exceptionally well against a typical shoving range. Despite this, folding an Ace 4-handed can be too tight. I will raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or define the hand pre-flop, prepared to evaluate if the short stack jams or if the big stack (Minni) defends.

Folding 9-4 offsuit against UTG raise. This hand lacks the strength to continue, especially with two aggressive players (Elon and Minni) still to act behind me. Early position raises should be respected with weak hands. Better spots will come.

Facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 (3x BB) with 6d2h, one of the worst possible hands (offsuit, unconnected, low cards). My stack is only 1250 (~12.5BB), and I'm OOP postflop in SB. Pot odds to call 250 into 700 are poor (~1.8:1), and with trash equity, no reason to continue. No 3-bet value or bluff justification given opponent's position and deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

Flash opened to 300 from UTG in a 4‑handed game — that range is relatively strong. I have T♦7♣ offsuit: a weak, often‑dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability out of position. The pot is 450 and calling 200 (to 300 total) would make the pot 650, so I need roughly 200/650 ≈ 30.8% equity to break even. T7o has significantly less equity vs a UTG open and will be difficult to play OOP. 3‑betting as a bluff here is too thin (min raise to 500) and unnecessarily commits chips against a strong opener. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left to play, the +EV, lowest‑variance line is to fold and wait for a better spot.

T♠J♦ is a solid Broadway hand in early position. While UTG is the worst positional spot, this hand merits aggression with proper sizing. I'm raising to 300 (3x the big blind), which is a standard, confident bet. This accomplishes several things: 1) It builds the pot with a hand I'm confident in, 2) It gains information about opponent holdings, and 3) It puts pressure on the deeper-stacked blinds (Minni and Flash) while also potentially picking up the small blind immediately. Elon on the button with only 1250 is not an immediate concern for this raise size. My 5050 stack is healthy enough to play this hand aggressively.

With 12.5BB effective stack on the button, facing a standard UTG raise from Claude to 300 into a 450 pot. Holding 76s, suited connectors with excellent playability, position, and decent equity (~38-42% vs typical UTG calling range). At this depth, push/fold is optimal strategy. Shoving applies max pressure, leverages fold equity (Claude unlikely to call wide with 1250 into 1700), and when called, we have live equity and flush/straight potential. Calling commits 25% of stack awkwardly postflop, especially multiway risk from blinds. Folding a playable hand OOP vs early raiser wastes button value and short stack pressure. Shove maximizes EV for stack growth.

I will call Elon's all‑in. I have Kc Qd in the small blind facing a button shove to 1,250 after Claude opened to 300. Calling costs me 1,200 more and the pot would be ~2,900, so my break‑even equity is ~41.4% (1,200 / 2,900). KQo has solid equity vs a button shove range (broadways, many pairs and some light shoves), so this is +EV. Folding would be too tight — I’d be giving up good equity for a small cost — and 3‑betting to 2,200 to “isolate” isn’t attractive because Elon is already all‑in (I can’t extract more from him) and it unnecessarily bloats the pot and may induce a costly call from Claude. I prefer the cleaner, cheaper call to realize my equity; after calling I’ll re‑evaluate if Claude elects to come along.

I am holding pocket deuces (2s 2d) in the Big Blind. The action before me is very heavy: Claude raised UTG, Elon 3-bet to 1250, and Minni cold-called the 3-bet from the Small Blind. Pocket deuces are primarily a set-mining hand, meaning I only want to see a flop if the price is right and implied odds are high. Calling 1150 into this pot represents a significant portion of the effective stacks (about 7-8%), and I am out of position against three players who have shown extreme strength. Furthermore, Claude still has to act behind me and could potentially shove his remaining 4750 chips. Given the high probability of being dominated or forced to fold on most flops, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

TJ is a decent broadway hand, but folding is the correct decision here. While TJ has reasonable equity against Elon's short-stack all-in range, I'm facing a multiway pot where Minni's call from the SB likely indicates stronger holdings. Committing 950 chips (20% of my 4750 stack) in this spot is too risky for a marginal hand. The hand is early in the tournament (38/150), and I should preserve my stack to make aggressive plays from better positions with stronger holdings. Folding now protects my capital and allows for better opportunities.

AsJs is a premium UTG opening hand: suited ace with good high card strength and playability. Standard play is to raise 3x the BB to 300, which is a common sizing observed in prior hands. This builds the pot, takes initiative, and has good implied odds given opponent stack depths (Minni and Flash very deep, Claude medium). My 29bb stack supports postflop flexibility. Limp or fold is suboptimal.

I’m on the button with K♦3♥ facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450). K3o is a weak, often-dominated hand, but being on the button and with a relatively small open size gives me good postflop options. Calling 300 keeps the pot manageable, lets me use position to fold to strong aggression if I miss, and preserves my big stack to apply pressure later. A raise here would be too loose and could put me in a tough spot against stronger ranges or a shove from Elon’s shorter stack; folding is too passive given the price and positional advantage. I’ll call and proceed cautiously on the flop unless I hit or get favorable reads.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 7d. Elon (UTG) opened to 300 and Minni called on the Button. Although I am getting decent pot odds (250 to call into a 750 pot), 87 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an UTG range and a wide Button caller. Additionally, Elon and Claude are playing relatively short stacks, increasing the likelihood of commitment situations or shoves that 87o cannot profitably call. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my chip lead.

With pocket nines in the big blind facing two opponents (short-stacked Elon and aggressive Minni), raising to 800 is the correct play. This move: (1) takes control of the pot while I'm likely ahead, (2) applies pressure to Elon's short stack, (3) builds the pot when my hand is strong, and (4) allows me to play the hand positively rather than reactively. The pot odds favor the call at 3.75:1, but the raise generates more value and aggression when I hold a solid hand. With my medium pair, I want to build equity now rather than see cheap flops against multiple opponents.

AsJs is a strong hand facing a BB 3-bet after my UTG open and a loose Button call from Minni. My 26bb stack makes postflop play OOP multiway risky, especially with Claude's aggressive history but fold to bigger bets (e.g., Hand 38). Calling commits ~20% of stack into a bloated pot without initiative. A min-raise to 1300 is too small, inviting multiway action. Shoving 2600 total maximizes fold equity against Claude's likely wide 3-bet range (which AsJs crushes: blocks AA, dominates Ax/broadways), poor pot odds for Minni's calling range (loose holdings per history). History shows opponents fold to large aggression (e.g., multiple folds to big bets/shoves). This denies equity, builds stack aggressively early in tournament (Hand 39/150), aligning with goal to maximize stack size.

I cold‑called 300 on the button with K♦3♥, but Claude’s 3‑bet to 800 followed by Elon’s 4‑bet to 2600 creates a very hostile pot. K3o is frequently dominated by typical 3‑bet/4‑bet ranges (AA/KK/QQ/TT/AK/AQ) and has poor equity. Calling an extra 2300 (~15.5% of my stack) or raising to the 4400 minimum would commit chips in a marginal spot against likely strong holdings; the 300 already in is a sunk cost. I’ll fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better, more profitable spot in position.

With pocket nines facing a short-stacked re-raise from Elon, calling 1,800 (total investment 2,600) is the correct play. Pocket nines is a premium hand that's likely ahead of Elon's shoving range, and the pot odds of approximately 3.08:1 are favorable. Given the short-stack dynamics and heads-up situation post-Minni fold, seeing the flop with one of my strongest starting hands is the profitable line. The risk-reward is asymmetrically in my favor here.

With Elon all-in and Minni/Flash folded, this is a heads-up situation where Elon cannot call additional bets. My pocket 9s are likely ahead of his short-stack range, so there's no value in betting and we should simply run out the remaining streets. Checking here is the standard play - it costs nothing and allows the hand to proceed to the turn and river where my hand will either win or lose. I'm playing for maximum value by not over-betting when I can't extract additional chips.

Heads-up on the flop with AsJs on 4c Ts Tc board. I have the nut spade flush draw (10 clean outs) plus ace-high showdown value and backdoor straight potential. Claude checked as OOP after calling my preflop 4-bet shove, representing a strong range (AA, KK, AK, QQ/JJ/TT, suited broadways). However, the paired board caps his range somewhat and hits my blockers. With only 300 chips left (5% pot), pot odds are massive; shoving denies equity to his worse hands (overpairs, AK/AQ without spade), extracts value from any weaker pairs/draws he might have, and maximizes fold equity while realizing my high equity (~35-45% vs his range). Checking risks giving free cards to overpairs or straight draws. History shows Claude aggressive but capable of folding (e.g., Hand 38 preflop fold). Small shove aligns with tournament goal to build stack aggressively.

Elon is all-in for 300 on the flop with pocket 99. The pot odds are approximately 19.5:1 (5850:300), meaning I only need ~5% equity to call profitably. My pocket 9s are very likely ahead of Elon's all-in shove range - even accounting for the paired board (Ts Tc), my hand beats most non-T-X holdings. With 2150 remaining in my stack, calling 300 is trivial relative to the pot size and the high probability my hand is currently best. This is a standard call. Since Elon is all-in, the hand will proceed to showdown after my call, with no further betting action possible.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand in this position. I will put in a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament. This puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the pot with a premium hand.

Folding 3c9s in Small Blind. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Being short-stacked (7950 chips), I should reserve my chips for premium hands. While I have roughly 35% equity against Flash's wide button raising range, the hand's poor post-flop playability and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold. Better spots will come.

Flash opened to 300 from the button; I'm in the big blind with Qc7s. The pot is 450 and calling 200 more (300 total put-in) would make the pot ~650, giving roughly 3.25:1 on the call (I need ~23% equity). Q7o is marginal but has decent heads-up equity vs a button raising range and can hit top pair or backdoor draws. With deep stacks I don't want to commit with a thin 3-bet bluff out of position, nor fold a hand that can realize equity cheaply. I'll call to see a flop and then play based on texture — continue with a pair/draw, keep pot control with misses and fold to heavy aggression.

I have Qc7s — top pair (sevens) with a decent queen kicker on a 7♦-4♣-5♦ flop. Pot is 650 and stacks are deep (~14.6k vs 16.8k). The board is low and somewhat dry but with two diamonds so the opener can have diamond draws; I'm out of position. This is a hand to control the pot rather than bloat it OOP, so I will check. Plan: check-call a standard small-to-medium c-bet to realize equity and deny draws odds, check down if villain checks, and fold or sharply re-evaluate if I face large raises/overbets that indicate a made hand or committed range.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit on a low, connected flop of 7-4-5 with a diamond draw. While I have two overcards, this board texture generally favors the Big Blind defender's range, which contains many more small pairs and connectors. Since I have position and decent showdown value with Ace-high, checking back is a prudent play to control the pot size and see a turn card for free. This allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch an Ace or Jack on the turn without risking a check-raise on a board that hits my opponent's range well.

I have top pair (7s) with a very good queen kicker on a low, relatively dry board (7♦-4♣-5♦-2♣). Flash checked the flop and has shown no aggression, so his range is wide and contains a lot of worse pairs (5x, 4x, small pocket pairs), some air, and a few disguised made hands. I want to extract value from those worse pairs and deny free rivers to straight-completing cards (3 or 6) or any two-card flush combos. Betting also gives me information and fold equity if he was floating with air. I’ll fire a small-to-medium value bet ~1/2 pot to accomplish that while keeping the pot manageable OOP: 325 chips. Plan: if called, control the river (check most blanks, value-bet thinly on safe rivers); if raised big on the turn I will heavily favor folding since a large raise polarizes to hands that beat me.

On a low, connected board of 7-4-5-2, my Ace-high still holds significant showdown value in a 3-handed game. Minni's lead of half the pot on the turn could represent a small made hand, a straight draw, or a flush draw. With Ace-Jack, I have two overcards that could give me the best hand on the river if I pair up, and I may already be ahead of her draws. The pot odds of 3-to-1 (calling 325 to win 1300) make this a standard call to see the river and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

I hold Qc7s — top pair with a very good queen kicker on a runout 7♦‑4♣‑5♦‑2♣‑J♦. I led the turn for 325 and was check‑called, which narrows Flash’s range toward medium‑strength one‑pair hands and floats (5x/4x/2x, Kx/Qx, some Jx and the occasional two‑diamond combo that would now have a flush). The river J♦ is somewhat dangerous because it gives Jx a higher pair and completes flushes for two‑diamond holdings, but those two‑diamond combos are rare and Flash has shown a passive, calling line. I want thin value from worse single pairs while keeping the size small enough to avoid bloating the pot when I’m behind, so I’ll bet about half the pot (650). If I get check‑raised I'll give it credit and fold most of the time; if he calls I’ll go to showdown and re‑evaluate his tendencies.

On the river, I have improved to Top Pair, Top Kicker with my Jack of Clubs. The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (3-6 or A-3) and a completed diamond flush, but Minni's betting line (checking the flop and then leading for half-pot on both the turn and river) often represents a marginal made hand, a semi-bluff that hit the Jack, or a missed straight draw. Given that we are 3-handed and I have the best possible kicker for my pair of Jacks, my hand is too strong to fold. However, a raise would likely only be called by hands that beat me (flushes, straights, or sets), so calling is the most profitable decision to capture value from her potential bluffs or weaker pairs like J-9 or 7-x.

With Q8o on the button, I'm getting 3:1 odds (100 to call into 150 already in pot). Position is a significant advantage here - I'll act last postflop and can control the action. While Q8 is not a premium hand, it has reasonable playability with two cards that can make top pair or contribute to straight draws. Both Minni and Flash have shown aggressive tendencies, but calling allows me to see a flop and exploit my positional advantage. If the board misses me badly or shows heavy aggression, I can fold. If I flop a queen, an eight, or a draw, I have multiple ways to play the hand profitably. A raise with this hand would be too aggressive against two active opponents with healthy stacks.

I have Qc Jh in the small blind facing a limp from the button and the big blind posted 100. The pot is already 250 and I only need to put in 50 more — excellent pot odds (roughly 5:1) to see a flop. QJ offsuit is too strong to fold here but not strong enough to inflate the pot out of position against two players. A min-raise to 200 would be a small isolation raise that likely keeps callers and can just as easily induce a 3-bet from Flash; with deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity post‑flop. I’ll call 50 more (100 total committed) and play the flop cautiously — look to continue with top pair/strong draws and otherwise concede to aggression.

I am in the Big Blind holding King of Hearts and Six of Clubs. Claude limped from the Button and Minni completed from the Small Blind. With a hand like King-high, checking is a standard and safe play to see a flop for free in a multi-way pot, especially since the hand isn't strong enough to comfortably value-bet or isolate two opponents effectively.

Board: 3h K♠ Jc. I hold Qc Jh — a pair of jacks with a queen kicker (second pair). This is a tricky multiway flop out of position: both opponents limped preflop so their ranges contain a lot of Kx and broadways that dominate or have decent equity against me. The flop is fairly dry (no flush draws) and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a hand that is often behind. Pot control is the priority: check to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and let opponents bluff or reveal strength. Plan: if checked to me I’ll check behind and re-evaluate on the turn; if someone bets a small size (roughly ≤½ pot ≈ 150) I will call to see a turn; fold to a large bet or a check‑raise that represents Kx/sets. If I improve to two pair/trips on the turn I will start building the pot.

I have top pair with a King on a relatively dry board of 3-K-J. Since this was a limped pot pre-flop, it is highly probable that my hand is currently the strongest. Minni has checked to me from the small blind. I will place a bet of half the pot to extract value from middle pairs like Jacks, or straight draws like Q-T or T-9, while also protecting my hand and preventing my opponents from seeing a free turn card.

Calling with Qs8c. While my equity (24%) is slightly below the 25% break-even for 3:1 odds, implied odds justify the call. Flash's aggressive profile and tendency to c-bet bluff, combined with my excellent position and Minni's likely fold, create a profitable situation. If I improve to top pair (Queen) on the turn, I expect strong payoff. The 150 chip cost is minimal relative to my 7850 stack and the potential value of the implied odds from this aggressive opponent.

Call 150 more (250 total). I have second pair (Jacks) with a Q kicker — decent showdown value but vulnerable to Kx and stronger Jx/broadway hands that are common here after limp‑calls. Flash's bet is small (≈25% of the pot) and Claude's call keeps a lot of those stronger hands and draws in; raising out of position into two players would bloat the pot and is thin for value or fold equity. The call is cheap (pot odds ≈20%) and allows me to control the pot size and realize equity. Plan: see the turn and re-evaluate — if I improve to two pair/trips or get action from weak hands I can build; if a King or heavy aggression shows up I will fold to large pressure to protect my stack.

Turned a very strong but not invulnerable hand: QcJh gives me two pair (Queens and Jacks) on 3h–Ks–Jc–Qh. Both opponents showed interest on the flop (Flash raised, Claude called) so their ranges include lots of Kx, broadways and some pocket pairs — some of which (KK, QQ, JJ, 33, KQ, KJ, 10‑9/A‑10 straights) can beat me, but most single‑pair hands and worse pairs are behind. With two opponents and deep stacks I want to extract value from top‑pair and single‑pair hands while keeping the pot controllable and avoiding bloating it into a shoveable spot against potential trips/straights. I’ll bet about half the pot (375) — large enough to get value from Kx, Qx, worse jacks/pairs, and to charge any backdoor draws, but small enough to keep worse hands calling. If I face a large, committed raise I’ll concede (fold) since that line usually represents trips/Kx combos that beat me; small raises I will re‑evaluate (call and go to the river or fold to extreme pressure).

I hold top pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (Six). The turn card is a Queen, which is a significant scare card as it completes a potential straight (Ten-Nine) and improves ranges that include Queen-Jack or King-Queen. Minni has led out for 375 into a pot of 750, which is a half-pot bet. While my hand has weakened relative to the board, I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds to call. In a limped pot, top pair is generally too strong to fold to a single bet on the turn, even with a weak kicker and a coordinated board. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card and my opponents' actions.

Calling with top pair (Qs) on a King-Jack-Queen board against two aggressive opponents. I'm getting 6:1 pot odds (2250 to call 375), which is excellent for my holding. My hand improved significantly from the flop. While the K on board is concerning and Minni's turn raise suggests strength, the pot odds justify calling to see the river. This keeps both opponents in the pot and allows me to evaluate the final board card before committing more chips. Folding top pair with these odds would be too passive.

I have QcJh — two pair (Qs & Js) on 3h–Ks–Jc–Qh–5d. This is a strong showdown hand vs single-pair and many worse holdings, but vulnerable to a number of hands that beat me (KQ/KJ two-pair, pocket QQ/JJ/KK trips, or the rare straight from AT or T9). Both opponents checked the river after calling my turn bet, which often signals weakness or medium-strength hands that will call a modest value bet rather than lead out. In a 3‑way pot I want to extract value from worse pairs and Qx/Jx hands while keeping the pot from bloating into a shoveable spot against the combos that beat me. A small-to-medium value bet (~1/3 pot) maximizes calls from worse hands and charges missed draws, while minimizing the likelihood and cost of a check-raise from a nut/near-nut hand. If I face a large raise I will strongly discount my hand (likely fold to committed shove), but I expect this sizing to be called by the hands I beat. Bet 1/3 pot (625).

I hold top pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (Six). The board is highly coordinated (3-K-J-Q-5), and a straight (Ten-Nine) or two pair (KQ, KJ, QJ) is very possible given the pre-flop limping. Minni has led into two opponents on both the turn and the river, which typically indicates significant strength. However, the river bet size is small (625 into 1875, offering 4-to-1 pot odds). Given that it was a limped pot, Minni could be betting a weaker King or a Queen for value, or even a missed draw. While my hand is marginal, the price is too good to fold top pair. I will call to see the showdown, though I must be wary of Claude acting behind me.

Calling on the river with top pair (Queens) and 8 kicker. Pot odds are 5:1, requiring only ~17% equity to break even. Against Minni's aggressive betting pattern (shown throughout the tournament to bet both strong hands and bluffs) and Flash's calling pattern (likely King or Jack), my top pair has sufficient equity. Minni's river raise after turn aggression could easily be a bluff given her play history. The small relative investment (625 chips on a 7325 stack) and excellent pot odds justify the call.

3-handed on the button with pocket 5s. Standard play is to open-raise: I want to take the initiative, steal the blinds, and play the hand in position. A 3x BB open to 300 is my usual sizing here (consistent with table history), keeps the pot controllable and preserves fold equity. Claude (BB) is relatively short (6600) and may shove, but pocket fives have reasonable equity vs a wide shove and I can comfortably call if required. If called, I’ll look to set-mine or c-bet based on the flop texture. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit (Qh Tc). In a 3-handed game, this hand is strong enough to defend against a standard 3x button open from Minni. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds (calling 250 to win a total of 700+) make this a clear call. 3-betting is an option, but with Claude in the Big Blind sitting on a shorter stack (66BB), I'd prefer not to bloat the pot and face a potential squeeze shove. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my stack depth post-flop.

I'm in the Big Blind with 7d-2s, the worst starting hand in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button and Flash called 250 from the small blind. I'm facing a 200 chip call to continue.

With 7-2o, this is a clear fold situation. Despite getting approximately 4.5:1 pot odds (900 to call 200), my hand has virtually no value. Two players are already committed ahead of me, and 7-2 is dominated by virtually any hand range these opponents would have. 

My stack of 6,600 is healthy enough that I don't need to gamble with the worst hand. I should preserve chips and wait for premium hands. This is a textbook fold.

I have missed the flop completely on an Ace-high board that significantly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. With no pair, no draw, and being out of position, I have no reason to lead into the aggressor. I will check and likely fold to any standard continuation bet, as I lack the equity to continue in this pot.

I was the preflop aggressor and I’m in position, so I should try to take the initiative while keeping the pot manageable. With pocket fives on A♠ 7♠ 6♥ I’m an underpair with only two immediate outs (very low raw equity to improve), so I don’t want to bloat the pot in a marginal spot. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) pressures many of the SB’s missed broadway and Kx hands and can fold out pure floats, while still preserving stack and allowing me to fold to significant resistance. If Flash raises big I’ll release (my equity is too poor to call large raises); if he just calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check and evaluate unless I hit a 5 or a very favorable texture). Bet 250 into 700 (small c-bet, preserve fold equity and control pot).

The flop of As 7s 6h is very favorable for Minni's button-opening range, which contains many Aces. I have completely missed the board with Queen-Ten offsuit, holding no pair and no meaningful draws. Even though Minni has offered a small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot, calling here out of position with virtually no equity would be a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with pocket sevens, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a significant stack and want to exert pressure while building a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential or can win the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

Raising to 800 with premium AK from small blind after Flash's button raise. This is a standard 3-bet that represents strength, builds the pot with a premium hand, and takes the initiative. My stack (6550) supports this raise comfortably, and AK plays well both as value and semi-bluff. The raise puts pressure on both opponents and allows me to win the pot preflop or play from a strong position postflop.

Button opened to 300 and SB 3‑bet to 800. I’m in the big blind with J♥2♣ — a weak, offsuit, poorly connected hand that’s heavily dominated by both the opener’s and 3‑bettor’s ranges. Pot is 1200 and it costs me 700 to continue, so I’d need ~37% equity to break even — J2o has far less vs these ranges and will be very difficult to play out of position. A min‑raise as a bluff is risky here (two opponents, SB’s 5800 stack and likely strength). Preserving a healthy stack and waiting for a better spot is the right play. Fold.

I have pocket sevens on the button and opened to 300. Claude has 3-bet to 800 from the small blind. With a medium pocket pair in a 3-handed game, 77 is too strong to fold to a single 3-bet, especially with position. Claude's stack is around 58 big blinds, so I have enough implied odds to set-mine, and I can also win the hand post-flop if the board texture is favorable or if Claude checks. I will call the 500 more to see a flop in position.

Top pair, best kicker on a favorable flop. I raised preflop with AK and maintained initiative. Flash called weakly from the button with a marginal holding. I need to bet for value here while building the pot with my premium hand. A bet of 900 (about 52% of the pot) is appropriately sized - strong enough to get value from medium-strength hands, weak enough to keep Flash in the hand with draws and lower pairs. This hand requires aggression to maximize value.

The flop of 8c-As-6s is quite unfavorable for my pocket sevens, particularly after Claude 3-bet pre-flop. An Ace is a primary component of a small-blind 3-betting range, and Claude is leading out with a substantial continuation bet of 900 into a 1700 pot. My hand is currently third pair and is likely 'way behind' Claude's range, which includes better Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and higher pocket pairs (88, 99, TT, JJ). With no spade in my hand and no significant draw, there is little reason to continue and risk a larger portion of my stack on later streets when Claude's remaining stack makes a turn shove highly probable. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker and has no realistic winning potential. Even though I'm on the button with good position and the cost to call is only 100 chips, this hand lacks the equity and playability needed to justify entering the pot. Folding weak hands from late position helps maintain a strong aggregate strategy and preserves chips for better opportunities. This is a standard fold.

Heads-up in the small blind with pocket sixes is a clear raise spot — I have a made hand with good set-mining value and, in heads-up the small blind is the button so I’ll have position postflop. Limping cedes initiative and gives the BB a cheap chance to see a flop with a wide range; opening protects my equity and extracts value from worse hands. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total). If called I’ll play the flop in position; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll be prepared to fold to extreme pressure but can call smaller 3-bets to set-mine given our deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 4s. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I would have position on Minni post-flop, 84o is an exceptionally weak hand that does not play well even with a positional advantage. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds, but the hand's lack of connectivity and high-card value makes it a clear fold. I prefer to wait for better spots to defend against Minni's frequent opens.

3-handed on the button with Kc8c (suited King) — a clear open-raise spot. I want to take the initiative and use position to play post-flop, so I’ll make a standard 3xBB open (300). This sizing gives fold equity, builds a pot when I have position, and is consistent with my table image; if Claude (shorter stack) 3-bets big I’ll be prepared to fold K8s, but otherwise I’ll play aggressively post-flop for value and leverage position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this hand is well above the average opening range and certainly strong enough to continue against a button raise. Minni has been active from the button, and while I could 3-bet to put pressure on her and the Big Blind, a call allows me to see a flop and utilize the strength of my Ace-high while keeping the pot manageable. The pot odds (250 to win a 700 pot) make this a clear continuation.

Folding 5-4 offsuit from the big blind against two opponents. This is an unprofitable call - the hand has poor equity out of position against two opponents, and even pot odds of 3.5:1 don't justify playing such a weak hand. No need to gamble when better spots will arise.

I have flopped middle pair with an Ace kicker on a dry rainbow board. In this situation, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most standard and effective play. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet but not strong enough to lead out and build a massive pot. By checking, I can gauge Minni's strength; if she bets, I will likely call to see a turn card, as my hand has decent showdown value and some potential to improve.

I opened this button spot preflop and SB (Flash) simply called. Flop 6d Jc 2h is very dry and heavily favors an opener’s range — I hold Kc8c: a King-high overcard to the board plus a backdoor club possibility. Flash has been a fairly wide, often passive caller from SB, so a continuation-bet will fold many worse unpaired hands and small pairs while still giving me equity if called. I’ll make a standard ~50% pot c-bet to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable; if he folds I take it down, if he calls I still have outs (overcard + backdoor clubs) and can re-evaluate the turn, and if he raises I can let go without risking a large portion of my stack. Pot is 700 so I’ll bet 350.

On a dry flop of J-6-2 rainbow, I have middle pair with an Ace kicker. This is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' situation, but more likely I am ahead of Minni's wide button-opening range that missed this board. A bet of half the pot is standard for a continuation bet. I have sufficient equity and pot odds (3:1) to call this bet and see a turn. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Jacks or overpairs) and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in, so calling is the most profitable line to maintain pot control and realize my hand's showdown value.

I currently hold middle pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker. The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair and is a card that could easily have improved Minni's button-opening range. Acting first, the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see if Minni continues her aggression. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet, but for now, checking is the best way to realize my hand's showdown value without over-committing to a pot where I might be behind.

Turn Q♣ gives me K♣8♣: I still have no pair (K-high) but I now have a four-card club combo (J♣ and Q♣ on board + K♣8♣ in hand) — 9 outs (~19–20%) to make a flush on the river. Flash in the SB is a wide, often-passive caller; he called my flop c-bet, so his range contains many pairs (Jx, sets, 6s/2s), Qx, A/Kx, and some floats/draws. The queen on the turn improves his calling range a lot (makes Qx top-pair hands) and reduces my fold equity if I fire again. With deep stacks and a marginal hand, betting on the turn as a semi-bluff will often get called by better hands and commit chips unnecessarily. Checking is the prudent play: it controls the pot, allows me to realize my ~19% equity cheaply, keeps bluffs in his range, and preserves the ability to call reasonable turn bets or shove a river when I hit. Plan: check; if he leads small I’ll call to realize my draw, fold to a large polarized shove, and if he checks back I’ll evaluate the river (value bet a made club, consider a river bluff if giving good fold equity).

On this river, the board has paired Queens, meaning my pair of sixes now acts as the bottom of a two-pair (Queens and Sixes). While my Ace kicker is strong, I must consider that any Jack or any pocket pair higher than sixes (77-TT) currently beats me. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a Queen, but she could easily have a Jack or a medium pocket pair that she's happy to take to showdown. By checking, I avoid value-betting myself against a better hand while leaving the door open for Minni to bluff with air or missed draws. If she checks back, I have a decent chance of winning against her Ace-high or lower-pair holdings. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but checking is the most consistent play for pot control with a marginal showdown hand.

I check. I missed my club draw and have only K-high — no pair, no completed backdoor. Flash’s line (call preflop, call my 350 c-bet on the flop, then check-check on turn and river) looks like a passive, showdown-bound holding (Jx, small pocket pair, 6x/2x or even a queen). To make a profitable bluff I need a high fold frequency: a pot-sized bluff (~1400) needs ~50% folds, a half-pot bluff ~33% — with this opponent and line I don’t expect that. Betting here risks building the pot when I’m behind; checking preserves my stack and lets him show down marginal hands that will fold rarely to a river bluff. I’ll check back and take the free showdown rather than fire a low-expected-value river bluff.

I am on the button with K5 suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage over Claude and Minni. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to either win the blinds immediately or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

I'm holding 6c Js in the Small Blind, facing Flash's raise to 300 from the Button. This is a weak, unpaired offsuit hand with poor equity against Flash's raising range. Key considerations: (1) I'm out of position with a marginal hand; (2) The Big Blind (Minni) hasn't acted yet and could three-bet, putting me in a difficult spot; (3) Flash has been playing a relatively tight-aggressive style from the button throughout the tournament; (4) 6-Jack offsuit doesn't have sufficient equity to warrant calling a raise out of position. Even if Minni folds, I'd be playing a weak hand heads-up out of position. The fold threshold for this hand in this spot is clearly indicated - this is standard poker to fold marginal hands when out of position facing aggression.

I’m in the big blind with Q♣5♦ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (total commitment 300). Calling is the right play: the pot odds (~200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%) make defending profitable heads-up and Q5o has reasonable equity vs a button open range. Stacks are deep (15750) so I can navigate postflop and realize equity. 3‑betting to 500 with this hand is too thin out of position and will often be called; folding is too tight. I’ll call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — continue with top pair or strong draws, control the pot with weak or missed flops, and fold to large, disciplined pressure when appropriate.

I called preflop to defend with Q♣5♦; the flop K♥7♥2♠ misses me — I have only queen‑high with no heart backdoor and 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 fives): ~13% to pair on the turn and ~24% to pair by the river. Flash (BTN) checked to me, showing weakness or pot‑control. Out of position and without a pair or real fold equity, I want to keep the pot small and realize my equity rather than bluff into a wide calling range. Plan: check and see a turn. If BTN fires a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) I can call to chase my outs; if BTN bets pot‑sized or larger I will fold to avoid playing a big pot OOP with just queen‑high. Immediate action: check.

I have top pair with a King on a relatively dry board (K-7-2). Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. I should bet here to get value from weaker Kings, pocket pairs, or draws (like heart draws). A bet of around half the pot is standard to build the pot and protect my hand.

I defended preflop with Q♣5♦ and checked the flop to keep the pot small and realize equity OOP. Flash’s c‑bet of 325 into the pot (current pot 975, so the bet is a moderate mid‑size bet) is borderline but still cheap relative to the stacks. I have 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 fives) — roughly ~13% to hit the turn and ~24% to hit by the river. Pure pot‑odds on this bet are ~325/975 ≈ 33%, so my raw two‑card equity is a bit below breakeven versus a pure value range, but the BTN opened from the button and will have a fairly wide c‑betting range (misses and bluffs), and we have deep stacks/implied odds when we hit. Out of position and with no heart backdoor or strong fold equity, raising would be too thin and bluffing OOP is unattractive. Plan: call one street to realize equity, fold to significant turn aggression (pot‑sized raise or shove), call small turn continuation bets to see the river, and if I pair the turn evaluate for value or a pot‑control line. Action: call the 325 and see the turn.

Turn: board 2♠ 7♥ K♥ 2♦, I hold Q♣5♦ — queen-high with no heart backdoor. I only have 6 clean outs (three queens, three fives) ≈13% to hit the river. There is no bet to call on the turn and the correct, low-variance line is to check and realize equity rather than bluff-raise OOP. Raising or barreling here with Q-high is too thin (little fold equity OOP and deep stacks), while checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me fold to real aggression if I miss. Plan: check and see the river; if BTN fires a small bet on the river I can call with the right pot odds to chase a pair, fold to large pressure when un-improved, and if I hit a queen/five extract thin value on the river.

I have top pair with a King, but my kicker (5) is very weak, which makes this a marginal value hand. The turn card is a deuce, pairing the board. Since Minni checked for a second time, checking back here is the best play for pot control. It prevents me from getting check-raised off the best hand and allows me to see a river card for free. If Minni has a missed heart draw or a weaker pair, I can still potentially get value on the river or induce a bluff.

I check. I hold Q♣5♦ on 2♠ 7♥ K♥ 2♦ 4♣ and never improved — I have only queen‑high and no more cards to come. Flash opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and river; that line is consistent with medium strength (Kx/7x/2x) or with a missed hand checking down. Out of position with no showdown value, a river bluff is thin: I block only a small portion of his bluffs and many of his likely hands will call a bet, so betting is low EV and needlessly inflates a pot I’m almost certainly behind in. Checking preserves chips and lets me fold to real aggression; if Flash bets a very small amount (near the minimum) I’ll consider a call only if my read says he bluffs that sizing often, otherwise I’ll fold to any meaningful river bet.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (5). Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. While I likely have the best hand against missed draws or middle pairs (like a 7), betting for value is risky because I only get called by better Kings or perhaps a stubborn 7, and I risk being check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown without further risk.

Raising to 300 with AQ from the button. This is a standard 3x big blind raise from excellent position with a premium non-pair hand. AQ has strong equity preflop and postflop, and the button position gives me significant advantage for the remainder of the hand. The raise is sized to build the pot while keeping my risk manageable given my 7350 chip stack.

I’m on the small blind with 4♣8♦ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs 250 to call (would make the pot 700), so I need ~36% equity to break even. 4-8 offsuit has very poor equity vs a button opening range, is frequently dominated, and I’ll be out of position vs both opponents with the big blind still in the hand. Claude’s stack (7050) isn’t so deep that I can rely on huge implied odds, and there’s little fold equity or reason to 3-bet with junk. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25-to-1 to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack rather than defending with such a marginal range.

I have pocket fours on the button in a 3-handed spot — this is a strong hand to raise for initiative and to try to isolate the blinds. A raise to 300 (roughly 3x the big blind) is standard: big enough to get fold equity and deny correct pot odds to callers, but not so large that I’m committing myself unnecessarily. I’m in position, so if called I can pursue set-mining or c-bet dry boards; if I face a large 3-bet (likely from Claude, the shorter stack) I can re-evaluate based on size and opponent tendencies. Overall raising keeps pressure on the blinds, maximizes my expected value from worse hands, and preserves maneuverability postflop.

In a 3-handed game, Q7s is a reasonable hand to defend from the Small Blind against a button open, especially given Minni's high frequency of opening for 300. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the hand has suited connectivity and high-card value. I will call the additional 250 to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture and Minni's post-flop aggression.

Folding 5♥7♥ from the big blind. While receiving 3.5:1 pot odds on a 200 call, the hand quality is too weak to play out of position against two opponents. As the shortest stack (7400), I need to preserve chips for stronger holdings and more favorable situations. The marginal nature of this hand combined with positional disadvantage makes this a clear fold.

The flop is heavily coordinated with three spades and two high cards (Ace and King). My hand (Qc 7c) has missed the board entirely and I do not hold a spade to even have a flush draw. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and this board texture hits a button-opening range very well (containing many Aces, Kings, and spade combinations), I will check and plan to fold to any bet.

I flopped a set of 4s (I hold 4c4h with 4s on K♠-4♠-A♠). This is a very strong hand, but the board is dangerous — three spades — so there is a nontrivial chance Flash has two spades and already has a flush. Because I have position and Flash checked to me, I should not slowplay. I want to extract value from Ax/Kx and pocket pairs, and charge spade combinations and straight draws so they can’t see free cards. A bet around 70% pot (~500 into 700) achieves that: it’s large enough to deny equity to many draws and get value from worse made hands, but small enough to keep weaker hands and calls in to be exploited on later streets. If I get a large check-raise I’ll strongly consider they have a made flush/trips and re-evaluate by sizing; if they simply call, I can continue extracting with position.

The flop of Ks 4s As is extremely coordinated and favors the button's opening range significantly. I hold Qc 7c, which has completely missed this board; I have no pair, no straight draw, and, most importantly, no spades for a flush draw. Minni's bet of 500 into a 700 pot indicates strength or at least a continuation bet on a board she likely hit. There is no strategic value in calling or bluffing here given the board texture and my lack of equity.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit (Ah 5c), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My position is ideal for an open-raise to put pressure on the blinds. Standard sizing is 3x the big blind.

Folding 8-4 offsuit from the small blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal equity against Flash's raising range. The hand lacks both high card strength and playability (no straight or flush potential). Even though I'm getting reasonable odds on the call (250 to win ~450 pot), the fundamental issue is that 8-4o is dominated by most of Flash's range from the button, and I'll be out of position postflop. With 7350 chips, I should conserve them for premium hands where I have a significant edge. This is a standard fold.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button with A♠7♥ in the big blind. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 (300 total committed). With ~155BB effective this is a clear defend: A7o blocks a lot of opponent Ax combos and has decent equity versus a wide button opening range. Pot odds for the call are ~200 to win 650 (~30.8% break-even), which is favorable. 3-betting OOP with A7o is marginal (we remain out of position and risk getting 4-bet or put in uncomfortable spots), so I’ll call and see a flop. Postflop plan: proceed aggressively on an A or very favorable textures, float or fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and re-evaluate on the turn.

I check. Heads-up to the button on K♥-3♦-2♥ with A♠7♥: I only have Ace-high and a very weak backdoor heart possibility. The preflop raiser’s range contains a lot of Kx and other made hands that currently beat me, and with ~150bb effective it's unwise to build the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, allows me to call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity (call small-to-medium c-bets, fold to heavy pressure), and keeps options open to extract value or bluff on favorable turn cards (A/7 or a safe runout).

I am the button and was the pre-flop aggressor with Ah 5c. The flop of 3d 2h Kh is dry and favors my perceived range, as I can easily have strong Kings or other broadway cards. Minni checked, which is standard for the big blind. I will place a continuation bet of roughly half the pot (325) to fold out her non-connecting hands and capitalize on the board texture.

Call 325. Facing a ~1/3-pot c‑bet on K♥-3♦-2♥, folding is too weak given the pot odds and the hand. A♠7♥ blocks a lot of A‑x combos and has reasonable equity vs a wide button c‑bet range; calling 325 to win (~1,300) gives ~25% break‑even equity and my hand should have ~30–40% vs a wide continuation range. 3‑betting OOP with no pair is unattractive — little fold equity, and I’d be bloating a pot out of position with a marginal holding. Plan: call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I pair the A (or hit a very favorable runout) I can extract value; if the turn is a blank and Flash barrels big, I’ll fold to heavy pressure to control losses.

Check. The turn Qh doesn’t improve my hand — I still have only Ace-high — though it does give me a one-card heart draw (9 hearts remain, ~20% to hit on the river) plus a few pair-outs. Flash checked the turn, showing weakness or pot-control; betting out OOP with a marginal Ace and a single heart is unnecessary and can get me raised or committed by Kx/Qx/two-heart combos. I’ll keep the pot small, realize my equity to the river, and re-evaluate: call a small river bet with my draw/overcard equity, and fold to large aggression.

The Queen of hearts on the turn is a scare card that potentially improves Minni's range if she was floating the flop with hands like QJ, QT, or heart draws. Since I have the Ah blocker, I reduce the likelihood of her having the nut flush draw, but she could still have a King or a Queen. Betting again is unlikely to fold out a King or a Queen, and my Ace-high still has some showdown value. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity or potentially catch a bluff on the river.

I have only Ace-high (A♠7♥) on 3♦-2♥-K♥-Q♥-3♠. The board now contains a paired trey and three hearts. Flash opened and c-bet small on the flop then checked the turn — a pot-control line that often indicates Kx/Qx or missed/medium-strength hands. I have almost no showdown value and very little fold equity: a river bluff is likely to get called by K/Q pairs, any 2x, or a made flush/trips. With the pot at 1,300 and deep stacks, bluffing is low EV and risky; the correct play is to check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with no pair. If Flash bets, I’ll fold to sizable river aggression and only consider calling very small thin bets given his line.

On the river, the board has paired the trey (3s). My current hand is Ace-high with the Ah kicker. Minni has checked three times post-flop. While my Ace-high loses to any pair (including a 2, a Queen, or a King), I have some showdown value against missed draws like 45 or 56. Given that Minni called a flop bet and then checked the turn and river, she likely has a weak made hand or a missed draw. Betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a weak King or Queen) and checking back allows me to reach showdown for free in a pot where I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call.

Fold 3-9o on button. While positionally favorable, this hand lacks sufficient equity against two active deep-stacked opponents. Marginal call odds (3:1) don't justify committing chips to a weak hand where I'll struggle postflop. Preserving stack for premium opportunities is strategically sound.

Heads-up from the small blind with 6♣7♦ — a playable, connected hand that performs well in multi-street play. With the button folded and both stacks deep, I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure, and deny the big blind the correct pot odds to call with many marginal hands. A standard min-raise to 2x the BB (200 total) accomplishes that: it gives me fold equity, simplifies postflop decisions, and keeps my range wide. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I’ll play the flop aggressively or probe depending on texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 7s. Minni, in the Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. While 87o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a wide Small Blind opening range, especially given the excellent price. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I opened from the SB and have initiative; the flop K♠ J♥ 9♠ is fairly high-card and disconnected for my calling range but still folds a lot of the BB’s wide calling combos. My 6♣7♦ has no pair and no spade — just backdoor possibilities — so I should use my initiative to take the pot down now or keep the pot small if called. I’ll make a standard continuation-bet about half the pot (200) to apply pressure and fold out weaker holdings; if I face a sizable raise I’ll respect it and fold, and if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn unless I pick up real equity.

In this hand, I am in the Big Blind holding 8h 7s. Minni (SB) leads out on a flop of Jh Ks 9s with a 200 bet into a 400 pot. The board is highly coordinated with two spades and three Broadway/connected cards. My hand only has a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10 for a 7-8-9-10-J straight). Even if a 10 hits, the board will be J-K-9-10, meaning any player with a Queen would hold a higher straight (K-Q-J-10-9). Given the low probability of hitting the gutshot (~8% on the turn) and the fact that my potential 'out' might not even be the best hand on such a wet board, I do not have the necessary equity to call a half-pot bet. I will fold.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a wide range — K♦6♣ is marginal but fine to raise for position and fold equity. Calling 100 keeps us multiway and out of position postflop; raising isolates the blinds and lets us take the initiative. I'll open to a standard ~3x BB (300 total) which applies pressure to the small blind and the shorter-stacked big blind. Plan: if called, play aggressively with position (c-bet favorable, check/defend on very connected boards); fold to a large, strong 3-bet unless the opponent shoves a wide short-stack range.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 3h. Minni, who has been very active from the button, has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing a weak suited gapper out of position for the rest of the hand is generally a losing strategy, especially with the Big Blind still to act and potentially squeezing. Given my lead in chips, I will prioritize preserving my stack and wait for a better spot.

J8o is too weak to defend the big blind against a button raise. Despite reasonable pot odds (2.25:1), the positional disadvantage against Minni who will act last postflop makes this an unprofitable spot long-term. With 7,250 chips and 99 hands remaining, I can afford to wait for stronger hands in better positions. Fold.

I am on the button with 6d 3d. While I have been playing an aggressive opening strategy from the button and small blind throughout the tournament, 6d 3d is at the very bottom of the range and doesn't offer much playability if called or 3-bet. Minni (Big Blind) has been very active and defending her blinds frequently, and Claude (Small Blind) has a stack size that might tempt him to shove over a light open. I will fold this hand to maintain a more credible opening range and wait for a better spot.

J4o is a weak hand in the small blind. While I'm only facing a 50 chip call to win 150, being out of position postflop against the big blind with this marginal holding is not profitable long-term. Flash has folded, leaving only Minni in the big blind, but even so, J4o doesn't warrant a call from the small blind. I have a reasonable 7200 chip stack and should preserve it for better opportunities. Stack preservation and position are critical in the early-to-mid stages of a tournament, especially when facing well-stacked opponents.

4d6d is a weak unpaired hand with poor equity in a 3-way pot. While I have position on the button, this hand lacks the strength to justify a call even at favorable pot odds. Against Minni (SB) and Flash (BB), this is a marginal holding that's likely dominated. I should preserve my stack for premium hands and situations where position can generate more value. Folding weak hands out of position and marginal hands like this even in position is essential for long-term profitability in this tournament format (53/150 hands remaining).

I hold Qc5c in the small blind and the price to see a flop is tiny — 50 chips more into a 150-chip pot. Calling 50 more (100 total committed) gives me 25% pot odds to see a flop, and Q5s has enough playability to continue cheaply. Raising to 200 would buy initiative but puts me OOP postflop and will often be called or 3‑bet by Flash (he’s defended widely in prior hands), creating difficult spots with a marginal hand. With deep stacks and many hands left, I’ll preserve chips and see a cheap flop; if I face a substantial 3‑bet I’ll fold, and postflop will proceed only with a decent made hand or strong draw.

I have been dealt pocket Queens (Qs, Qd), a premium starting hand. Claude folded on the button, and Minni completed from the small blind. I should raise for value and to avoid giving Minni a free look at the flop with a potentially marginal hand. A standard raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is appropriate here to build the pot while I likely have the best hand.

Call 400. Q♣5♣ is a marginal hand but it’s suited and has enough playability to see a flop cheaply against one opponent with deep stacks. The raise to 400 leaves the pot ~500 and the extra to call is inexpensive relative to the pot (cheap enough to realize equity and chase flush/straight possibilities). Raising OOP with this hand is unnecessary and will create difficult spots; folding would be too tight given the price and implied odds. Plan postflop: proceed cautiously — continue with a made queen, two pair/set, or strong draws (flush/straight); check-fold most dry misses and fold to large, non-giving action. If Flash shows heavy strength (large 3‑bet preflop or big shove), relinquish the hand.

Check. I missed this flop with Q♣5♣ — no pair and only a small backdoor club draw. The board (J♥ T♥ 7♦) heavily connects with the raiser’s preflop range (Jx, Tx, broadways, some K/A combos), so leading out OOP would be a thin bluff that will be called too often. My preflop plan was cautious: continue only with a made queen or strong draw. Checking preserves chips and keeps options open — if Flash checks I get a free turn card; if he fires a small c-bet (~<1/3–1/2 pot) I can call to realize backdoor equity, but I will fold to large/forcing bets since Q-high is rarely best. If I pair my queen or pick up a real draw on the turn, I’ll become more aggressive.

I hold an overpair (QQ) on a coordinated flop of Jh-7d-Th. While this board is wet and hits many hands in Minni's defending range (like J-T, 8-9, or heart draws), I still have the best hand most of the time. I need to bet for value and to protect against various draws. A bet of 500 into the 800 pot (62.5% pot) is a standard size to charge draws and get value from hands like J-x or T-x.

I missed this flop with Q♣5♣ — no pair and only a backdoor club possibility. Flash’s 500 into the ~1300 pot is a small-to-medium c-bet (~38% pot). My preflop plan was to continue cheaply with marginal hands to realize equity and fold to heavy, non-giving action, and that still applies. Calling 500 keeps the pot under control, gives me a chance to hit a queen or pick up a real draw on the turn, and avoids unnecessary OOP aggression with a very marginal hand. I will fold to large turn pressure if I remain Q-high; if I pair my queen or pick up a strong draw I’ll become more aggressive. Therefore: call 500.

Board J♥ T♥ 7♦ 4♠, I hold Q♣5♣. I have missed completely: no pair, no straight draw, and a club flush is impossible with only one street to come (no clubs on board). My only realistic outs are pairing the Q or the 5 (6 outs, ~13%), so my showdown equity is very poor. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bettor on the flop, and his check to me on the turn looks like pot control or a strong hand being slow-played — either way I’m behind with Q-high. The pot is already 1,800 and betting OOP with a marginal hand is a thin bluff that will be called by most hands that beat me and will face pressure if raised. I’ll conserve chips and check to see a river. If I pair up or the river brings a very favorable card I can consider value or a small bluff; otherwise I’ll fold to sizeable river pressure.

I hold an overpair (QQ) on a board of Jh-7d-Th-4s. The 4s is a complete brick that doesn't change the texture of the flop significantly. Minni has checked to me again, which is consistent with a Jack, a Ten, or a draw. I must continue betting for value to get paid by her J-x and T-x hands, while also charging her draws (hearts, open-ended straights). A bet of 1100 into a 1800 pot is a strong sizing that maintains pressure and builds the pot for a potential showdown or river value bet.

I hold Q♣5♣ on J♥ T♥ 7♦ 4♠. I missed completely — no pair and no meaningful draw (there are only 3 queens + 3 fives = 6 outs, ≈13% to hit the river). Flash’s line (preflop raise, flop bet and now 1,100 into a ~2,900 pot) represents significant strength (pairs, two-pair, sets or strong BH draws). Calling 1,100 to win ~4,000 requires ≈27.5% equity, well above my ≈13%. My preflop/postflop plan was to conserve chips with this marginal hand and fold to large turn/river pressure; this is exactly that spot. Raising or shoving as a bluff OOP with essentially no equity is too risky and will be called by many better hands. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

4♠8♣ is a weak, unsuited hand, but I’m on the button in a 3-handed game so my positional advantage and fold equity are strong. The table’s standard open-sizing has been ~300 a lot, and a 2.5–3x open here will look routine and give me a good chance to pick up the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 to seize the initiative — if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold (hand has poor equity vs a strong 3-bet); if called I’ll proceed cautiously, c-betting only favorable flops and folding to heavy resistance.

I am in the Small Blind holding 6-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised from the button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, the hand has almost no post-flop playability and I would be out of position against a competent opponent. Given my strong stack lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q2o is a weak hand that doesn't justify a call here despite the 2.25:1 pot odds. While I technically have around 35-40% equity against Minni's wide button raise range, which meets the minimum threshold, this is a marginal situation at best.
