My stack of 7100 chips (71 big blinds) is reasonable but not deep, and I want to preserve chips with weak holdings. Even if I flop a queen, I'll often be in a difficult spot. Minni has demonstrated aggressive button play throughout the session, suggesting a wide range, but Q2o plays poorly postflop with limited ways to improve. This is a standard fold from the big blind. Better hands will come around shortly.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a significant chip lead over my opponents. I will make a standard 3x open raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand from a position of advantage.

8h5h is a weak holding from the small blind facing an aggressive raise from Flash. While this is a moderate raise size that could suggest Flash is raising with a wide range, my hand has limited showdown value with only weak flush draw potential and no straight possibilities. With a stack of 7050 chips, I should preserve chips for stronger spots. My pot odds are approximately 1.8:1, but 8h5h likely has only 30-35% equity against Flash's range. The risk/reward doesn't justify the call here, especially with Minni still in the big blind who could 3-bet. Folding allows me to play a stronger hand from a better position.

I’m on the big blind with Td4c facing a button open to 300. This is a weak, unconnected offsuit hand that will be out of position postflop and is heavily dominated by a typical BTN opening range. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~31% equity; Td4o’s equity vs a reasonable open is well below that, and a tiny 3-bet to 500 would give little fold equity and is a poor bluff candidate (no useful blockers). With ~151 BB effective I’ll preserve chips for better spots — fold here.

Folding Jack-Five offsuit on the button against two opponents with larger stacks. While 3-handed play is wider, J5o is too weak to justify the call, even with position and reasonable pot odds. Maintaining discipline early in the tournament by avoiding marginal spots is more profitable than marginal calls that have negative EV.

Heads-up in the small blind with K♦J♠ — a playable, pro-active hand. Calling 50 to see a flop out of position gives the BB good odds and initiative; I prefer to take the lead. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB) to isolate the big blind, gain fold equity, and make post‑flop play more controlled. If Flash calls I plan to c-bet favorable textures and play straightforward; if he 3-bets large I’ll generally fold KJ offsuit unless the sizing and pot odds justify a call. Overall raising maximizes EV here while keeping the pot manageable OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, a strong hand for a blind-versus-blind encounter. Minni has raised from the Small Blind, and since I will have positional advantage for the remainder of the hand, I will call to see the flop. This allows me to keep her wider range in the pot and utilize my position to make better-informed decisions on later streets without bloating the pot pre-flop with a marginal powerhouse.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and the flop (3♦ 3♠ Q♠) is relatively dry. I hold K♦J♠ — no pair and only a very weak runner-runner spade draw — so this is a pure continuation-bet spot to take advantage of the BB’s wide calling range (lots of overcards and small pairs that will fold). I’ll make a standard c-bet about half the pot (325) to buy the pot down and fold out equity from missed hands while keeping the size manageable if called. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll exercise pot control on the turn with no improvement; if he raises strongly I’ll fold KJ OOP since it has poor showdown value.

In this blind-versus-blind situation, I have Ace-high on a paired board [3d 3s Qs]. Minni's lead of 325 into a 600 pot (roughly half-pot) is a standard continuation bet. My Ace-Nine has significant showdown value against her range, which includes many unpaired high cards and potential bluffs. The paired board makes it less likely she connected strongly unless she has a Queen or a pocket pair. I will call to see how the turn develops, as I am in position and folding would be too exploitable in this matchup.

I c‑bet the flop and was called; on the turn (3♦ 3♠ Q♠ 7♠) I hold K♦ J♠ — I didn’t pair but picked up a four‑card spade draw. There are 9 spades left (~19.6% to hit on the river) but the board is paired, so Flash’s calling range includes many hands that currently dominate me (Qx, 3x, sets) as well as spade draws. With only a draw and poor showdown value I want pot control: check and see what he does. Plan if he bets: call smaller bets (roughly ≤400 chips) because pot odds justify chasing the flush; fold to large bets/raises since KJ OOP is not worth building a big pot against that range.

On the turn 7s, the board is now 3d 3s Qs 7s. Minni has checked after being the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. I hold As9c, giving me Ace-high with the nut flush draw (As). In this position, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit the nut flush on the river without risking a check-raise that would force me to fold my draw or over-commit. Furthermore, Ace-high still has some showdown value against Minni's potential bluffs or missed draws.

I hold K♦J♠ on 3♦ 3♠ Q♠ 7♠ 7♦ — I missed the river and have no pair (only king-high). I was the preflop and flop aggressor, got called on the flop, then checked the turn to control the pot when I only had a backdoor spade draw. The river pairs the board, creating obvious full-house/trips combinations for any opponent holding a 3 or 7 and leaving Qx and many calling hands that beat me. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) looks like a medium/strong or showdown‑willing range, so bluffing here has very low fold equity and is thin EV. With a healthy stack and only 1250 in the pot, the prudent play is to check and take the showdown (or fold to a river shove) rather than invest chips in a likely losing bluff.

The board has double-paired (Sevens and Threes) with a Queen. My hand is Ace-high, which on this specific board plays as Two Pair (Sevens and Threes) with an Ace kicker (7-7-3-3-A). Minni has checked the turn and now the river after being the aggressor on the flop. While my Ace kicker makes this the best possible version of the board's two-pair (beating any other non-pair/non-Queen hand), betting is unlikely to get called by anything I beat. If she has a Queen or a pocket pair higher than Threes (like 44-66 or 88+), she has a better two-pair and will call or raise. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and win against her missed draws or lower Ace-high hands without risking a check-raise.

I have pocket Aces on the button with only the blinds to act (Flash SB 18,375; Claude BB 6,950). This is the single best hand preflop — raise for value and to thin the field/isolate. I want a size big enough to charge callers and discourage multiway action, but not so large that I overcommit if I face resistance. I raise to 900 total: large enough to extract value from weaker pairs/draws and to pressure the blinds (Claude’s shorter stack makes him think twice), while leaving room to maneuver post‑flop or respond to a 3‑bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 7s. Minni opened for a significantly larger-than-normal raise of 900 (9x the big blind). Usually, Minni opens for 300. This increased sizing suggests a very strong range or a polarized attempt to take the blinds. 9-7 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against such a large open. I will fold to preserve my stack, as I currently hold a significant chip lead and do not need to defend with marginal hands.

I'm in the big blind with Qh 3s - a weak hand with very limited potential. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (1050:800), Q3o is among the weakest starting hands and has roughly 25-35% equity against Minni's typical button raising range, well below the 43% needed to justify a call. Being out of position post-flop against an aggressive opponent like Minni compounds this weakness. With a healthy stack of 6,950 chips, I'm not in survival mode and should be selective about marginal situations. This is a clear fold - I should preserve my chips for premium hands where I have better equity and position.

I am on the button with JTo in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. My stack is healthy, and while Claude is relatively short-stacked, JTo plays well enough to raise. I will open for 300, which has been the consistent opening size at this table.

Jack-Three offsuit is a weak, marginal hand. While I have reasonable pot odds to call (450:250), several factors strongly favor folding: (1) I'm out of position in the small blind facing an aggressive button raiser; (2) My hand has poor equity against Flash's likely raising range and virtually no value if the big blind re-raises; (3) With a 6,900 stack in a 150-hand tournament, I need to preserve chips for premium situations; (4) I'm vulnerable to being squeezed by the big blind. This is a straightforward fold to wait for better opportunities. Jack-Three doesn't justify commitment of chips from out of position.

Call 200 more (300 total). A♣4♣ is a hand I want to defend heads-up — suited Ace with a club backdoor and a useful Ace-blocker against the button’s opening range. Flash’s 3x open from the button is likely fairly wide, so calling keeps weaker hands in and lets me realize equity with deep stacks (I still have ~145 BB). I don’t want to thin 3‑bet a marginal Ace out of position; calling keeps the pot small and gives me postflop options (value-bet A-high flops, pursue club draws, and fold or pot-control on adverse boards).

Check. I'm out of position with A♣4♣ on 3h-6s-6c — a dry, paired flop that generally favors the preflop raiser. I have only A-high and a backdoor (running) club draw, plus an Ace blocker, so I don't want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets the button c-bet (so I can call small bets and fold to large ones), and preserves my deep stack for better spots. If Flash checks back I get a free turn; if he bets I will evaluate sizing and continue only with reasonable odds or strong improvement.

I opened JTo from the button and Minni called from the big blind. The flop comes 3h-6s-6c, which is quite dry. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a good board for a continuation bet (c-bet). Minni checked to me. While I haven't connected with this board, I have two overcards and the betting initiative. A small c-bet around half the pot should be enough to take it down or define where I stand against her range, which consists mostly of mid-to-low pairs and high cards that missed. If she calls, I can evaluate the turn.

Flash's bet is small (~1/3 pot) on a dry, paired flop (3♥-6♠-6♣). I have A♣4♣: an Ace-blocker, A-high, and a backdoor (runner-runner) club possibility. Against a wide button c-bet this sizing is likely to include many hands I currently beat or have to fold later. Calling 325 gives me correct pot odds (I need ~20% equity; I have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river ≈24% plus fold equity vs some bluffs) and keeps the pot under control out of position. Raising OOP with a marginal Ace is unnecessary and invites bigger commitments; folding is too nitty given the price and my blockers. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy aggression, and if I improve (pair an Ace or pick up clear equity) I will act for value.

Check. On the turn I have A♣4♣ on 3♥-6♠-6♣-5♠ — no pair and my backdoor club is now dead. Flash c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which is consistent with medium strength (pocket pair, Kx/Qx) or missed bluffs; I have very little value to extract and little fold equity OOP with just Ace‑high. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position and expose me to raises I should fold, so I control the pot and realize equity by checking. Plan: fold to sizable turn/river aggression, but if checked to on the river re‑evaluate — consider a delayed bluff only on a very favorable runout or value‑bet only if I pair the Ace.

The turn 5s does not significantly change the board texture. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she could have a small pocket pair, a piece of the board like a 3, or she is floating with overcards. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from her air or weak overcards, Minni has shown some stickiness in previous hands. By checking back here, I control the pot size and take a free card to try and hit my overcards (Jack or Ten) which are likely to be the best hand if they land. It also avoids the risk of being check-raised off my equity.

Flash bet small on the flop then checked both turn and river — a line consistent with a wide, medium-strength or missed-c-bet range rather than strong made hands. I hold A♣4♣: only Ace‑high with my backdoor club now dead and very little showdown value. There is essentially no value to extract on the river (I only beat pure air), and a thin bluff here is unattractive because he can comfortably call a small bet with many hands that still beat me and I must fold to a raise. The pot is tiny relative to our deep stacks, so there’s no reason to bloat it or risk a larger fight in a marginal spot. I’ll check back and take the showdown rather than bluff or bluff‑catch with no real equity.

On the river, the board is 3h-6s-6c-5s-2h. I have Jack-high (Js-Th), which has almost no showdown value. However, the action has been passive since the flop. Minni check-called my continuation bet on the flop but checked both the turn and the river. While my J-high is unlikely to win at showdown against her range (which likely includes Ace-high, small pairs, or even King-high), Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky and check-down marginal hands in previous pots (like Hands #45 and #49). Given that I have a healthy lead in chips and the pot is relatively small (1300), I will check back. Bluffing into a player who doesn't fold Ace-high or small pairs easily is a low-frequency play here. I'll take my chances at showdown or simply concede the small pot.

I'm on the button with As4d, facing 100 to call with 6900 chips. While As4d is a marginal hand, my button position gives me a significant advantage. The pot is 150 with 100 to call, meaning I'm getting decent odds (2:1), but more importantly, I can leverage my position.

Key considerations:
1. **Position advantage**: As the button, I act last postflop, which is crucial with a weak hand
2. **Stack depth**: At ~69BB, I want to be aggressive when I can win chips without excessive risk
3. **Hand strength**: While As4d is weak in isolation, the ace has value and can flop top pair
4. **Initiative**: Raising here allows me to take control and potentially win the pot preflop or play in position postflop

A raise to 300 (3x BB) is standard and aggressive without committing too much with a marginal holding. This accomplishes multiple goals: it shows strength, steals blinds some percentage, and if called, I maintain positional advantage with better pot odds if I hit my ace.

Against the two larger stacks, raising for value is better than calling and playing out of position postflop.

I have Q♠ J♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 in a 3-handed pot. Against a button's fairly wide opening range this is a strong hand to 3-bet for value and fold equity rather than passively calling out of position. I want a size that isolates Claude and gets some respect but doesn't overcommit the stack if a big 4-bet arrives. I will 3-bet to 1000 total (about 3.3x the open). Plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet appropriate flops and pot-control on unfavorable boards; if I face a large 4-bet (near-pot or shove) I will fold most of the time since that line polarizes to premiums from Claude’s 66BB stack. Calling 250 is too passive and folding is too weak here, so I raise to take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 7c. Claude raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind. Holding a weak suited connector and facing significant action from two opponents (including a 3-bet), my hand is not strong enough to call or 4-bet. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Folding As4d to Minni's 3-bet. While my initial raise from the button was appropriate positional aggression with a marginal hand, I cannot continue when facing a 3-bet that shows genuine strength. As4d is too weak to call and play postflop out of position against a 3-bet range. The hand doesn't have the equity needed to justify the call despite the 3:1 pot odds, and preserving my 6600-chip stack for better opportunities is the correct strategy.

4♠7♣ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability. Although I'm on the button, both the small blind (Flash) and big blind (Claude) will act after me; raising to try to ‘steal’ risks a 3‑bet from Claude (who has shown willingness to reraise) or getting called and playing a difficult multiway pot with almost no equity. Calling 100 to see a flop is also unappealing — my equity multiway is very low and I’d be investing with a marginal holding. With a healthy stack (15750) I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with K6s. Minni, on the button, has folded. It's just me and Claude (Big Blind). K6s is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, especially with a stack advantage. I will raise to 300 total (a standard 3x open) to put pressure on Claude's blind and take control of the pot early.

A2o is the worst starting hand in poker and cannot be profitably called even with 2:1 pot odds. While Flash has been aggressive, I don't have sufficient evidence he's raising so wide that A2o becomes +EV. With a moderate stack, I should preserve chips for premium hands and avoid marginal situations. Folding big blind with A2o against a standard small blind raise is correct fundamental poker.

I am on the button with 6d 5h, which is a marginal hand. The pot is currently 150, and I need to put in 50 more to complete the blind (since I am in the dealer position and usually post a small blind equivalent if it were heads-up, but here the standard 50/100 blinds are in play). However, in this 3-handed structure, I am the first to act pre-flop. Raising with 65o from the button is a standard wide-opening range play to steal the blinds, but given the stack sizes and the fact that Minni has been quite active and aggressive, a fold is also acceptable. Looking at my recent history, I've been opening frequently. I will fold this hand to maintain a tighter image and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if called.

I'm holding 9-3 offsuit, one of the weakest hands in poker. While I'm in the small blind and only need to call 50 more chips to win a 150-chip pot (3:1 odds), the fundamental issue is that 9-3o has extremely poor equity against any reasonable opponent hand. With a medium stack of 6,450 chips and good position and hand selection so far in the tournament, I should not bleed chips away on marginally unprofitable spots. Folding weak hands from the blinds is a key element of solid poker strategy. This allows me to preserve chips for higher-expected-value spots where I have better cards or position advantages.

Raising with Q9o from the button in 3-handed play is standard strategy. The position advantage combined with decent hand strength justifies a raise to 250 chips. This applies pressure to the blinds, potentially wins the pot preflop, and maintains reasonable stack efficiency if called. With my shorter stack, I need to be selective about building chips in favorable spots, and this is one of them.

3♦7♠ is a garbage hand with almost no playability out of position in a three-handed pot. Claude raised to 250 (I must call 200 more) — calling here puts me OOP vs the raiser and the big blind into a multiway pot for only 33% immediate pot odds, and my hand has very poor equity and no useful blockers for a credible 3-bet bluff. The only other realistic option would be a large 3-bet/all-in to try to take it down preflop, but with Claude fairly deep (~62 BB) he can call or 4-bet, and 3♣7♠ is a bad hand to commit a large portion of my stack with. Preserve the stack for better situations and fold.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Seven of clubs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Claude, the short stack on the button, has raised to 250. This is a standard button open. With 17,650 chips, I am the chip leader. I will 3-bet to put pressure on Claude's stack and potentially take the pot down immediately. A raise to 750 (three times the initial raise) is appropriate.

Flash's 3-bet to 750 from the big blind against my button raise signals a strong hand or aggressive play. While Q9o was a reasonable button raise for positional advantage, facing this re-raise puts me in a difficult spot. With my short stack (6200 chips, ~62BB), I need to be selective about getting involved in marginal spots. Q9o is vulnerable out of position, and if I call and miss (which is likely), I'll have limited fold equity and chip efficiency. Folding preserves my chips for premium hands or better stack-to-pot situations. Against a much larger stack (Flash has 17000), I should avoid marginal hands in disadvantageous positions.

Hand is 9-2 offsuit — one of the worst starting hands. I’m on the button facing the small blind and a shorter big blind (Claude) who can easily defend or shove; raising to try a steal risks being called by better hands or getting squeezed. This hand has almost no playability postflop and is frequently dominated by callers. With stacks as they are and only a small pot, the +EV play is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2c, which is a very weak hand. The action folded to me, and the cost to complete is only 50 chips. However, playing 92o out of position against Claude (the Big Blind) is generally a losing play, even with a large stack. Claude has a relatively short stack compared to me and Minni, making him more likely to defend or jam. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table, and both opponents are significantly shorter, especially Claude. Raising here puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the pot early. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to build the pot and define the ranges.

Folding Ac6h from the small blind. This is a weak hand with poor playability out of position against a button raise with the big blind still to act. The risk of facing a squeeze from Minni is high, and I have no reason to force action with this particular hand. I'm the shortest stack but have sufficient chips and plenty of hands remaining to find better spots. Standard fold.

I call to 300 (put in 200 more). A♠9♣ is a hand I want to defend the big blind with vs a wide button open to 300 — it has good Ace-blocking value and reasonable equity. The pot is 450 and calling 200 gives ~30.8% immediate equity threshold, and with deep stacks I can realize implied odds postflop. I avoid a marginal 3-bet out of position; calling keeps my range wide and lets me play postflop (probe for value on A-high boards, pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on dry/face-up pressure, and value-bet/raise when I hit top pair or better).

Check. I have A♠9♣ — a single pair (nines) with a very useful Ace blocker but I’m out of position against the button’s opening range. The flop Q♠ 9♦ 5♠ favors the raiser (many Qx and broadways) and there’s only a backdoor spade possibility (need both turn+river), so leading out risks getting raised or priced out by top pair/sets. With deep stacks I want pot control and to let the opener c-bet bluffs and worse Ax/Kx hands. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity; fold or seriously re-evaluate to large sizing or check-raises that represent Qx/sets. If checked to me on later streets I’ll continue to control the pot and re-evaluate on any Ace/9/spade turn.

I have flopped top pair with a strong King kicker (KQ on Q-9-5). This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni checked to me after calling from the big blind pre-flop. I should bet here to extract value from weaker queens (like QJ or QT), middle pairs (9x), and various draws (spade flush draws, straight draws like JT). A bet of roughly half the pot is appropriate to build the pot while making it expensive for drawing hands to continue.

Call 325. I have second pair (9♣) with the A♠ blocker — a hand that fares well vs a wide button c-bet range. The pot is 975 and calling 325 requires ~25% equity, which my hand comfortably has against Flash’s range of Qx, many A‑highs, spade draws and bluffs. Ace of spades cuts down nut-flush combos, and my kicker is strong vs worse nines and many bluffs. I’m out of position, so a raise would be too polarizing and risky; folding is too weak given the pot odds and hand strength. Plan: call to keep bluffs/worse hands in and control the pot, then re-evaluate on the turn (fold to very large turn aggression, continue vs small bets, and extract value if I improve).

I have A♠9♣ — second pair with the best possible kicker vs worse nines and a reasonable showdown hand out of position. The turn (Tc) removes the backdoor spade possibility (no flush now), so this is a pure one-pair hand. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop; him checking the turn (action is checked to me) looks like pot-control/weakness or an attempt to induce a bluff. Betting OOP here would inflate the pot with a marginal hand and opens me up to check-raises from Qx/sets; a small probe bet would fold out some worse hands but also risks getting priced into bigger spots. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot under control and realize equity rather than build it OOP. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call reasonable bets from bluffs/worse pairs, and fold to large, polarized aggression representing two pair/set.

I have top pair with a King kicker. However, the Ten of clubs on the turn is a very coordinated card that connects well with the range of a player calling from the big blind. It completes possible straights like King-Jack or Jack-Eight and improves hands like Queen-Ten or Jack-Ten to two pair. By checking back, I control the pot size and avoid being check-raised off my hand, while still keeping the pot manageable to call most river bets or value bet if the river is safe.

Check. I have A♠9♣ — second pair (9s) with the ace blocker on Q♠ 9♦ 5♠ T♣ T♥. Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which looks like a lot of Qx, A‑highs and missed draws in his range; some tens/trip/full‑house combos are possible but not dominant. Betting now is thin value: it folds out lots of air I already beat and gets called by Qx and any ten/trips that crush me, and it also opens me up to a raise OOP. Checking controls the pot and realizes equity. Plan: check to showdown; if Flash bets a small/medium amount I’ll call for value (I beat most missed draws/Ax), and fold to large, polarized river raises that represent trips/full houses.

On the river, the board has paired Tens, which changes the dynamic slightly. I have two pair, Queens and Tens, with a King kicker. While this is a strong hand, the board is quite coordinated with potential straights (KJ, J8) and any player holding a Ten now has trips. Minni's check on the river following a check on the turn suggests she is either in showdown mode with a marginal hand like a Queen or a Nine, or she's missed a draw. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and avoid the risk of being check-raised by a hand that has me beat (like a Ten or a straight). I have plenty of showdown value and the pot is already a decent size.

I'm holding A9 suited (hearts), a solid hand from the button. With a short stack of 6200 chips, I need to be strategic about building my stack. This is a prime position to raise and potentially take down the blinds or play in position. A raise to 300 (3x BB) is standard and gives me multiple ways to win: either taking it down preflop or continuing with a strong hand if called. My opponents have much larger stacks (15k+ each), so demonstrating aggression from this strong position is crucial for my tournament life. A9 suited has good equity against most hands and plays well post-flop.

I’m in the small blind with Q♣5♥ — a weak, offsuit hand that’s frequently dominated by the button’s opening range. Claude opened to 300 from the button and the big blind (a very deep stack) still has to act, so calling 250 more to play out of position risks facing a call or 3-bet and being stuck playing a marginal hand OOP in a multiway pot. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive here either — I should reserve aggression for stronger holdings. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to pick from, folding and preserving chips for better spots is the highest EV line.

In a 3-handed game, QTs is a strong starting hand. Claude's raise from the button can represent a wide range of hands. With the pot odds being 2.25:1 (200 to call into a 450 pot), this is a standard defend from the Big Blind to see a flop and realize the hand's potential.

On a flop of 3c-Jh-4h, I have two overcards (Q and T) and a backdoor heart flush draw with the Qh. Standard play from the Big Blind after calling a pre-flop raise is to check to the aggressor. I will check here to see how Claude reacts and decide whether to continue based on the sizing of any potential continuation bet.

I'm holding A9 of hearts on a flop of 3c-Jh-4h with a pot of 650. Flash checked from the big blind, indicating weakness or a drawing hand. 

My hand has strong equity here:
- Flush draw (4 hearts to complete)
- Ace high
- Decent kicker with the nine

As the preflop aggressor, I have the initiative and need to continue my aggression. Flash's check is a clear sign of weakness, making this an excellent spot to bet and either take down the pot immediately or build value.

Given my short stack situation (5900 chips), I need to build my stack while I have good hands and position. A continuation bet of around 400 is appropriate - roughly 60% of the pot - which maintains aggression while preserving my stack for future streets.

This bet accomplishes multiple objectives:
1. Takes down the pot now if Flash folds
2. Gets value from worse hands (ace-king, ace-queen, pocket pairs below jack, etc.)
3. Applies pressure given my preflop raise
4. Allows me to continue my tournament strategy of being aggressive from strong positions

The flush draw also gives me additional equity if called, making this a profitable spot regardless of outcome.

On a flop of 3c-Jh-4h, I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) and a backdoor heart flush draw with the Queen of Hearts. Claude's continuation bet of 400 into a 650 pot is a standard play from the pre-flop aggressor. With pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, I have enough potential equity and implied odds to see a turn card. Holding the Queen of Hearts is valuable as a blocker and for flush potential. I will call to see if the turn improves my hand or if Claude slows down on a non-heart card.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a significant card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. My hand, Queen-Ten, has not improved and holds no real showdown value or significant draws. Since I am out of position and Claude has shown aggression on both the pre-flop and the flop, the best course of action is to check and see how he responds to the Ace. If he bets, I will likely have to fold as I lack the equity to continue.

With A9 of hearts on a 3c-Jh-4h-Ac board, I've just improved to top pair (pair of aces). Flash has shown weakness with two consecutive checks. My short stack demands that I extract value from good hands like this. A bet of 700 achieves multiple objectives: it takes down the pot immediately if Flash folds, gets value from draws and weaker aces, and maintains my aggressive tournament strategy. This bet size is proportional to the pot and stack situation, leaving room for river play while building my chip stack in a critical tournament moment.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a major scare card and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I currently hold only Queen-high with no significant draws or showdown value. Claude has shown consistent aggression by betting both the flop and the turn. Given that I have no pair and very limited outs to improve, calling a second barrel on this board texture is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Button vs both blinds in a 3-handed game — Td7o is marginal but I should be opening a wide range to use my positional advantage. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call; I’ll raise to seize initiative, apply fold equity and avoid playing a bloated multiway pot with an unsuited, gap hand. 300 is the standard open size seen at this table (3x BB), big enough to pressure the blinds but small enough to control the pot if I face resistance. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small defenses and play post‑flop in position, c-betting favorable flops and exercising pot control on dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 7d. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. This is a speculative hand that is out of position against a player who has been quite active. Calling 250 more into a pot of 450 with Claude still to act in the Big Blind is generally not profitable with a weak, unsuited hand like 9-7 offsuit. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Q8o is a weak hand with poor equity against Minni's raising range from the button. My stack is relatively short at 6850 chips in a 50/100 game, which means I need to be selective and preserve chips for premium spots. The risk/reward on calling 200 more into a 450 pot with this marginal hand isn't favorable. With only Flash folding and Minni's positional raise, I should fold and wait for a better opportunity to put my chips at risk. This is a standard fold from the big blind with a weak non-paired hand.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand in most contexts. Claude (Small Blind) and Minni (Big Blind) have been active, and given my hand strength, it is not worth entering the pot, even for a standard raise or calling the blind. I will fold to preserve my stack.

QsJs is a premium hand - broadway cards, both spades, plays exceptionally well in position. From the small blind with only 50 chips to call into a 150-chip pot, I have excellent implied odds. However, I should be aggressive here rather than passive.

Raising to 250 total accomplishes several goals:
1. Defines my hand strength to avoid playing out of position postflop
2. Puts pressure on both opponents to fold and win the pot immediately
3. Maintains healthy chip position while playing premium holdings
4. Allows me to play in position if called

QsJs has good equity against a wide range - I'm happy to play this hand aggressively. Flash (17,925) and Minni (15,125) both have deep stacks, so they may have fold equity respect. The raise is measured but assertive.

Claude opened to 250 from the SB. I’m in the BB with 9♦7♦ (offsuit connectors), pot is 350 and it costs me 150 to call (total commit to match the raise = 250). With deep stacks and heads-up against a likely wide opening range, 9-7o has reasonable equity and playability to see a flop. A 3‑bet here would be overaggressive with a marginal offsuit connector and puts me out of position postflop, so I’ll defend by calling and see a flop. Plan: proceed cautiously OOP — look to realize equity with pairs/straight draws, and fold or pot-control against heavy aggression on unfavorable boards.

With QJ high on a 2-6-7 flop, I'm likely ahead but vulnerable against Minni's call range. Checking is prudent here because: (1) my hand is marginal without additional equity, (2) I need to control pot size as a medium-stack player, (3) checking allows me to gauge Minni's hand strength and make better decisions on future streets. If she bets, I can evaluate; if she checks back, I maintain flexibility on the turn. This is more profitable than betting and potentially getting raised or facing significant uncertainty.

Claude opened wide preflop and checked the flop — likely a mix of floats, weak overcards and some pocket pairs. I hold middle pair (7s) with a 9 kicker and I’m out of position. This is a marginal made hand: ahead of many missed hands but behind pairs/sets and vulnerable to aggression. Betting here would bloat the pot OOP and create difficult turn decisions against better hands from Claude’s raising range. With deep stacks and a short-ish opponent, I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the turn — call small/medium bets to keep showdown range, fold or proceed cautiously against large, polarizing bets.

Semi-bluff with strong drawing hand (flush draw + open-ended straight draw). Minni's flop check suggests weakness, and I have significant equity even if called. A turn bet of 350 applies pressure, maintains fold equity against weaker holdings, and leverages my premium draws effectively. This bet represents strength as the pre-flop aggressor while having strong outs to improve on the river.

I’m OOP with middle pair (7s) and a 9 kicker on 2s-6h-7s-Td. Claude was the preflop opener and checked the flop, then fired a substantial but not crippling bet (350 into ~500). Calling 350 into a 850 pot gives ~2.4:1 pot odds (need ~29% equity). My 7s beats a lot of missed overcards and bluffs in a wide SB raising range, and is reasonable to continue with at this price. Raising OOP with a marginal pair would bloat the pot, lose fold equity to hands that already beat me (T, straights like 9-8) and create tough decisions vs a shorter-stacked opponent who can shove. I’ll control the pot and realize equity — call 350 and re-evaluate on the river (fold to large river overbets, extract small value if checked to).

On the river with Queen-high (QsJs) on a final board of 2s-6h-7s-Td-9d, I'm holding a marginal hand. The 9d on the river didn't complete either my flush draw (I still need one more spade) or any straight draw. My semi-bluff on the turn was well-constructed with strong equity (flush draw + draws), but those outs didn't materialize. Now facing showdown with just Queen-high, the prudent play is to check. This line accomplishes: (1) avoids further value loss on a marginal hand where Minni could easily have a pair or better given her call on the turn, (2) allows the hand to reach showdown economically, (3) respects Minni's big blind position and her call pattern, which suggests she likely has a made hand or draw. Checking behind also controls pot size given my moderate 6250-chip stack. If Minni checks back, my Queen-high may still win; if she bets, I can make a pot-odds based decision on what is essentially a showdown value scenario.

I’ve just rivered two pair (9s and 7s) on 2s-6h-7s-Td-9d. Claude was the preflop opener, checked the flop and fired a modest turn bet (350) that I called — that line is consistent with a wide SB raising range containing single-pair hands (Tx, small pairs), missed draws (8x) and occasional strong hands. When he checks the river he’s showing either weakness or a desire to get to showdown cheaply; there are still hands that beat me (any 8 for the 6-7-8-9-10 straight, trips/sets or T9/T7), but many of his turn-calling combos are single pair hands that will pay a value bet. I want to extract from those worse one-pair hands while keeping the bet small enough to be called — a half-pot value bet (~600 into 1200) accomplishes that. If Claude check-raises big I’ll assume a strong made hand (straight/trips) and fold to large aggression; if he calls I’ll go to showdown and expect to be ahead of much of his range.

Calling with Queen-high on the river. The pot odds are 4:1 (need to win ~20% of the time), and Minni's line (flop check → turn call → river bet) doesn't scream premium strength. She may be bluffing with a weak pair or drawing hand, or checking flop with a marginal holding that improved on turn/river. With my chip stack at 6250, the 600 call is reasonable variance, and Queen-high has sufficient showdown value at these odds. Folding would be too tight given the favorable pot ratio.

Playing hand #68 with 8-9 offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. My stack is 5650 (56.5 big blinds), which is manageable but not comfortable. In 3-handed poker, position is paramount and button ranges are significantly wider than full-ring. 8-9o is a marginal but playable hand from the button. The pot is already 150 and I'm only facing a 100 call. Both blinds likely have weak holdings given the antes and tight spots. Folding every marginal hand while short-stacked will cause slow attrition. I'll call to see the flop with position, and can leverage my button advantage post-flop. The flop will give me crucial information about hand strength - if I whiff completely, I can fold to aggression; if I catch anything meaningful or both opponents check, I can take the pot down.

K3o is a marginal hand, especially out of position in a limp pot with two opponents. Folding wastes only my posted 50, but calling 50 more to complete to 100 gives very good pot odds to see a flop and keeps the pot small — I avoid bloating the pot or isolating myself out of position by raising. Plan: see the flop cheaply, proceed only with clear improvements (top pair with good kicker, two pair+, strong draws) and fold to significant aggression if I miss. Therefore I will complete the call.

I am in the big blind with QJ offsuit. Both the button (Claude) and the small blind (Minni) have limped into the pot. While QJo is a strong hand compared to a typical limping range, I am out of position against both players. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small, which is a low-variance play given my healthy stack. I can navigate the post-flop play effectively based on the board texture without overcommitting pre-flop against potential traps from a short-stacked Claude or a wide range from Minni.

I limped preflop to see a cheap flop; the A♦ 8♣ 7♥ flop completely misses K♠3♥. This is a three-way pot and I have no pair, no meaningful draws (no backdoor flush/straight), and an Ace on board likely hits opponents' calling ranges. Multiway continuation-betting is thin and usually gets called or raised by better hands, so I should control the pot and preserve chips. I’ll check and give up to significant aggression — only consider calling very small bets that offer correct pot odds, otherwise fold and wait for a better spot or a King on later streets.

On this Ace-high board (8c-7h-Ad) in a limped multi-way pot, I have Queen-high with no direct draw or pair. Minni has already checked. Since I am out of position against Claude (the button) and have no real equity in the hand currently, checking is the most standard and low-risk play. I want to see if I can improve on the turn for free or simply fold if someone else shows strength.

Betting pair of eights on a relatively dry flop where both opponents checked. With a short stack (55.5 BB), I need to build chips by betting when ahead. The ace is a concern but both players' checks suggest they don't have premium holdings. A 200-chip bet (2/3 pot) applies reasonable pressure and gets value from weaker hands or takes down the pot immediately. This is a +EV spot given my position and hand strength relative to opponent weakness indicators.

I limped preflop to see a cheap flop and intentionally checked — K♠3♥ missed A♦ 8♣ 7♥ entirely. Claude’s small stab of 200 into a 500 pot (~40% pot) is not something I want to chase. Calling 200 would require ~28.6% equity (200/(500+200)) to break even. I have no pair, only three kings as outs (≈12.5% to hit a king by the river), and even pairing a king often won’t be good vs hands containing an ace, pair, or better kicker. The big blind still acts behind me, so this is likely to become a multiway decision or a raise — neither favorable for K3o out of position. Raising as a bluff here is too risky multiway. Decision: fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

On this Ace-high board (8c-7h-Ad) in a limped pot, I have Queen-high with no draws and no pair. Claude's bet of 200 into 300 after everyone checked is a standard positional stab, but without any equity or a backdoor draw, there is no reason to continue. I have a very healthy stack and do not need to defend a small pot with nothing. Folding is the most prudent and low-risk decision here.

I’m on the button with A♣7♥ — a playable ace in position. With only the SB (deep) and a relatively short BB (Claude, ~57BB) to act, the +EV play is to open-raise and take the initiative: it pressures the blinds, isolates the short-stacked BB, and gives me positional advantage postflop. The table’s standard sizing has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 total (a ~3x BB open). This size keeps fold equity vs blind defenses but doesn’t overcommit me vs a 3-bet. Plan: if both fold I pick up the blinds; if SB calls and BB folds I play aggressively in position; if BB shoves as a short-stack I will call (his shove range is wide), but I’ll re-evaluate/fold to a large 3-bet from the deep SB.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 5d, a speculative suited one-gapper. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.8:1 to call, I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against an aggressive opponent (Minni) and still have Claude in the Big Blind to act behind me. Claude's stack is relatively short (57.5 BB), making him more likely to shove or squeeze. 35s is near the bottom of my range and generally not strong enough to defend out of position in this 3-handed dynamic. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm facing a button raise to 300 from Minni with K2o (Deuce-King offsuit) from the Big Blind. This is a marginal hand at best - while the King has some value, the Deuce is one of the worst kickers possible. Looking at the tournament context: (1) I have only 5750 chips (short stack relative to opponents), (2) Minni has been raising frequently throughout the tournament showing a loose style, (3) K2o has poor equity even against a wide range, (4) Post-flop play will be difficult with this weak hand, (5) I need to be selective with limited chips and should prioritize stronger hands. The pot odds (450 to call 200) aren't compelling enough for a weak kicker problem like K2. With a short stack, I should fold weak hands and wait for stronger spots.

I am on the button with pocket fours, which is a solid opening hand in a three-handed game. I want to raise to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with the initiative. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

JT offsuit is a playable hand from the small blind with reasonable equity against Flash's button raise. Current pot odds are 450:250 (1.8:1), requiring approximately 36% equity to call profitably. JT offsuit has roughly 40% equity against a typical button raising range, making this a mathematically sound call. With a 5700 chip stack (57 big blinds), I have sufficient depth to see a flop and play post-flop poker. Minni is still to act in the big blind, so the pot may grow, improving my odds further. This is a better spot than folding marginal hands and waiting for premium holdings given my current chip position. The hand has good playability in position and can win large pots when it connects.

A♣J♥ in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 and the small blind calling. Pot is 700 and I need to put in 200 more. AJo is strong enough to defend, but with two opponents the value of a light 3-bet is reduced and a small squeeze to 500 won’t reliably isolate—it just bloats the pot and can get me in tough spots. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth, and lets me see a flop where my Ace-high and backdoor possibilities have good postflop equity. I’ll call and play the flop based on texture and opponents’ actions.

Checking with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) in an out-of-position, three-way pot. J-T has good equity but is primarily a drawing hand on this board. By checking, I gather information about opponent strengths while maintaining flexibility with my medium-sized stack. If checked back, I'll see a free turn card; if bet, I can evaluate the amount and make appropriate decisions. This is superior to betting from an out-of-position, drawish holding against two aggressive opponents with larger stacks.

Three-way flop with pot 900 and I’m on the button’s blind. I hold A♣J♥ on K♥ 9♦ 6h — I have Ace‑high, one backdoor heart (runner‑runner), and only one real overcard (the A). Versus the button’s opening range and the small blind’s calling range there are a lot of Kx and pocket pairs that already beat me, and with two opponents my fold equity from a continuation bet is low. Claude (SB) checked, which looks like weakness or pot‑control, but Flash (BTN) will act after me and is capable of C‑betting. With marginal showdown equity and a relatively deep effective stack (but a shortish Claude at 5,450), I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Plan: check and control the pot. If faced with a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot ≈ 300) I’ll call to see a turn (decent equity vs a wide range); I’ll be prepared to fold to large overbets or a check‑raise. If both opponents check through I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and only invest further if I improve (pair an A/J) or get a very favorable card to bluff. Action: check to keep pots manageable and realize equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button and the board has come out K-9-6. Both opponents in the blinds have checked to me. While my pocket fours are vulnerable and unlikely to be the best hand if called, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. The King heavily favors my perceived range as the raiser, and a bet of half the pot puts pressure on my opponents to have a piece of the board to continue. If I am called, I can re-evaluate on the turn, but I expect to take the pot down here often against two checks.

Calling 450 chips with J♠T♣ on a 6♥9♦K♥ flop. I have 8 outs to an open-ended straight draw plus 6 additional outs to a pair, providing approximately 35-40% equity. The pot odds of 4:1 require only 25% equity, making this a profitable call. Flash's bet could be a continuation bet (Minni's check suggests weakness), and hitting my straight would likely result in a significant payoff from both opponents given the three-way pot composition. My 5450 chip stack provides sufficient depth to continue post-flop play.

I checked the flop earlier to keep the pot manageable. Flash c-bet 450 (~1/2 pot) and Claude called, making the pot 1,800; calling 450 requires roughly 20% equity (450 into a 2,250 pot after my call). With A♣J♥ I have decent showdown value vs a wide c-bet+call range and I block some A-high combinations, so a flat call realizes equity without bloating the pot. Raising into both the big-stacked aggressor (Flash) and a calling SB (Claude, short stacked) is thin and can get me in awkward spots or face a shove. Plan: call 450, see a turn, and only invest further if I pair/improve or have a clear fold/vs-bluff read — fold to large turn aggression if I don’t improve.

On the turn with J♠T♣ on a 6♥9♦K♥8♠ board, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to any Q or 7 completing a straight), which provides approximately 32% equity. The turn action requires careful consideration given my stack depth (5000 chips) and out-of-position status. Checking is the optimal play because: (1) It preserves my stack for potential river decisions; (2) An OESD is a hand that benefits from seeing free cards or gathering information about opponent strength; (3) If Flash checks back, I get a free river with significant equity; (4) If Flash bets, I can evaluate the bet size relative to my stack and make a disciplined decision based on pot odds. With only 5000 chips remaining, I need to play cautiously in a three-way pot where I'm out of position. This is a natural progression from my flop check - consistent with a drawing hand that isn't strong enough to bet from poor position but has sufficient equity to continue. I'll reassess after seeing Flash's action on the turn.

Board is 6h 9d Kh 8s, I hold A♣J♥. My runner–runner heart draw is dead (turn is a spade), so I only have Ace‑high with two overcards to most of Flash’s and Claude’s calling ranges. This is a multiway pot against an aggressive button and a caller; betting here has very limited fold equity and just bloats the pot when I have marginal showdown value. Claude checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control or weakness, and Flash still acts after me — raising or barreling into two players is thin. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity and keep options open. Plan: call a small stab but fold to large aggression; if checked through I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only invest further with a pair (A or J) or a very favorable board/runout.

On the turn, the 8s completes some potential straight draws (though unlikely for my opponents' calling ranges) and generally makes the board more connected. After my flop continuation bet was called by both Claude and Minni in a three-way pot, it is highly probable that at least one of them has a piece of the board, such as a King, a Nine, or a Six, or a flush draw. Pocket fours have very little showdown value here and almost no equity against their calling ranges. Since both opponents have checked again, I will check back to see a free river and control the size of the pot, as a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a better hand given that they both stayed in on the flop.

River value bet with the nuts (King-high straight, 9-10-J-Q-K). This is a strong made hand that beats all pairs and draws. Betting 1,200 (approximately half the 2,250 pot) is a standard value bet sizing that should induce calls from weaker holdings like pairs or second-best straights while appearing natural given my checking pattern on the turn. My 5,000 chip stack can absorb this bet comfortably.

I hold A♣J♥ and never paired on the river (board: 6h 9d Kh 8s Qh). Claude (short, 3.8k) check‑called the flop, checked the turn and now leads for 1,200 into a 2,250 pot — pot is shown as 3,450 including his bet. Calling 1,200 to win ~4,650 requires ~26% equity. His line polarizes to thin value (Qx/Kx/9x/pairs) or rare bluffs; against that range my Ace‑high has well under break‑even equity. Raising as a bluff is thin (I block few bluffs and would likely face a shove from the short or a call from the big stack). Earlier plan was to only invest on the river if I improved to a pair or had a very favorable bluff card — I did not. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

On the river, the board has become quite dangerous with the Queen of Hearts completing a potential flush and several straight possibilities. My pocket fours have almost no showdown value in this three-way pot, especially after both opponents called my flop continuation bet. Claude leading out for 1200 into 2250 after checking twice suggests he either improved on the river (hitting a flush or a pair of Queens) or was slow-playing a hand like a King. Given the strength shown by the flop calls and the significant river lead, my hand is almost certainly beaten, and there is no profitable reason to call or bluff raise.

J3s is too weak to play from the button with a short 7,250 chip stack. While position is valuable, this hand lacks sufficient strength (no pair, weak draws, low kicker). With two opponents having significantly larger stacks, I need to preserve chips for premium opportunities. Folding the small blind posting preserves my tournament life for better spots.

I have T9o (Ten♦ Nine♥) on the button (small blind) heads-up. This is a playable, connected hand with good implied odds and I'll have positional advantage postflop. With both stacks deep (~156bb) I want to take the initiative, deny the big blind free equity, and isolate—so I’ll open to a standard heads-up size of 3xBB. If Flash 3-bets heavily I’ll fold to big aggression; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet/disciplined barrels on favorable textures, pot-control or check when the board is dangerous).

I am in the big blind with 4d 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni raised from the small blind to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the strength of my hand relative to any reasonable opening range, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

I’m on the button with Kc7c — a suited King in a three-handed pot with deep stacks and position. This is a good spot to open and apply pressure: I want to steal the blinds and gain initiative while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to balance fold equity and avoid inviting too many callers. If I get one caller I’ll use position to play postflop (c-bet when I hit or when boards favor a blocker/steal line); if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll usually fold K7s since it’s often dominated and not worth a large confrontation.

I am in the Small Blind with Q-J offsuit, a strong broadway hand. Minni has raised to 3x from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, calling is the most standard play to see a flop. While raising is an option, calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to utilize my post-flop skill against a player who has been active.

Calling 200 more chips with 6d-7h from the big blind. Strong pot odds of 3.5:1 justify the call. The hand has straight-making potential, and I can evaluate post-flop. Already invested 100 chips as BB, so incremental cost is low for seeing a flop in a multi-way pot.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker on a board that is quite wet, featuring both flush and straight draws. As the first to act on the flop in a three-way pot, leading out for value is a strong play. A bet of half the pot (450) aims to extract value from draws (like clubs or 9-10) and middle pairs, while also protecting my hand. Checking risks giving a free card to hands that could easily overtake me on the turn.

I have a pair of sixes with strong secondary equity. The board is 6c-8c-Jh, and my 7h gives me an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or 5 makes a straight). I have approximately 11+ outs: three remaining sixes for a set, plus eight cards for the straight (4 nines and 4 fives). Flash's raise from the small blind on this board could be a continuation bet, flush draw, or a legitimate strong hand—difficult to narrow down exactly. Pot odds justify the call: I'm getting 4:1 odds (450 to call into a 1800 pot) while holding a hand with roughly 45-50% equity against most of Flash's range. I have sufficient stack (6950) to make this call and still have flexibility post-turn. The multi-way nature of the pot and my hand's drawing potential make this a clear call.

I have Kc7c on a 6c-8c-Jh flop — a very strong drawing hand: a nut‑flush draw (9 clubs left) plus extra pair/overcard outs (three kings, three sevens). The pot is 1800 and it costs me 450 to continue, so I’m getting about 20% pot odds (450/(1800+450) ≈ 20%). My raw equity to make the flush by the river is ~35%, and including pair outs my total realistic equity vs a wide calling/c-betting range is well above the break‑even threshold. Flash’s small bet (450) looks like a standard small c-bet into a multiway pot and Claude’s call narrows his range to at least decent hands or draws. Raising to 900 as a semi‑bluff is an option, but multi‑way and with a caller behind it’s likely to get called and needlessly inflate the pot or commit me to uncomfortable spots vs strong made hands or a shove. In position I can realize my equity, keep pot control, and extract value on favorable turns (bet big if I complete the flush or pair the K; if I miss a blank I’ll evaluate a delayed c‑bet or a pot control check depending on action). Call 450 to see the turn and play the hand in position.

The King of Spades on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Jacks. Both Claude and Minni called my lead on the flop, suggesting they have pieces of the board or draws. The King is a card that hits a lot of a button opening range (Minni) and big blind defending range (Claude). By checking, I am controlling the size of the pot and moving into a mode where I can evaluate my opponents' actions. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a very difficult spot, and if I bet and get called, I'm likely behind a King or a better Jack. Checking allows me to see how the rest of the street plays out.

Checking is optimal here. I have a vulnerable pair of sixes with limited outs (only the three remaining sixes provide meaningful equity improvement). Both opponents have checked, indicating they're not confident in their holdings, making a bet risky and potentially wasteful. A free river card maximizes my chances of improving while minimizing my risk on a weak holding. With ~6.5% equity to hit a set, I need to see the river as cheaply as possible rather than committing additional chips to a bluff that could be called or raised.

I have Kc7c on 6c-8c-Jh-Ks: top pair (Kings) with a K kicker plus a 4‑card club flush draw. Both opponents checked the turn, showing weakness or pot control — this is a spot to both get value from worse one‑pair hands (Jx, weaker Kx) and charge/price out pure club draws. The pot is 2250; I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1500). That size builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, charges draws (9 club outs to make a flush on the river and another ~5 outs to make two‑pair/trips — ~14/46 ≈ 30% to improve), and keeps enough fold equity vs a single caller without needlessly committing my whole stack multi‑way. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (bet for value if I improve, pot‑control or check back a blank); if met with a large check‑raise/shove I’ll fold to extreme aggression unless the read suggests a lighter range.

On the turn, the King of Spades is a very significant overcard that likely connects with Minni's button-opening range and her decision to call my flop lead. While I flopped top pair with an above-average kicker, the Jack is now second pair. Minni's bet of 1,500 into two players after I checked is a show of strength, often representing a King, a better Jack like Ace-Jack, or a high-equity draw. Furthermore, Claude is still to act behind me, and since his stack is relatively short (6,500), calling risks facing a squeeze or being committed to a large pot out of position with a marginalized hand. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, the most disciplined play is to fold and avoid a potentially costly second-best scenario.

Folding on the turn with a vulnerable pair of sixes. I have only ~15% equity with 7 outs (3 sixes + 4 fives), but I'm facing 1500 to call into a 3750 pot (2.5:1 odds requiring 30% equity to break even). Minni's turn raise after both Flash and I checked strongly suggests a strong made hand, especially after the K came. The math doesn't support calling, and my stack (6500) is too valuable to commit on an underdog situation. Folding is the correct decision.

In a 3-handed game, K8o on the button is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Standard tournament strategy for the button is to open roughly 40-50% of hands, and K8o sits well within that range. Opening for 3x (300) has been my consistent sizing and allows me to take control of the pot with position on both the small and big blinds. Claude has a shorter stack of approximately 64 BBs, while Minni is the chip leader, so I need to be prepared for resistance, but K8o is a solid starting hand to lead with.

Q7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position in the small blind. While the pot odds are decent at 1.8:1, the marginal equity (~35-40%) doesn't justify the call with a short stack. I'm folding to preserve chips for premium hands in better situations, especially with Minni still to act and potential for a re-raise.

I have JJ in the big blind facing the button open to 300 (pot 450, to call 200). With ~172 BB effective this is a textbook 3-bet spot: JJ is a strong value hand that wants to isolate the opener, gain fold equity against wide button opens, and take the initiative postflop. I’ll 3-bet to ~900 total (~3x the open). That sizing extracts value from worse broadways and medium pairs, reduces the frequency of multiway or cheap flops, and keeps 4‑bet decisions comfortable given the deep stacks. If Flash 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (possibly fold to extreme polarized 4-bets, call or shove depending on sizing); if called I’ll play straightforward postflop aggression on favorable textures.

K8o is a standard opening hand from the Button, but facing a 3-bet from an active and aggressive chip leader in Minni, it becomes much less attractive. While I have position, K8o is easily dominated by Minni's 3-betting range (which likely includes better Kings, Aces, and pocket pairs). I've already invested 300, but calling another 600 to play a bloated pot with a mediocre hand is not ideal for long-term stack preservation in this tournament setting. I will fold and look for a better spot.

4-7 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker with essentially zero value. Despite being on the button with excellent position, this hand lacks any meaningful potential - no flush draw possibility, disconnected cards, and only creates a weak straight. With my stack being the shortest at the table (6450), I should preserve chips and wait for premium hands. Position value cannot overcome this hand's fundamental weakness, especially when facing two active opponents with deep stacks who can exploit post-flop. Folding here is the mathematically and strategically correct decision.

I’m in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind with J♣5♣. This is a marginal but suited hand with decent equity versus a wide blind-defending range. Calling the extra 50 (100 total committed) is cheap and preserves stack size while allowing me to see a flop and realize my equity (flush/backdoor/paired J). Raising to 200 would bloat the pot out of position and open me to 3-bets or difficult postflop spots with a marginal holding; folding is too tight given the price. Plan postflop: play cautiously out of position — check-call small C-bets, look to bluff only when I have blockers or fold to heavy aggression unless I improve.

I have Ad7d in the big blind. Minni has limped from the small blind, which is a common play in three-handed poker but often represents a weaker or polarized range. Ace-high suited is a very strong hand in this spot. I want to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or define the hand further. Raising to 3.5x-4x the big blind is standard to punish a limp.

I’m in the small blind with J♣5♣. I already have 100 committed and Flash (BB) has raised to 400. Folding is too tight in heads-up — J5s has flush/backdoor and showdown equity and I should defend a wide range from the BB. 3-betting OOP with this hand is poor strategy (bloating the pot out of position and running into stronger hands); min-raising to 700 is unnecessary. Calling 300 more (total 400 committed) gives good pot odds: I’m calling 300 to win a ~800 pot (≈37.5% break-even equity) and it’s a tiny piece of my 17,575 stack (~1.7%). Plan postflop: play cautiously out of position — check to control the pot, check-call small continuation bets with draws or one-pair hands, fold to heavy aggression when missed, and only lead or bluff when I have good blockers or fold equity. Proceed to see a flop.

I have J♣5♣ on K♦-5♥-8♠ — middle pair (fives) with a reasonable J-kicker and only backdoor club potential. I’m out of position in a big pot versus a wide preflop raiser, so I want to control the size and realize equity rather than bloat the pot. Checking keeps worse overcards and bluffs in the hand so I can extract value on a call, lets the opponent fire their c-bets so I can react, and avoids committing too many chips with a marginal hand. Plan: check-call small-to-medium c-bets for value, fold to large overbets that strongly represent Kx/sets, and re-evaluate on the turn if I improve or get clear info.

I hold Ad7d and was the pre-flop aggressor after Minni limped from the small blind. The flop comes Kd-5h-8s, which is a fairly dry board. Although I missed the board, I have the best possible Ace-high and a backdoor nut flush draw. Since I represent a strong range as the pre-flop raiser, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's limp-calling range, which likely consists of many hands that missed this board. A small sizing around 40% of the pot should be effective in either winning the pot immediately or narrowing her range.

I have J♣5♣ on K♦-5♥-8♠ — middle pair (fives) with a weak kicker and only backdoor club potential. Flash is the preflop raiser and fired a relatively small bet (325 into a 1,125 pot), which gives me ~22–23% break-even equity to call. Raising OOP with this marginal one-pair hand would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots against stronger Kx/overpairs; folding is too tight given the price and my showdown value. I’ll call the 325 to control the pot and realize equity, then re-evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy continued aggression unless I improve or get a clear read). Total committed after the call will be 725.

Check. I have only middle pair (5s) with a weak J‑kicker on K♦‑5♥‑8♠‑Q♠ and I’m out of position vs the preflop aggressor. Betting OOP with this marginal one‑pair hand would bloat the pot and will get called by most Kx/Qx and draws; a small bet is unlikely to fold enough better hands. The turn also brings a second spade (more draw combinations), so I prefer to control the pot and realize my showdown equity by checking and reevaluating on the river. Plan: if Flash leads a sizable river bet I’ll fold unless I improve or get a clear read; if the river bricks I’ll check‑showdown and extract value only if I improve to two‑pair or better.

I hold Ad7d on a board of Kd-5h-8s-Qs. I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, which Minni called. The turn Qs is an overcard that doesn't particularly help my hand, but it also doesn't necessarily hit Minni's limp-calling range unless she has Qx hands. However, since she called the flop, she likely has a piece of the board (like a King or an 8) or a draw. I have some showdown value with Ace-high against her missed draws, and I have a chance to improve to the best hand if an Ace falls on the river. Checking back here allows me to see a free river card, control the size of the pot, and potentially realize my equity without being forced to fold to a check-raise.

I check. With J♣5♣ on K♦-5♥-8♠-Q♠-4♣ I only have one pair (fives) with a weak kicker and I'm out of position. Flash was the preflop aggressor, fired a small flop c-bet (325) and then checked the turn — a line consistent with a lot of Kx/8x value hands and some missed draws. A river bet here would be thin: it will fold the pure air sometimes but will get called by most hands that actually beat me (K/Q/8 and many medium pairs), so betting is likely negative EV. Pot control and showdown realization is the better line — I preserve my stack and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. If Flash now bets, I can re-evaluate (call small, fold to large raise), but for now I check to go to showdown.

I hold Ace-high (Ad7d) on a board of Kd-5h-8s-Qs-4c. After Minni limp-called pre-flop and check-called the flop, her range is likely capped at marginal hands like a pair of 8s, 5s, or possibly a weak King or Queen. By checking the turn, I have indicated I might be giving up or have a marginal showdown hand. On the river, Minni has checked again. While Ace-high wins against some of her missed draws (like JT or 97), it loses to any pair. However, betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a weak 8 or 5) given the pot size and the way the hand has played out, and I risk being check-raised. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free with my marginal equity.

Open-raise to seize the initiative from the button in a 3-handed game. T5o is marginal, but vs two blinds and with deep stacks it’s profitable to open a wide range and use position. A standard 3x (300) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable; if either opponent 3-bets large I’ll fold this holding, if called I’ll c-bet on dry flops and otherwise control the pot. Raise to 300 to pressure the blinds and play the postflop hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play against a button open. Minni has been quite active and opening a wide range of hands. While calling is an option, raising (3-betting) allows me to take initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or isolate Minni while putting the short-stacked Claude in a difficult spot in the Big Blind. A raise to 1000 is a standard size that forces the button to define their hand.

With 9d4d in the big blind facing a 3-bet from Flash after Minni's raise, this is a straightforward fold. 94o is a weak hand with limited value, especially out of position. Flash's aggressive 3-bet from the small blind typically represents a strong range (pairs, AK, AQ combos), and even if I connect with a 9, I have a poor kicker. My stack of 6,350 is also relatively short, making it inefficient to build a big pot with marginal holdings. Better spots will present themselves where I can deploy chips more profitably. Early in the tournament with 75 hands remaining, I should preserve chips and only engage with stronger hands.

I opened to seize the button, but Flash’s reraise to 1,000 is a sizable 3‑bet after my 300. I hold T♠5♦ — a weak, easily‑dominated hand vs a typical SB 3‑bet range (A‑x, broadways, medium/strong pairs). Calling 700 into a 1,400 pot requires ~33% equity; T5o doesn’t have that against this range and has poor post‑flop playability even in position. 4‑betting or min‑raising as a bluff is too risky with this holding. Preserve the healthy 18k stack and fold here.

I am on the button with K5o. This is a marginal hand, but standard strategy on the button is to raise to put pressure on the blinds, especially in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack of 15550. A standard raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to steal the blinds or take control of the hand post-flop.

2-Jack offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. From the small blind facing a button raise, this is a clear fold despite reasonable pot odds. The hand lacks both high-card strength and playability - even if I hit the Jack, it's often dominated by better hands, and the deuce is essentially dead weight. With a short stack of 6300 chips in a 150-hand tournament, I need to play tighter hands in marginal positions. Folding preserves chips for premium situations where I have a real edge. This is standard strategy against button raises with weak holdings.

I’m in the big blind with K♦9♦ facing a button open to 300 (to-call 200). Against a wide button opening range and with deep stacks (~180bb) K9s is worth defending — good suit, reasonable kicker and playable postflop. Calling 200 into a 450 pot gives good pot odds (~2.25:1) and keeps the pot controllable while preserving my range. A tiny 3-bet to 500 is too weak and a larger 3-bet would commit OOP without strong justification. Plan: call to see the flop and play based on texture — continue aggressively on K‑high or diamond flops and exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on dry A/broadway boards.

Flop is A♣ 8♣ 5♠ and I hold K♦9♦ — no pair, no club/spade flush draw, just K‑high with two diamonds (no immediate backdoor). I defended the 3‑bet preflop to keep a wide range, but this texture is very bad for my hand: it hits the raiser’s range hard (many A‑x, broadways) and I have little equity. With deep stacks and out of position, checking for pot control and to see what the opener does is the right play. A c‑bet from the opener will usually represent an Ace or a strong continuation range; I’ll fold to a pot‑sized or sizable c‑bet and only consider calling a very small probe if I think the raiser bluffs extremely frequently. No reason to bluff‑cbet OOP with such a weak hand. Action: check now and reevaluate if Flash bets.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in position on an Ace-high board. I have bottom pair (5h), which gives me some showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards and draws. Minni has checked to me. A continuation bet of around half the pot is standard here to represent the Ace and put pressure on her range, while also protecting my hand against potential draws or overcards like 9s or Jacks. If she calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, potentially checking back to get to a cheap showdown.

I’m on the BB with K♦9♦ facing a button c-bet 325 into a 650 pre‑flop pot (pot now 975). I have no pair and no club backdoor; this A♣8♣5♠ flop hits the raiser’s opening range very hard (lots of A‑x and broadways). To call 325 into the 975 pot I need ≈25% equity (325/(975+325)=25%); my chance to make a pair by river is ~24% and actual equity is likely lower versus his c‑betting range. Out of position and with deep stacks I don’t want to chase marginal equity or get into multi‑street pots dominated by A‑x, and raising here would be irresponsible with this hand. Decision: fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

8-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have positional advantage on the button, the hand quality is too poor to justify putting chips at risk. Even though I could potentially steal blinds with a raise, I'd only win 150 chips for risking 200+ against opponents with significantly deeper stacks (Minni: 17,750, Flash: 15,800 vs my 6,300). With 63 big blinds, I should be selective and preserve chips for premium hands in better spots. The risk/reward here is unfavorable - if called, I'm out of position postflop with a weak hand that rarely improves. Folding is the standard play with such a weak holding.

Heads-up to the big blind (Claude folded). In small blind I should open-raise a wide range to take the initiative rather than limp-call. Q♦5♥ is marginal but acceptable in heads-up; raising gives fold equity and isolates Flash. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets strongly I’ll tighten up (likely fold); if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable runouts and play postflop with position disadvantage in mind.

I hold A5s in the Big Blind. Minni (Small Blind) raised to 300, which is a standard open from that position. A5s is a very strong hand for defending the Big Blind, offering both nut-flush potential and straight potential (the wheel). Given the stack depths and the fact that we are three-handed, I can either call or 3-bet. I have been playing somewhat aggressively, but a call here allows me to keep the pot manageable and play a strong hand in position relative to the aggressor post-flop. I will call the additional 200.

I have Qd5h on Qh-4s-Ks — top pair but a weak kicker and I’m out of position vs Flash who called preflop. As the preflop aggressor this is a good continuation-bet spot: I’m ahead of a large portion of his defending range (weaker Qx, jacks, A highs, small pairs) while behind Kx and sets. Betting charges worse queens and mid pairs, denies equity to any backdoor draws, and extracts value rather than letting him realize equity for free.
