I’ll size ~2/3 pot (400) — big enough to get value and fold equity but small enough to fold to a large check-raise. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises small I’ll consider calling; if he check-raises large/aggressively I’ll strongly consider folding given my poor kicker.

On this King-high flop with a Queen, my A5 of spades has a backdoor nut flush draw and an overcard with the Ace. Minni has led out for 400 into a 600 pot (a 66% pot bet). While I have missed the flop, I have some equity with my backdoor draws and the possibility that an Ace could be good if Minni is betting with a wide small blind range. However, calling 400 to win 1000 is a bit steep given the board texture heavily favors a raising range. Since I have position and some potential to improve, but not enough to justify a raise or a loose call against a 2/3 pot bet on a board that likely hit her range, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with 8♥6♦: marginal but valuable because of position and fold equity. I don’t want to limp; standard button open to seize the blinds and play postflop in position. A 3x open (to 300) is the table norm and gives good fold equity against both blinds. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; versus a single call I’ll plan to continue with position and look for straights/paired boards and pot-control.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven suited (Ks7s). Minni, on the button, has raised to 3x the big blind. In a three-handed game, K7s is a strong hand that performs well post-flop. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, calling allows me to play a pot with a very playable hand while keeping the pot controlled against a deep-stacked opponent. Claude in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked, so I need to be aware of a potential squeeze, but calling the 250 is the most standard and efficient play here.

2-8 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker with virtually no playability. Facing a raise from Minni (Button) and a call from Flash (Small Blind) in a 3-way pot, I'm in a terrible situation. Even with favorable pot odds (700:200), I need approximately 22% equity to break even, but 2d-8c has minimal equity and will be played out of position on all streets. With a moderate stack (6200 chips) and no reason to gamble with trash, this is a clear fold. Preserving chips for premium hands is the correct strategy in a tournament setting.

On this flop of 7c-9h-6h, I have middle pair (sevens) and a backdoor spade draw. The board is extremely coordinated with many straight and flush possibilities. Since I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard and most prudent play. It allows me to see how she reacts to this wet board and gives me the opportunity to potentially check-call a reasonably sized bet, or fold if the action becomes too heavy, preserving my stack while I have some showdown value.

Flop is 7c 9h 6h and I hold 8h6d: middle pair (sixes) with an 8 kicker, plus strong turn/river equity (outs to straights with 5/T, extra outs to two pair/trips and backdoor hearts). I was the preflop aggressor and Flash checked, showing weakness or a wide calling range. In position I want to extract value from worse pairs/draws and deny free cards to many turn outs while keeping the pot controlled. A ~1/2 pot c-bet applies pressure, gains fold equity versus air, and is small enough to continue on many turn cards without overcommitting. Plan: bet 350 into 700; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet good cards: 5/T/8/6, check back blanks); if he raises big I’ll fold, since my hand is vulnerable.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a very coordinated and wet board. Minni's 350 bet into a 700 pot is a half-pot continuation bet, which she could be making with a wide range of hands including top pair, draws, or even complete air. Folding here would be too weak given my pair and the pot odds. Raising is unnecessarily risky on such a connected board where I'm likely only called by better hands or big draws. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, evaluate the turn, and see if I can get to showdown cheaply or improve my hand.

The 2c on the turn is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold middle pair (sevens) with a strong King kicker. Since I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will continue with the plan of checking to her. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how she proceeds. My hand has decent showdown value, but it is not strong enough to lead out into a board that contains many straights and potential flushes. I will check and evaluate based on her action.

Turn 2♣ is a blank — my hand is second pair (6s) with an 8 kicker after I c-bet the flop and was called. Flash’s check on the turn shows weakness or a float; the turn also kills the backdoor heart-runout. I have ~10 river outs (two sixes + fours fives/tens) — roughly a 20–22% single-card improvement — but right now this is a marginal hand that’s vulnerable to any 7x/9x or better. Pot control is the priority: avoid bloating the pot out of position with a hand that has limited showdown value and can be exploited by a check-raise or large bet. I’ll check back the turn to realize my equity and keep decision flexibility on the river (call small bets, fold to big pressure).

On the river, I have improved to two pair (Kings and Sevens) with the King of Spades falling on the river. The board texture (7c 9h 6h 2c Kc) is somewhat dangerous as a straight (58 or 8T) is possible, but given the action, my hand is very strong. Minni checked the turn after I check-called her flop bet, which often suggests she has a marginal made hand like a Nine, a Seven, or a middle pocket pair, or a draw that she's looking to realize for free. By betting now, I can extract value from those weaker made hands (like a Nine or a Jack-Ten/Queen-Ten type hand) that she would likely check back if I checked to her. A bet of approximately 60% of the pot (850) is a standard value sizing that is small enough to be called by second-best hands but large enough to build the pot.

Flop/turn recap: I opened and c‑bet the flop; Flash called and then both of us checked the turn. On the river K♣ he makes a small lead 850 into a 2,250 pot (~38% pot). I have second pair (6s) with an 8 kicker. Pot odds: calling 850 into a 2,250 pot (3100 final) requires ~27.4% equity to break even. Range/read: Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is consistent with a mix of thin value hands (Kx, 9x, 7x, some pocket pairs) and missed draws/blocker bluffs (missed hearts, overcards, straight draws). I beat most pure bluffs and some weak pairs/overcards, and likely have ~30–40% showdown equity vs this river betting range. Raising would over‑represent and commit too many chips with a marginal hand; folding here gives up too much +EV given the pot odds. Plan: call the small river bet to realize equity and fold only to significant post‑call action (if it occurs).

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo), which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I have 17,200 chips, putting me in a strong position relative to Claude (6,150) and Minni (16,500). Standard strategy from the button is to raise to around 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Raising also helps define the strength of my hand and potentially win the blinds and antes immediately.

QK is a premium hand in the top 10% of starting hands and I need to play it decisively given my short stack position (62 BB). Flash's button raise from early position could be a wide range, and I hold a strong hand that plays well both in a showdown and multiway pots.

With only 6150 chips, folding premium hands is a path to slow attrition. By raising to 800 (roughly 2.5x Flash's raise), I:
1. Define my hand strength and take down the pot if possible
2. Build a pot when I have superior equity against a likely wide button range
3. Leave myself with enough chips (5350 remaining) to continue postflop if called
4. Stay active and aggressive as the tournament progresses (hand 79/150)

This is a standard aggressive play with premium holdings from the SB when short-stacked.

Flash opened to 300 and Claude 3‑bet to 800, leaving the pot at 1,200 and me needing 700 more to call. That means I’m getting ~36.8% pot odds (700 / (1,200+700)). My hand, 5♠4♠, is a speculative suited connector but is a marginal holding facing a polarized SB 3‑bet and the button who often continues after being 3‑bet. Multiway (or vs a strong 3‑bet) my equity with 5-4s will typically be well below the ~37% needed to make a +EV call. A minimum 4‑bet to 1,300 would be a thin bluff and can easily backfire against Claude’s stack (5,400) or a calling button. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the right long‑term play. I will fold this spot.

I opened to 300 from the button with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo), and Claude 3-bet to 800 from the small blind. With Minni folding, the action is back to me. ATo is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Claude's 3-bet sizing is relatively small, requiring only 500 more to call into a pot of 1200. I have direct position on Claude for the remainder of the hand and plenty of chips to navigate post-flop. Calling allows me to keep his range wide and use my positional advantage without bloating the pot or risking a 4-bet shove from his 54BB stack.

With QK (Queen-high) on an Ace-high board, I don't have a made hand and face a very dangerous board texture. My short stack (5,400 chips) makes preserving capital critical. Checking allows me to: 1) Gather information about Flash's holding without committing more chips speculatively; 2) Keep the pot manageable with a draw-heavy holding that has limited made-hand value; 3) Avoid putting chips in with Queen-high against an opponent who called my aggressive preflop raise. If Flash bets into me, I can make an informed decision based on pot odds and stack-to-pot ratios. My overcards still have equity if needed, but betting first with this hand and stack size is unnecessarily risky.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a board of A-T-7. This is a very strong hand, especially in a three-handed game. Claude 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked to me on the flop. While he could be trapping with a set of Tens or Sevens, it is more likely he has a big Ace (AK, AQ, AJ) that he is playing cautiously or a pocket pair like JJ/QQ. I need to bet for value and to build the pot. A bet of 600 into the 1700 pot is a good size to get called by his range while keeping the pot manageable if he decides to check-raise.

Calling 600 with QK is the correct play in this short-stack spot. My hand has strong equity against Flash's mixed range (draws, weaker pairs, bluffs). While I'm nearly pot-committed after calling, I need to be aggressive with premium hands to accumulate chips. The 16 outs give me reasonable equity to hit on the turn/river. Folding would be giving up with a strong hand in a spot where I need to stay active and build my stack during hand 79/150 of the tournament.

Flash checked the turn on a very unfavorable board texture for my QK. With no made hand (just Queen-high) and only 4800 chips remaining, betting Queen-high into an opponent who just called my flop bet lacks fold equity and risks unnecessary chips. Checking allows me to: (1) see the river card for free with 16 outs; (2) avoid committing chips speculatively with a marginal hand; (3) better position myself for river decisions if Flash bets. While I need to accumulate chips, doing so with Queen-high when checked to is unnecessarily risky given my short-stack status and lack of a made hand.

I have top two pair (Aces and Tens) on an extremely dry board (As Td 7h 3c). In a 3-handed game, this hand is effectively the nuts, as the only hands that beat me (sets of 7s, 10s, or Aces) are statistically unlikely given the pre-flop action and my own blockers. Claude 3-bet from the small blind and has now checked both the flop and the turn. This suggests he either has a big Ace (AK, AQ, AJ) that he is playing cautiously or a medium-to-high pocket pair (KK, QQ, JJ) that he's trying to get to showdown with. With Claude having approximately 4,750 chips remaining and a pot of 2,900, I need to bet for value to ensure I can realistically get his stack in by the river. A bet of 1,400 is approximately half the pot; it is large enough to build the pot significantly while likely keeping his Ace-high range interested.

Calling 1400 with QK on As-Td-7h-3c. I have ~10 outs (3 Kings, 3 Queens, 4 Jacks for Broadway straight) giving me approximately 21.7% equity on the river. While pot odds require 24.6% equity, the marginal shortfall is justified by tournament dynamics: I'm 48 BB with a premium hand in a critical accumulation phase (hand 79/150). At this stack depth, I'm nearly pot-committed anyway (3400 chips remain vs 5700 final pot), and folding QK repeatedly leads to tournament death by attrition. QK has solid equity against Flash's mixed range, and hitting my outs would provide crucial chip momentum to extend my tournament life.

River with A-high (QK on As-Td-7h-3c-3h board). Short-stacked at 3400 chips in a 5700-chip pot with a strong unpaired hand. Flash checked to me after aggressive turn betting, suggesting either a made pair (likely Ace) or a missed draw. Betting all-in is correct because: (1) wins the pot immediately if Flash folds or has a weaker holding; (2) I'm already nearly pot-committed at this depth; (3) at hand 79/150 in the tournament with 48 BB, aggressive accumulation play is required; (4) waiting passively leads to chip death by attrition. Even if Flash calls with an Ace, I still have outs to Broadway (any Jack), and the equity makes this a reasonable risk at this tournament stage.

I have top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a board of As-Td-7h-3c-3h. In a 3-handed tournament, this is a premium hand. Claude's betting line is slightly polarized: he 3-bet from the small blind pre-flop, then check-called the flop and turn before leading all-in on the river. While the board pairing on the river is a slight concern, the only hands that realistically beat me are 77 (which would have likely played more aggressively on the flop or turn) or a very unlikely A3. My hand beats all of his single-pair value hands like AK, AQ, and AJ, which are very much in his 3-betting range. Given the pot odds—I need to call 3,400 to win a total pot of 12,500—I only need to be right about 27% of the time. This is a clear call.

Heads-up on the button with Qc8h — a hand worth opening. Limping is passive and gives the BB good odds; I want initiative and to play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure, build the pot, and get to see how Flash reacts. Plan: continuation-bet many favorable or dry flops, value-bet if I pair the Q, and fold to a large 3-bet (or re-evaluate vs very small 3-bets). This sizing balances fold equity and postflop playability without overcommitting a big portion of my stack.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. With only two active players remaining, AJ is a very strong hand. Minni has been raising frequently from the button/small blind position. I will put in a 3-bet to 900 to build the pot, take initiative, and potentially end the hand immediately. If called, I have a strong top-pair candidate to play post-flop.

Facing a 3‑bet to 900 after my open to 300 with Qc8h heads‑up. Calling costs me 600 more (900 total) into a ~1200 pot — about 3:1 pot odds, so I need ~25% equity. In position with a marginal but playable hand I can realize equity, control the pot, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss. Q8o is too weak to 4‑bet for value or as a reliable bluff, and folding surrenders too many profitable postflop spots against a wide heads‑up 3‑bet range. Plan: call 900, play cautiously on flop (check/muck versus strong action, bet for value when I pair the Q or have strong draws).

On a flop of Ac-Kh-3c, I have top pair with a Jack kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up 3-bet pot. I am the pre-flop aggressor and this board favors my range significantly. I will lead out with a continuation bet of approximately 36% of the pot (650 chips) to extract value from King-high hands, weaker Aces, or flush draws, while keeping the pot size manageable and maintaining initiative.

Board: A♣ K♥ 3♣. I hold Q♣8♥. I checked and Flash bets a small 650 into the 2450 pot (to call 650). Flash’s preflop 3‑bet range is weighted toward A/K and big pairs but heads‑up it will contain a lot of bluffs and wide broadway hands as well. The bet is small — calling 650 here costs little relative to my stack and gives me position. Pure pot‑odds: calling 650 to see the turn (final pot ≈ 3100) requires roughly ~21% equity. My obvious outs (3 queens) give ~12–13% to pair by the river and the remote two‑card club flush chance adds another ~4% or so — combined equity is borderline (~15–18%). Given position, the small price to see a turn, and the ability to fold to genuine turn strength, calling is the most +EV line. Raising here with Q8 is overly ambitious (I don’t block Ax/ Kx and would get in tough spots). Plan: call 650, see the turn; if I pair the Q or pick up club equity I’ll press for value or raise selectively, but if I miss and face significant turn aggression I will fold to protect my stack.

On the turn, the 2s is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture. I have top pair with a Jack kicker, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up 3-bet pot. I will continue betting for value to extract chips from weaker Aces, Kings, and flush draws. A bet of 1,600 (approximately half the pot) keeps the pressure on Minni's marginal hands and draws while allowing me to control the pot size if she decides to raise.

Fold. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, c‑bet on A♣ K♥ 3♣, then a big turn bet to 1,600) is polarized toward strong Ax/Kx and big pairs; the sizing here is too large for a speculative call. The pot is 4,700 and calling 1,600 requires ≈25% equity. After the 2♠ turn I no longer have a realistic draw (a club flush is dead with one card to come) — my only clean outs are three queens and three eights (≈6 outs, ~13% raw), many of which still lose to Ax/Kx. Raising as a bluff is unattractive because I don’t block A/K combos and would be dominated when called. Preserving ~14.95k for better spots is the +EV play, so I fold.

I am on the button with 8d 7d, which is a very strong suited connector hand for heads-up play. With a significant chip lead and the advantage of position, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain aggression, as I have done in previous hands.

I’m in the big blind with J♣4♣ facing a button open to 300. Calling costs 200 more into a 400 pot (≈33% immediate pot odds) and J4s has decent heads-up playability (suit, some backdoor straight/club potential) against a wide BTN opening range. Flash is an active opener and with deep stacks I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand by 3-betting to the minimum; calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me see a flop and evaluate. I’ll call the raise and proceed postflop. (Total committed to pot after action = 300.)

I have J♣4♣ on a 7♣-4♦-T♥ flop — pair of fours (weak/low pair) with a decent J kicker and a backdoor club possibility. Pot is 600 and I’m out of position vs an active opener who raised preflop; this hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to tens, sevens and sets. I want pot control and to let the opener c-bet with bluffs or worse hands; checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to call reasonable bets to realize equity while folding to large, polarized aggression. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the turn (check-call small/medium bets, fold to big overbets).

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a 10-7-4 rainbow board. This is a strong hand in heads-up play, but it is vulnerable to overcards. Minni checked to me, and since I have position and the initiative from pre-flop, I will place a continuation bet. This bet serves to extract value from weaker hands like draws (5-6, 6-8) or smaller pairs (4x) and provides protection against overcards that might peel. A bet of slightly over half-pot is standard here.

Call. Total committed after calling = 650 chips (300 preflop + 350 now). I have bottom pair J♣4♣ on 7♣–4♦–T♥: vulnerable but reasonable showdown value plus a backdoor club/runner-runner improvement. Flash is an active BTN opener who will c-bet a wide, sometimes-bluffed range; his 350 into a 950 pot is a small sizing. Calling 350 into a 950 pot means I need roughly 350 / (950+350) ≈ 27% equity to break even — against a typical c-bet range my pair should have enough equity, and the call is tiny relative to my 14.65k stack. I won’t inflate the pot OOP with a bluff-raise; plan is to call and re-evaluate on the turn: call small/medium bets to realize equity, fold to large polarized overbets, and only raise or commit further if I improve to two pair or better or pick up a clear read that Flash is bluffing.

Check. I have bottom pair (4♣) with a decent J kicker but I’m out of position against an aggressive, wide opener who c-bet the flop. The 9♥ on the turn is a relatively blank for my holding (it helps some 9x combos) and doesn’t materially improve me — my only realistic clean outs are Js/4s to make two pair/trips (small chance). Betting OOP here would bloating the pot with a marginal hand, get called by better hands, and fold out only worse hands; that’s thin value and exposes me to raises. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable turn bets to realize equity, and keeps bluffs in his range. Plan: check and call small-to-medium bets (I’ll fold to a large polarized shove or big overbet), and if he checks back proceed to the river and re-evaluate.

On the turn, the 9h has improved my hand significantly, giving me an open-ended straight draw (6 or J) to go with my middle pair of sevens. Minni has checked to me for the second time. While betting could put pressure on her marginal hands or draws, my pair of sevens provides decent showdown value in a heads-up pot. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and potentially hit a straight on the river without the risk of being check-raised off my hand. This 'pot control' strategy is effective here as I am ahead of her air and draws, but behind her tens or better made hands.

Check. I have only bottom pair (4♣) with a J kicker and the 8♠ river does not improve me. Flash c‑bet the flop but checked the turn, which signals a wide, often weak/middle range that includes many overcards I beat but also a lot of hands that currently crush me (T/9/7, any completed J/6 straights, trips). Betting to “steal” is thin value — it will fold pure air but get raised or called by all the hands that beat me, and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding. Pot control to showdown is the lowest variance, most +EV line here: check and show down; if Flash fires a small bet I’ll consider a call, but I will fold to a large polarized river bet.

On the river, the board is 10-9-8-7-4, which means any Jack or Six makes a straight. I have two pair (Eights and Sevens), which is a strong hand in heads-up play, but the four-card straight on the board makes it very vulnerable. Minni has checked to me for the third time (counting the turn and river). While I could bet for value, there is a significant risk that she has a Jack or a Six and is waiting to check-raise, or that she will fold everything I beat and only call with hands that beat me. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her missed draws or single-pair hands without risking a check-raise on this dangerous board.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with pocket 4s and deep stacks. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but pocket pairs perform well when I take the initiative heads-up. A standard open to ~3x the BB (300 total) achieves several goals: isolates the BB, builds the pot when I'm often ahead, denies free cards, and preserves postflop position. I can still set-mine if called and have fold equity vs many hands. If Flash 3-bets big I'll fold; if he cold 3-bets small I'll consider calling and playing carefully. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and play the hand proactively.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-trey offsuit is a standard hand to defend in the Big Blind against a button open. Minni's 3x raise is consistent with her previous play. While my kicker is weak, folding an Ace here would be too tight. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the strength of my hand relative to the board.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a very dry board (A-7-2). In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand, but out of position, it is often best played as a check-call to allow the opponent to bluff or bet with a wider range of hands, such as pocket pairs or air. Betting here might force out the hands I beat while keeping me in against better Aces. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

I raised preflop to take the initiative heads‑up and will continue that plan on this checked Ace‑2‑7 rainbow flop. I have a small pocket pair (44) — a marginal made hand that loses to any Ace or big pair but beats most single overcards (Kx/Qx/Jx) and many bluffs/called hands from the BB. A continuation bet here both denies free cards and folds out a lot of those worse hands while allowing me to take the pot down now. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300) — big enough to apply pressure but not so large that I’m committed with a weak pair. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll be prepared to fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re‑evaluate depending on the card and his action.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, albeit with a weak trey kicker. On this dry board (A-7-2), I am likely in a 'way ahead or way behind' scenario. Minni's 1/2 pot bet is a standard continuation bet. If I raise, I risk bloating the pot and isolating myself against better Aces or rare sets (77, 22), while folding out her bluffs. By calling, I keep her range wide, allow her to continue betting with air on later streets, and keep the pot size manageable given my kicker strength. I will check-call this street.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker is very weak (3). On a dry board like A-7-2-8, I am likely either far ahead or far behind. If I bet, I may fold out her bluffs and only get called by better Aces or sets. By checking, I control the pot size and give her the opportunity to continue bluffing with hands like KQ or JT, or to bet for thin value with a seven. I plan to check-call this street to see a river and keep her range as wide as possible.

Heads‑up on the button with pocket 4s. I opened pre and c‑bet the A‑7‑2 rainbow flop; Flash called. Turn 8♣ is a relatively blank card and Flash checked, showing either weakness or pot control (could be A‑x, a middle pair, or air). My 4s have showdown value but are easily dominated by Ax or any pocket pair >4. Betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and expose me to a costly check‑raise with deep stacks; it also won’t get value from the hands I lose to. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the river. Plan: call small/medium river bets to see showdown; fold to very large/overbet river aggression if it looks strong.

On the river board of As-7d-2h-8c-9c, I have top pair with an Ace and a weak kicker (3). After calling a standard flop continuation bet from Minni, the turn went check-check. Minni's turn check suggests she either has a medium-strength hand (like a pair of sevens or a small pocket pair) that she wants to take to showdown, or a weak Ace that she is pot-controlling, or perhaps a hand that has completely missed. By checking the river, I am playing my hand as a bluff catcher. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by anything I beat except perhaps a very stubborn seven, and I would likely be called or raised by better Aces or two-pair combinations that improved on the turn or river (like A8 or A9). Checking allows Minni the opportunity to bluff with missed draws or to check back with her own showdown value, which is a safer line given my weak kicker.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the A‑7‑2 flop, Flash called and then checked the turn and the river. I hold pocket 4s on A‑7‑2‑8‑9 (board: 2h 7d As 8c 9c). This is a small pair with showdown value only versus bluffs and missed overcards; I’m behind any Ace, any pair >4, and two‑pair/trips. Betting here would bloat the pot and get called by hands that beat me while folding only pure air — there’s little value to extract and unnecessary risk with deep stacks. Flash’s passive line (call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with an Ace or a medium pair pot‑controling, so I’ll take the low‑variance line and check to showdown. If he now leads out I’ll be prepared to call small/medium bets but fold to large overbets.

In heads-up play, while I should play a wide range of hands from the button, 6-3 offsuit is at the very bottom of the starting hand hierarchy. Given that Minni has been quite active and aggressive in previous hands, I prefer to fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my significant chip lead rather than entering a pot with poor equity and playability.

I'm on the button (small blind) with Th6d heads-up. In HU the button should open a very wide range — calling the extra 50 is too passive and surrenders initiative. I want to take position and build fold equity, so I will raise to 300 (3x). This size is standard to pressure the big blind and still keep stacks manageable. With T6o I will fold to a substantial 3-bet but c-bet and proceed aggressively on favorable flops (or check/call when I hit a pair), playing cautiously when I completely miss.

In a heads-up situation, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the big blind against a standard button raise. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call for a 400 pot), and while I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding would be too tight given the strength of my hand relative to a typical wide button opening range.

On this flop of 10-7-4, I have flopped middle pair with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot, especially as the defender. However, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. I will check and plan to call a continuation bet, as my hand is well ahead of most of her air bluffs and still has equity against some of her top pairs.

Heads-up on the button, I opened preflop and have position. Flop 4c-7h-Ts gives me top pair (T6) on a fairly dry board with few realistic straight/flush draws. Flash checked, so a c-bet accomplishes three things: extracts value from worse tens/second pairs (7s/4s/medium pairs), charges any backdoor draws, and uses position to take the pot down. I don’t want to give a free turn card or let floats see cheap cards, but I also don’t want to bloat the pot too much with a marginal kicker — a 2/3-pot bet balances value and fold equity. If Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if they check-raise large I’ll likely fold given my weak kicker, while a small raise is worth a call and further evaluation. Bet 400 into the 600 pot to get value and protect my hand.

I have flopped middle pair (7s) with a strong King kicker on a fairly dry board (10-7-4 rainbow). Minni's 2/3 pot bet is a standard continuation bet for an aggressive player. In a heads-up match, middle pair is a very strong holding to defend against a c-bet. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (like 10x or overpairs) and might force out her bluffs, which I want to keep in. Therefore, the most profitable play is to call and evaluate the turn, keeping the pot manageable while remaining ahead of most of her air and semi-bluffing range.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a strong King kicker. The turn 9d makes the board more coordinated, potentially completing some straight draws (like 86) or giving Minni more semi-bluffing equity with hands like J8 or QJ. However, my hand is still very strong in a heads-up context and has significant showdown value. I will check to Minni to control the pot size and allow her to continue bluffing with her air or weaker draws. I plan to call a reasonably sized bet to reach the river.

I have top pair (T6) with a weak kicker. I c-bet the flop (400) and Flash called; the turn 9d is a somewhat scary card because it completes some straights and gives 9x two-pair combos, but many of Flash’s calling hands are still worse tens, small pairs, or floats. Flash checked the turn, which signals weakness or pot control — a bet here should extract value from worse tens/pairs and deny free cards to any backdoor combinations. I’ll make a half-pot value/protection bet (700 into the 1400 pot). If Flash check-raises big I’ll strongly consider folding the weak-kicker top pair; a small check-raise I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the board is now 10-7-4-9. I hold middle pair (sevens) with a King kicker. Minni's bet of 700 into a 1400 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds, requiring me to be ahead only 25% of the time to justify a call. In a heads-up match, middle pair is a significant hand and folding to a half-pot bet would be overly tight and exploitable, especially against an aggressive opponent who could be semi-bluffing with various straight draws (like J8, QJ, or 8x) or betting a wide range of hands. Raising would likely only result in being called by superior hands (like top pair or better), so calling is the most profitable way to reach the river and realize my hand's showdown value.

I hold a middle pair of sevens (Kd 7c) on a board that has become increasingly coordinated and contains two overcards (Ts and As). My hand has some showdown value in a heads-up context, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands. Checking is the most appropriate action here, as it allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown if Minni checks back with her marginal hands or missed draws. If she bets, I will have to evaluate her sizing and the likelihood that she is bluffing with missed straight draws (like 8x, QJ, or J8) versus value betting an Ace or a Ten.

River A♠ is an overcard to my T6 and significantly reduces the value of my top pair. Flash’s line (call flop 400, call turn 700, then check river) is consistent with either a pot‑control Ace or a ten/single pair — both of which beat or split me a large chunk of the time. Betting risks getting called by better hands (any A, two‑pair) or getting check‑raised, and my weak kicker makes continuing to a raise a clear mistake. A small “thin value” bet could fold out some air, but the frequency of worse hands that will call is low and the downside if I’m raised is large. With stacks deep relative to the pot and my hand deteriorated on the river, the safest, highest‑EV line is to check and take the showdown.

In a heads-up situation, K7o is a solid starting hand on the Button. I have a significant stack lead and want to maintain pressure. My standard open throughout this tournament has been 3x (300 chips), which is effective for building the pot and forcing the Big Blind to make a decision with their wider range. I will raise to 300.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Js6s — a suited jack with decent postflop playability but not a premium. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (my total commit will be 300). Pot odds: 200 / (400+200) = 33.3% break-even equity. Versus a wide button opening range my hand has about ~40%+ equity and good implied odds with deep stacks (~160BB), so calling is +EV. A min‑3bet to 500 is possible, but 3-betting with J6s bloats the pot with a marginal holding and turns a lot of postflop decisions awkward out of position; I’ll keep the pot controlled, realize equity and play the flop (raise only with a solid read that villain folds to 3-bets). Plan: call to see flop, proceed aggressively if I hit or have strong draws, and fold to heavy, non-giving action when I miss.

I have Js6s on Qh-5s-9s: I don’t have a made hand but I have a strong spade draw (4 spades visible including my two = 9 outs). Pot is 600 and we’re out of position against a wide button opener who will c‑bet a lot. I want to control the pot size and realize equity rather than bloating the pot out of position with a marginal semi-bluff. Checking lets him barrel a wide range so I can call reasonable c‑bets and use my ~35.6% chance to make the flush by the river (9/47 on the turn, ~35.6% to river), plus occasional pair/upside. Plan: check here; if Flash bets a small/medium c‑bet I’ll call and evaluate on the turn (raise only with a strong read or if I pick up a better hand). If he checks back I get a free turn to continue. I’ll fold only to heavy, polarized pressure that represents a made nut/near‑nut hand when I miss.

On the Button in a heads-up situation, I opened for 3x (300) with K7o and was called by Minni. The flop comes Qh-5s-9s, which doesn't directly hit my hand but provides a decent opportunity for a continuation bet. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should frequently bet this board to fold out Minni's weak range and random undercards. While I only have King-high, a half-pot bet (300 chips) is often enough to take the pot down right here if she missed the board or is playing a 'check-fold' strategy with her wide calling range. If she calls or raises, I can re-evaluate on the turn, as I still have some showdown value with King-high and potential runner-runner draws.

Call 300. On Qh-5s-9s I have Js6s: a strong spade draw (9 outs, ~35.6% to make the flush by the river, ~19.1% to hit on the turn) plus a small chance to pair my jack. The pot is 900 and calling 300 requires ~25% equity to break even, so this is +EV against a wide button opening/c-betting range. Flash’s 300 sizing is a standard, relatively small c-bet — he can have many bluffs and weak one-pair hands — so I prefer to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity out of position rather than inflate it with a marginal semi-bluff. With deep stacks I also have good implied odds when I hit. Plan: call to see the turn; if I complete the flush or pair a jack I’ll extract value, if the turn is a blank I’ll fold to heavy polarized pressure but call/float versus small-to-medium turns to continue. If Flash checks I get a free turn to re-evaluate.

I have J♠6♠ on Q♥5♠9♠3♠ — a made Jack-high spade flush on the turn. Villain (button) opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, then showed weakness on the turn (checking). His continuing range contains a lot of one‑pair hands and weaker spade combos that I beat, plus a minority of dominated or higher spades (A♠/K♠/Q♠) that would crush my flush. With one card to come I want to extract value while keeping the sizing small enough to be called by worse hands and to avoid getting pot‑committed versus a likely higher flush check‑raise. Pot = 1,200 — I’ll bet ~50% pot (600). This charges Qx/9x/pocket pairs and smaller spade combos, denies a free river, and leaves room to fold to a big check‑raise (which usually signifies a higher spade). If he just calls we go to the river with a lot of hands that pay me off; if he raises big I can credibly fold to preserve my stack.

On the turn, the board now shows three spades (5s, 9s, 3s). Minni, after check-calling the flop, has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. With only King-high and no spade in my hand to act as a blocker or provide a draw, I have very little equity against a range that likely includes flushes, Queens, or even middle pairs. Continuing here would be a pure bluff or a hopeful call with almost no outs. Given my stack advantage and the board texture, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 8c3d: a very weak hand, but in heads-up play you should open a wide range to exploit fold equity and your positional advantage postflop. The pot is small and the min-raise is 200; my standard open size at this table has been ~300, which puts pressure on Flash and avoids giving him a cheap flop. If he 3-bets I’ll fold this holding (poor equity vs a 3-bet); if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet selectively on favorable flops and check/fold when I miss. Raising keeps me in control and maximizes long-term EV in heads-up play.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. While the price to call is relatively low (200 chips), the hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my current chip lead, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand against Minni's frequent button raises.

Holding 9-5 offsuit on the button in a heads-up scenario. While this hand has some playability in position, it is generally at the lower end of the range. Given Minni's tendency to defend her big blind and play back, I will fold this marginal hand to avoid playing a bloated pot with low equity, preserving my current chip lead.

Heads-up on the button/SB with deep stacks. 4h6s is marginal but playable in position — enough connectivity to realize equity and to steal the BB. Limping hands here gives the BB better odds and surrenders fold equity; a standard small open preserves fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll raise to the standard heads-up size (200 total) to apply pressure, balance my opening range, and play the flop in position. If Flash folds I take the blinds; if he calls I’ll c-bet selectively on favorable runouts and control the pot when I miss; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate by sizing/read.

I am holding Ah8h in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand for heads-up play. Minni has min-raised to 200. I will 3-bet to take control of the pot and build value with my superior starting hand. A raise to 600 is a standard size to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I opened to 200 to steal in position, but Flash’s 3-bet to 600 puts me in a tough spot. The pot is 800 and I must call 400, so I need ≈33% equity just to break even (400/(800+400)). 4♥6♠ is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand with very poor equity and few meaningful blockers versus a 3-bet range (pockets, A/K, broadways, suited connectors). Calling will force difficult postflop decisions out of position against a range that will c-bet and barrel frequently; reraising (min to 1000) as a bluff is too large and too risky with this holding given opponent’s aggression and deep stacks. I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot (pairs, suited connectors, or Axs) rather than spew chips with 4-6 offsuit. Fold.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with 8-4 offsuit. While I have the chip lead and position for the rest of the hand, 8-4 offsuit is extremely weak. Minni has been playing a standard, somewhat aggressive game. I have successfully used aggression against her recently (Hand #88), but I don't want to start a habit of opening or calling with such low-equity hands. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack and wait for a better hand to leverage my positional advantage.

8♣4♣ is a marginal, low suited hand but heads-up on the button I have the postflop position. Calling 50 into a 150 pot is cheap (I need ~25% equity) and lets me see a flop and use position to realize equity. Raising to 200 with this hand unnecessarily bloats the pot and can get me into trouble against a 3-bet or 4-bet range. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and a flop in position with a speculative hand. I have already posted the 50 SB, so calling 50 brings my total contribution to 100.

I am in the Big Blind holding T3s. Minni has limped in from the Small Blind. Given that I have a weak hand and am out of position for the remainder of the hand, there is no need to bloat the pot by raising. I will take the free flop and see if I can connect.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey of spades. The flop is 4s-9h-Jh, giving me a backdoor spade flush draw but no current pair or straight draw. Since Minni limped pre-flop and the board is somewhat coordinated, I will check to see a free turn card and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

I have 8♣4♣ on a 4♠-9♥-J♥ flop — a weak pair (single 4) but I'm in position and Flash checked. Heads-up I want to use position to take the initiative: a half‑pot c-bet (100 into 200) extracts value from worse pairs/small pockets and charges heart/straight draws, while also folding out many unpaired overcards. The size is large enough to deny free cards but small enough that I can fold to a sizable check‑raise from a clearly stronger range. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises I’ll concede given how vulnerable my pair is.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey of spades. The flop is 4s-9h-Jh, giving me a backdoor spade flush draw but no current pair or straight draw. Minni limped pre-flop and has now bet 100 into a 200 chip pot. While the price to call is small (100 to win 300), my hand has very little equity on this coordinated board which likely hits a limping range. I have no overcards to the Jack or Nine, and my Ten-high has no showdown value. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with K4o. In a heads-up situation, a King-high hand is strong enough to open for a raise to maintain pressure and take control of the pot early, especially since Minni has been playing relatively balanced but susceptible to pre-flop aggression. Raising here is a standard play to pick up the blinds or see a flop with the lead.

Heads-up against a wide button opening, 9♠6♦ is marginal but has reasonable equity and playability. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (totaling 300 committed this street) — that gives ~33% breakeven pot odds, which justifies defending in the big blind rather than folding. I won't 3-bet this weak offsuit hand out of position; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me see a flop where I can continue with a pair or good draw and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards. Call to 300 total.

Heads‑up vs a wide button opener, my 9♠6♦ has an open‑ended straight draw to the J‑T‑8 flop (7 or Q completes) — 8 outs (~17% on the turn, ~31.5% by the river). With the pot at 600 I want to use fold equity and build the pot when I hit. A half‑pot semi‑bluff (~300) pressures unpaired overcards and weak Ax hands, and only needs ~33% fold frequency to break even vs calling — which is realistic versus a wide c‑betting range. If I get called I still have decent equity to improve; if I face a large raise I’ll fold (don’t want to commit with just a draw). Bet 300 to seize initiative and maximize EV from this draw.

On a very wet and coordinated board of 8h-Tc-Jh, my K4o has almost no equity. This texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, and Minni's lead (donk bet) of 300 into 600 suggests she has at least a piece of the board or a significant draw. While I have a King as an overcard and the King of Hearts as a blocker, the board connectivity makes it too dangerous to float or bluff-raise. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♥8♠ — a playable, reasonably strong hand in heads-up play. I’ve already posted 50 and face a 100 BB, pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but calling from the small blind is passive and gives the BB a cheap opportunity to see a flop in position. With deep stacks (~174 BB) and position postflop, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure. The table’s standard open size has been ~3x in many hands, and the min-raise to 200 is too small (invites cheap flops). I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB) to build the pot with initiative and fold out weaker hands or isolate. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold/disengage unless it’s small and favorable), and if he calls I’ll play the flop aggressively or pot-control depending on texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 3c. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. This hand is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the need to conserve chips for better opportunities in this heads-up/short-handed scenario, folding is the most disciplined and prudent play.

In a heads-up situation, a suited Queen like Qc 3c is definitely strong enough to open for a raise from the button. This allows me to take control of the hand and exert pressure on Minni while maintaining position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

A♣J♥ is a strong heads‑up hand vs a button open to 300. With deep stacks (≈17.4k vs 22.2k) I want to take the initiative and get value/fold equity rather than passively calling OOP. A min 3‑bet to 500 is too small and invites too many cheap flops, so I'll make a standard 3‑bet to 900 to isolate weaker Ax/Kx and medium pairs and to pressure speculative holdings. If Flash 4‑bets very large I'll re‑evaluate (fold to a shove or call/plan accordingly vs a reasonable sizing); if he calls I'll proceed aggressively with c‑bets on favorable textures and control pot size on dangerous boards. Total chips committed preflop = 900.

Minni's 3-bet to 900 represents a strong range. Although I have position as the button in this heads-up situation, Queen-Trey suited is too weak to justify calling a 3-bet. It is likely dominated by many hands in her 3-betting range (like AQ, KQ, or even QJ) and does not play well against pocket pairs or Ace-high hands. Given that I folded a stronger hand like King-Eight suited to a similar 3-bet in Hand #73, folding here is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with deep stacks. 6♦3♦ is a weak but playable hand in position — suited with some connectivity and good postflop playability. Against a single big blind I want initiative and to apply pressure, so I’ll open-raise a standard 2x BB (to 200). This sizing keeps the pot manageable, gives fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; call small 3‑bets only rarely; if Flash calls I’ll c‑bet many dry flops and use position to realize equity and pick spots to barrel or check back when the board is unfavorable.

In Hand #94, I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, requiring me to call 100 more to see a flop. With a stack of 22,050 and 9-high offsuit, the hand is marginal but playable given the pot odds and the head-up nature of the game. However, 9-6 offsuit doesn't flop particularly well and Minni has been aggressive throughout the session. Folding here preserves my stack and allows me to wait for a better spot, though calling to defend the blind is also a viable defensive strategy. Given the current tournament status and my slight lead, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a more advantageous position.

I am on the button (SB) with Th7s. In a heads-up situation, T7o is a marginal but playable hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs me only 50 to call. However, Minni has been playing aggressively recently. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand or potentially win the pot pre-flop. Given the stack sizes and the blinds, a standard 3x raise is appropriate for exploratory purposes and to maintain pressure.

Heads-up facing a 3x SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total committed 300), so my break-even equity is ~33%. J♣5♣ is a playable heads-up defense — suited with backdoor flush/straight potential and can make top pair. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a thin minimum 3-bet; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity postflop and exploit lines on favorable boards. Plan: see the flop, continue with pairs/draws, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I miss.

Out of position on A♠ 9♥ 2♣ with J♣5♣ — no pair, only a backdoor club draw (one club on board) and J‑high. Opponent opened wide preflop so he has lots of A‑x and broadway combos that dominate me. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep; I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with very marginal equity. I’ll check for pot control: call a small/medium bet to see a turn (especially if it’s a club or a J/5), fold to heavy aggression, and re-evaluate on the turn. If he checks back I take a free card and can barrel later if I pick up equity.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace on the board strongly favors my range. Minni checked, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent having an Ace or a strong pair and to put pressure on her. Since I currently have nothing but Ten high, betting is my best chance to win the pot immediately.

Heads-up, out of position on A♠ 9♥ 2♣ with J♣5♣. Flash opened wide preflop and his small c‑bet (300 into a 900 pot) is a standard continuation – cheap to call. Pot math: calling 300 into an effective pot of 1200 (900 + 300) requires ~25% equity to break even. J5♣♣ has roughly that versus a wide c‑betting range plus useful turn/river improvement potential (pairing a J/5 or runner‑runner clubs). Raising OOP with this marginal hand would bloat the pot and commit me with little fold equity; folding is too weak given the price. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, and if I hit a J/5 or pick up club equity apply pressure or extract value depending on the turn action.

I improved to a pair of 5s on the turn (Jc5c → 5h on turn). Flash checked to me — his check contains a lot of A‑x/float and air after a small flop c‑bet. With heads‑up dynamics, second pair has decent value vs his checking range and is worth extracting from worse Ax/9x/float hands. Pot is ~1200 so a modest value bet (≈1/2 pot = 600) gets called by worse while keeping the pot manageable OOP. If he check‑raises I’ll fold — I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with only a weak pair. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and size accordingly.

On the turn, the board is 2c As 9h 5h. I have Ten-high with no draws. Minni has led out for 600 chips into a 1200 chip pot after check-calling the flop. This 'donk lead' typically indicates a hand that has improved or a player trying to take control of the pot. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve to a winning hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and conserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with QJo — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Facing 50 to call into a 150 pot I don’t want to limp; I should take the initiative. The minimum raise is to 200, but a standard 3x BB (300 total) applies pressure, builds the pot with a hand that plays well in position, and narrows Flash’s range. Plan: if called, use position to continuation-bet on favorable flops; if met with a large 3-bet, re-evaluate (likely fold to a big 3-bet, consider calling small 3-bets depending on sizing).

In heads-up play, Ace-Six offsuit is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard button raise. Minni has been raising frequently from the button, and I have a comfortable chip lead. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that often holds significant equity against a wide opening range, while keeping the pot size controlled.

On a monotone flop of 9d-4d-7d, I have Ace-high with no diamonds. This is a very coordinated board that could easily have hit Minni's opening range or given her a flush draw. As the player in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I have no reason to lead out here without a diamond or a pair.

Heads-up on the button with Q♣J♦ on 9♦-4♦-7♦ (monotone diamond flop). I have one diamond (J♦) giving me a strong 4-card diamond draw (9 outs) plus two overcards — roughly ~35% turn-to-river equity vs a calling range — so this is an ideal semi-bluff. Flash checked; pot is 600. I want fold equity against many non-diamond hands (9x, 7x, weak A/K highs, small pairs) while still getting value from worse draws and weaker made hands that will call. Bet half the pot (300) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (diamond = made flush / continue for value; blank = decide whether to barrel or pot-control based on opponent), but I’ll respect a large check-raise (likely a made flush) and fold to heavy aggression.

On a monotone flop of 9d-4d-7d, my hand (Ac 6h) has very little equity. I have no pair and, more importantly, no diamond to draw to a flush. Minni's continuation bet is standard, but without any piece of the board or a backdoor draw, continuing would be a mistake. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation with Ace-Ten offsuit (Ad Th), which is a premier starting hand in this context. My goal is to extract value and maintain pressure. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) has been my consistent opening size and is appropriate here to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up against the button's standard open to 300, K♣8♠ is marginal but worth defending from the big blind. The open range is wide; I have decent equity and useful blockers to some strong Kx hands. With deep stacks, calling 200 more (total 300 committed) is cheap and keeps postflop options open. 3-betting with K8o is too thin — often dominated or a bluff that can get punished — so I’ll call and evaluate the flop: continue with top pair, two pair or strong draws; pot-control and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards without improvement; exploit weaker turns/rivers when appropriate.

Heads-up vs the button’s standard open, I defended Kc8s preflop. Flop Qc 5d 3h is very dry and connects well to the raiser’s range (lots of Qx, broadways and small pairs). I have no pair — just K‑high with a backdoor club — so I’m not strong enough to lead for value and don’t want to bluff into a range that contains many hands that will raise or call. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker bluffs in the opponent’s range, and lets me call small continuation bets to realize equity. Plan: check now. If Flash checks back I take a free turn; if he bets small (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot ≈ 300) I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn; if he applies heavy pressure (pot‑sized or a raise) I’ll fold to avoid bloating the pot with just K‑high.

On this dry Queen-high board (5d 3h Qc), I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong Ace-high hand with two overcards to the low cards and backdoor straight potential. Minni's check is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of 325 (slightly over half-pot) to maintain the initiative, deny equity to her air hands, and potentially fold out middle pairs or random holdings that missed the flop.

Heads‑up I defended Kc8s from the big blind and checked this dry flop (Qc 5d 3h). Flash’s 325 c‑bet (pot now 925) is a modest size and gives me ~26% pot odds to call (325 into a 1250 pot after I call). I have no pair but reasonable showdown value vs a wide button c‑bet range and Kc is a useful blocker to some strong Kx/KQ combos. I don’t want to bluff‑raise or 3‑bet with K8o (I’d be often dominated), so I’ll call and see a turn. Plan: fold to heavy, pot‑sized pressure on the turn without improvement; if I pick up a K or get a blank turn I can re‑evaluate for a barrel/fold or a delayed bluff/value line depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

Check. The A♥ on the turn is a big overcard to my K♣8♠ and significantly improves the raiser’s continuing range (Ax, Qx, sets), so I don’t have showdown-ready value or reliable fold equity to bet as a bluff. Checking controls the pot (~1,250) with a marginal hand, preserves my stack, and lets me fold to turn/river aggression if Flash shows strength. Plan: check and evaluate his action — call a very small turn stab to realize equity only if sizing is tiny, fold to pot-sized or larger pressure, and if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river for a delayed bluff or a safe showdown line depending on the runout and his tendencies.

The turn Ace is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor and gives me top pair with a strong kicker. Minni's check-call on the flop and check on the turn suggests a range consisting of Queens, pocket pairs, or perhaps a weak Ace that just improved. I will bet 750 (60% of the pot) to extract value from those hands while maintaining my table image as an aggressive player. This size is large enough to build the pot but small enough to keep her marginal hands in the pot.

I called preflop and the dry flop; the turn A♥ heavily improves the raiser’s continuing range (A‑x, Qx, sets) and they now bet 750 into a 2000 pot. That pricing gives me ~27% pot odds to call (750 to win ~2750). My hand (Kc8s) has no pair and limited outs (mainly a river King), so I’m behind most of the thin value lines the opponent is representing, but the sizing is small enough that a call is cheap to realize equity and deny bluffs. I won’t raise (we’re dominated too often) and folding now surrenders too much equity for the price. Plan: call the 750 to see the river; fold to sizable river aggression if I don’t improve, but consider calling or thin‑value betting on a King or very passive river.

I hold Kc8s on a Qc 5d 3h Ah Tc board — I never improved from the K‑high I had on the turn. There are only two clubs on board (Qc, Tc) so no flush; the river Ten actually helps the raiser’s continuing range (adds Tx, and completes broadway straights like KJ). Flash’s line (open, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is very consistent with Ax, Qx, Tx and a few straights/sets — hands that beat me. I have almost no showdown value and very little fold equity as a river bluff (my K only removes a few combos). Betting would be an unprofitable bluff a large portion of the time and a thin value bet vs worse hands is rare. Standard, lowest‑variance line is to check: if he checks back I save chips; if he fires a small bet I can call to realize equity vs pure bluffs but I’ll fold to pot‑sized/large river aggression. Action: check.

On the river, I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Tens). The board is relatively dry, although King-Jack would have completed a straight. Given Minni's line of check-calling the flop and turn, her range is heavily weighted toward one-pair hands like a Queen or a weaker Ace (AJ, A9-A2). It is unlikely she has a monster like AQ or a set, as she probably would have raised the turn or led the river. I have a very strong hand in a heads-up situation and must bet for value. A bet of 1750 (approximately 63% of the pot) is large enough to extract significant value from her marginal made hands while not being so large that she's forced to fold everything but better hands.

I checked the river with K♣8♠ and Flash bets 1,750 into a 4,500 pot. Calling costs 1,750 to win 6,250 (pot+call) so I need ≈28% equity to break even. Flash’s line — open preflop, small flop c‑bet, a turn barrel on the A, and another river stab — is textbook for Qx/Ax/Tx and occasional sets/two‑pair; those hands cleanly beat K‑high. He’s shown willing to use small sizing for value, so pure bluffs look like a minority. My K♣ blocks a few Kx combos but not enough to flip the math. Raising would be a naked bluff with almost no fold equity and huge downside. Given the range and my likely equity (<28%), the +EV play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with 6♠3♦ is a weak holding, but in heads-up play the button should open very wide. I’ll raise to the minimum (200 total) to take the initiative and use positional advantage to pressure the big blind. This is a low-cost attempt to steal or play postflop in position; if Flash folds I pick up the blinds, if he calls I can c-bet or control the pot depending on the flop texture. I will fold to a sizable 3-bet since this hand has poor equity vs a 3-betting range, but can consider calling very small, suspect 3-bets occasionally.

I am holding Jack-Ten suited (Jd Td), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened with a minimum raise to 200. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or build a larger pot with a hand that has great post-flop playability. Given the deep stacks, a raise to 600 is a standard size to put pressure on the opener.

I opened to 200 on the button with 6♠3♦ and Flash responded with a 3-bet to 600. Pot is 800 and it’s 400 to call (~33% pot odds). 6‑3o has very poor equity versus a typical 3‑bet range (roughly 20–25%) and is often dominated; despite having position postflop the hand has little playability and suffers reverse implied odds. A 4‑bet bluff here is too thin (no meaningful blockers and the opponent can comfortably call or shove), so the +EV play is to preserve my stack and fold. I had planned to fold to a sizable 3‑bet, so I’ll lay this one down.

In heads-up play, Ace-Nine offsuit is a premium starting hand and should be played aggressively from the button (Small Blind). I have a chip lead over Minni and have been effective in taking down pots with pre-flop raises recently. I will open for the standard 3x big blind (300 total) to build a pot with my range advantage and put pressure on the Big Blind.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with A♦6♣ — a strong heads-up hand but vulnerable out of position. The pot is 400 and it’s only 200 to call, so calling is cheap, but calling keeps me OOP on every street and lets Flash see flops cheaply with a wide opening range. I prefer to seize the initiative and isolate with a standard 3-bet size to get value and fold equity. Raising to 1000 (~3.3x the open) is large enough to fold many of his weaker limbs and define his range, but small enough relative to our deep stacks to keep maneuverability postflop. Plan postflop: continue-barrel A-high and connected boards, seek value when I pair the A or 6, and give up to heavy action if I miss and face strong aggression. Against a big 4-bet shove I’ll fold A6o; against a small 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing. Overall this play maximizes equity and exploits the opponent’s wide open range in heads-up play.

In heads-up play, Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand that is well ahead of a wide range of hands. Minni's 3-bet to 1,000 is significant, but folding here would be too exploitable given our positional advantage and the strength of our hand. Calling allows us to play a pot in position and see a flop without overcommitting our stack to a potential 4-bet battle where we might be dominated. We will call the additional 700 to see the flop.

I 3‑bet preflop and now see Q♠ K♠ 3♦ with A♦6♣. This is a relatively dry board (two overcards but only a two‑card spade draw) and my Ace is a valuable blocker to Ax/Asx combos. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to Flash’s wide SB defending range: a standard c‑bet of ~50% pot will fold many marginal hands and get value from worse Axs and some draws. If he check‑raises big I’ll release (A6 has poor equity vs strong ranges); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP — continue when I pair the A or the turn is inert, check-control on spade turns or heavy aggression. Betting keeps the pot manageable while maximizing fold equity and extracting thin value.

The flop [Qs Ks 3d] is extremely favorable for Minni's pre-flop 3-betting range, which likely includes strong pairs like AA, KK, QQ, and high cards like AK, AQ, or KQ. My Ace-high (As 9h) has very little equity against this range and no significant draws. Minni's half-pot continuation bet indicates she likely has a piece of this board or a strong pocket pair. Continuing with the hand would require me to either bluff at a board that hits her range or hope for one of my few outs (an Ace), which still might not be the best hand. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button with Th4c — very wide opening range is standard here and I have postflop position. Pot is 150 and only 50 to call; a min-raise to 200 buys fold equity, defines my range, and keeps the pot manageable if called. With a weak but playable hand I want to apply pressure rather than limp; I will fold to a significant 3-bet but, if called, use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot. Raise to the minimum (200) to steal or play position postflop.

With 9d 7h in the Big Blind, I am facing a min-raise from Minni. The pot odds are 3-to-1 (100 to call for a potential 400 pot), which is more than enough to defend with a hand like 97o in a heads-up situation. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop out of position.

I have an open-ended straight draw (6-7-8-9) on a paired board (8-8-6). Being out of position as the Big Blind, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react and potentially realize my equity or see a free card.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with Th4c on 8h 8d 6c. This is a very dry, paired flop with almost no draws and many of Flash’s calling hands (overcards, small pairs) will fold to pressure. I have no pair and very little equity, so I want to use my position and role as aggressor to take the pot down with a small continuation bet rather than give a free card. A ~1/3 pot bet (150) applies fold equity, represents an 8 or a strong overpair, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds I scoop; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only with improvement or a clear spot to continue as a bluff; if he raises I’ll give it up most of the time since my hand has little showdown value versus a polarized raise.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 9-7 on an 8-8-6 board. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a 400-chip pot. I am getting roughly 3.6-to-1 pot odds (150 to call for a total pot of 700), which is more than enough to justify continuing with 8 outs to a straight. While the paired board carries some risk if Minni has an 8, I cannot fold such a strong draw to a small bet. Calling is the most appropriate move to keep the pot manageable while I attempt to improve on the turn.

On the turn, the 10 of clubs completes my straight (6-7-8-9-10). While the board is paired with eights, making a full house possible, a straight is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. I will check to the pre-flop and flop aggressor, Minni, to allow her to continue betting with her bluffs or semi-bluffs, or to see how she responds to the turn card. If she bets, I will likely check-raise or call to maximize value.

Turn pairs my Ten, giving me two pair (Tens and Eights) with Th4c on 8h 8d 6c Tc. Heads‑up this is a very strong hand versus Flash’s flop‑call range (many overcards and single pairs). Flash checked the turn, so I want value and to deny a free river — a medium value bet will be called by worse hands while keeping my risk manageable. I’ll bet ~400 into the 700 pot. If Flash check‑raises big or jams, I’ll give it up (those lines usually indicate trips/full house/quads); if he simply calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and extract more or control the pot accordingly.

On the turn, the 10 of clubs completes my straight (6-7-8-9-10). Although the board is paired with eights, making a full house possible, a straight is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has continued her aggression with a bet of 400 into a 700-chip pot. To maximize value from hands like trips (an 8), top pair (a 10), or even overpairs, I will put in a raise. A raise to 1000 (an increase of 600 over her bet) builds the pot and forces her to decide whether to commit more with her range. If she is bluffing, she likely folds to any aggression, but if she has a piece of the board, I want to get paid now.

I call to 1,000 total (600 more). I’ve turned two pair (Tens & Eights) which is a very strong heads‑up hand and has a lot of showdown value. Flash’s check‑raise is polarizing and certainly contains value (8x trips, pocket 6s, tens turned to trips, some 9‑7 straights/full houses), but those combos are a relatively small subset of all possible two‑card combos. The pot is 2,100 and I only need to call 600 more, so my immediate pot odds are ~18.2% (pot becomes ~3,300 after my call) — that’s right around the frequency of hands that strictly dominate me, and I expect a fair number of his check‑raises to be bluffs or worse pairs. Raising here would bloating the pot and can get me committed versus the very few combos that crush me; folding is too nitty given the price. I’ll call, see the river, and then fold to clear strength (big river shove or very polarised action) but extract value or call smaller river bets from worse hands.

The river is an Eight, making the board 8-8-6-T-8. My straight (6-7-8-9-T) is now significantly devalued because the board has tripled the eights. Any player holding a Ten or a Six now has a full house (8-8-8-T-T or 8-8-8-6-6), which beats my straight. Given that Minni raised pre-flop and has shown aggression on the flop and turn, it is highly probable she has a Ten, a Six, or even the case Eight in her range. Since I was the aggressor on the turn with a check-raise, checking the river is the most prudent move. It allows me to see if she bets and evaluate the size of that bet. I cannot realistically bet for value here because almost everything that calls me has me beaten (Full Houses or Quads), and I only chop with exactly another 9-7.

River: board 8h 8d 6c Tc 8c — I currently make a full house, eights full of tens (888TT). That’s a very strong hand but also fragile vs the specific portion of villain’s turn check‑raise range that actually beats me: any 8 in Flash’s hand (quads) and pocket tens (TT). A lone Ten in his hand merely ties. Flash’s line (call pre, call flop, a big check‑raise on the turn) is highly polarized and exactly consistent with those two dangerous categories, so a river bet from me is likely to be met by a large raise from the small subset of hands that crush me. Betting here would risk building a huge pot against quads/TT for only thin value from the relatively rare worse full houses/pairs. With deep stacks relative to the pot I prefer the low‑variance, +EV line: check back to get to showdown and avoid inducing a river trap. Therefore: check (amount 0).

In a heads-up situation, the button (Small Blind) has a significant positional advantage. However, Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand. While it is common to open a wide range in this position, Minni has been defending aggressively and three-betting frequently in recent hands. Given the marginal nature of the hand and the opponent's current level of aggression, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure rather than playing a weak hand out of position or inducing a three-bet.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Jc6c — a suited, playable hand in position. With pot 150 and only 50 to call, I can either limp or take the initiative. I prefer to raise: a standard 3xBB (to 300) gives good fold equity, builds pots when I’m ahead, and preserves postflop flexibility with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable boards, realize equity on connected/slash-suited flops). Raising to 300 is the +EV line here.

I am holding pocket deuces in the Big Blind. In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand, but deuces are vulnerable post-flop. Minni has been raising frequently from the Button. Calling the 200-chip raise is the most prudent play here; it allows me to 'set mine' for a three-of-a-kind while keeping the pot manageable. If the flop doesn't improve my hand, I can evaluate based on the board texture and Minni's aggression. 3-betting here would likely just bloat the pot out of position with a hand that is rarely a huge favorite.

I have pocket deuces on a monotone flop of all diamonds. I do not hold a diamond. This is a very dangerous board for my hand, as any single diamond in Minni's range has me in a terrible spot, and any overcard (of which there are three on the board) also beats me. My plan is to check and fold to any significant bet, as I have almost no potential to improve and very little showdown value on this specific texture.

Flop: 3♦ J♦ 7♦. I hold J♣6♣ — top pair with a weak kicker on a very diamond-heavy board. Heads-up top pair is still good vs a wide checking range, but the texture is dangerous: any two diamonds in Flash’s hand is an immediate flush, and one diamond is a 9‑out flush draw. Flash checked, which can be weakness, a float/draw, or a slowplayed strong hand. I want to charge diamond draws and get value from worse Jx and medium pairs while protecting my hand. A 2/3‑pot bet (~400 into 600) is the right balance — big enough to deny free cards and charge draws, but not so large that I commit myself without reading a check‑raise. If he calls I’ll continue evaluating on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll give most of the range credit for a flush/set and fold unless I have a specific read to do otherwise.

I am facing a bet on a monotone flop (all diamonds) while holding pocket deuces without a diamond. This is a very poor board for my hand. Any single diamond in Minni's range has significant equity against me, and I have no redraws to a flush or a straight. Even if I hit one of my two remaining deuces, it would not protect me against a completed flush. Given that I am out of position and my hand has almost no showdown value or potential for improvement on this texture, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

In Hand #103, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Ah 8c. This is a very strong hand for heads-up play, as Ace-high hands are well above the average starting hand. I have a stack of 19,975, and Minni has 19,875. In previous hands, Minni has been quite active, frequently raising from both positions. My strategy here is to apply pressure and build the pot. A standard 3x raise is 300 total (the 100 I already have in as SB plus 200 more). This allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or see a flop with a lead.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I'm on the big blind with 7♠3♠. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (breakeven equity ≈ 33%). Heads-up the button's opening range is wide, and 7♠3♠ carries some equity and backdoor/flush potential. This is far too weak to 3‑bet for value or as a reliable bluff, but too good to fold routinely in HU vs a standard open. I will defend by calling to see a flop and play post‑flop cautiously: give up to heavy continuation on dry, high card boards; continue when I pair, hit a spade draw, or can realize equity cheaply with position postflop. Call 200 more (300 total committed).

Heads‑up on Q♠ T♣ 2♠ I have 7♠3♠ — a strong spade draw (9 outs). By the river I’ve ~35% to make my flush, plus some extra backdoor/runner equity. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so betting here as a semi‑bluff both gets fold equity against many non‑spade hands and builds a pot I can win when I hit. I’ll make a standard half‑pot semi‑bluff (300) — big enough to fold out some overcards but small enough not to commit my stack. If he raises big I’ll reassess (likely fold), if called I’ll continue aggressively on a spade or re‑evaluate on a blank turn.

On a board of Qs Tc 2s, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot.
