My Ace-high (Ac 8h) has very little equity on this texture, as it contains two broadway cards and a flush draw that align well with the Big Blind's calling range (hands like JT, Qx, and spade draws). Without a spade in my hand or a significant draw, floating this bet is risky despite having position. I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable board or a stronger holding rather than calling with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent.

Heads-up on the button (also small blind) with T♥J♦ — a very playable, aggressive spot. Against a single big blind I want to seize position and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, so I’ll raise rather than just calling 50. A raise to 300 (3x BB) gives good fold equity, prices out some of the worst hands, and is a standard heads-up sizing with deep stacks (~200bb) so we can leverage position postflop. Plan: take many flops as the aggressor, continuation-bet favorable textures, and fold to a large 3-bet — otherwise proceed postflop looking for value and fold equity.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ad Ac), the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. I want to build a pot for value immediately while I have a massive equity advantage. Based on previous hands, Minni frequently opens for 300 and has shown a willingness to call 3-bets (Hand #80, Hand #99). I will 3-bet to 1,000 to maximize value pre-flop and put pressure on her range.

I raised to 300 to seize position, but Flash 3-bet to 1000. I’m on the button with T♥J♦ (offsuit) and both stacks are deep (~200bb), so this hand has good postflop playability and implied odds. Calling 700 more (1000 total) is about 35% break-even equity (700 into a ~2000 pot after my call), which is reasonable heads-up versus a likely wide/aggressive 3‑bet range. 4‑betting would be too thin and folding is overly nitty — I keep position, see a flop, and plan to C‑bet favorable textures or use positional advantage to fold out weaker hands or extract value when I hit. Calling 1000 gives the best mix of pot odds, position, and postflop maneuverability.

On a flop of Ks-6h-Kc, I have Aces which is an incredibly strong hand, but the board is paired with Kings. Since I was the 3-bettor pre-flop, this board actually favors my range (which includes AK). I will lead with a small continuation bet of 650 (approximately 1/3 of the pot). This allows me to get value from pocket pairs like 77-QQ and some Ace-high hands that might call one street, while keeping the pot manageable in case Minni actually holds a King.

Flop is K♠ 6♥ K♣ and I’ve completely missed with T♥J♦. Flash 3‑bet me preflop to 1000 so his range includes a lot of value (AK, KK, AA, QQ, JJ, TT, strong Ax) but his tiny flop bet (650 into a 2650 pot) looks like a wide/standard probe or a small C‑bet rather than an all‑in value line. Calling 650 requires ~19.7% equity (650/(2650+650)). Given position, the cheap price and the likelihood that a decent portion of his 3‑bet range has me beat but some of it is bluff/air, I prefer to call and keep the pot small to see the turn. Raising as a bluff here is too high variance versus a 3‑bettor and folding is too nitty with these pot odds. Plan: call, reassess on the turn — fold to significant aggression if I don’t improve; if I pair a J/T or get a favorable turn I can apply pressure or extract value.

The turn 4 of clubs is a complete brick and does not change the board texture significantly. I still hold pocket Aces, which is an overpair to the board. Since I was the 3-bettor pre-flop and the aggressor on the flop, I should continue to represent a strong range, which includes AK or a King. I will bet 1,600 into the 3,300 pot (approximately 48% of the pot). This bet aims to extract value from medium pocket pairs (77-QQ) that may still believe I am continuation-betting with air or a lower pair. If Minni has a King, she is unlikely to fold, but I must continue to build the pot against the part of her range that I beat.

Flash 3‑bet preflop and has shown aggression on the flop (small probe) and now turns up the pressure with a 1,600 bet on the turn. Pot is 4,900 so calling 1,600 requires ~25% equity (1,600 / 6,500 ≈ 24.6%). I hold T♥J♦ with no pair and no meaningful draw on K♠-6♥-K♣-4♣, so my actual equity versus a continuing 3‑bet/continuation range (KK/AK/AA/QQ and some strong Ax/Kx) is effectively negligible. A raise would be a thin, high‑variance bluff that can get me stacked against value hands; folding preserves a healthy 18,625 stack to pick better spots. This matches my earlier plan to fold to significant turn aggression when I don’t improve, so I’ll fold.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand (King-Ten of spades). In heads-up play, KTs is well above the average hand and should be played aggressively. I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up situation: Flash (button / SB) opened to 300; I’m in the big blind with 3♠8♦. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (300 total). With ~185BB effective stacks this is a deep-stacked spot, but 3-8 offsuit is a very weak hand — poorly connected, few backdoor chances and frequently dominated by the SB’s opening range. Calling gives ~3:1 pot odds (~25%), but my equity vs a reasonable button open is lower (and being out of position postflop makes realizing equity harder). A light 3‑bet to 500 is also unattractive (no useful blockers, small re-raise invites calls and puts me OOP). Long-term EV favors folding and waiting for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with K♣9♣ is a strong, playable hand. I want initiative and to leverage position postflop, so I’ll open-raise rather than just complete. A standard min-raise to 200 applies pressure to the big blind, builds the pot when I have good equity, and keeps my risk reasonable with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or defend selectively); if called I can c-bet favorable boards, especially those with clubs or high cards.

I am in the big blind holding 8-4 offsuit. Minni, who is on the button, has min-raised to 200. While the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my current chip lead and the garbage nature of the hand, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with such a marginal range.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation. J6o is a playable hand in this position. Raising to 3x is a standard play to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand early, especially since I have the chip lead and positional advantage.

Heads-up in the big blind with Kc Th against a button open to 300. Flash’s open is likely wide in heads-up, but stacks are deep (~185bb effective). KTo is a reasonable defending hand but vulnerable to domination by better Kx and Ax hands. I don’t want to 3-bet and inflate a pot out of position or run into a 4-bet with a marginal hand. Calling 200 (300 total committed) gives good pot odds, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me realize equity and play postflop with position disadvantage in mind: look for a K/T or strong draw to apply pressure; check/control on dry boards and fold to heavy aggression.

Heads-up against a wide BTN open. I have top pair (T) with a very good kicker (K) but no diamonds on a 7♦ T♦ 3♦ monotone flop, so my hand is vulnerable to made flushes and to T+Ax combos that beat me. Flash opens wide frequently, so his range contains many worse tens, single pairs and diamond combinations. I want to extract value from worse hands and charge diamond draws while keeping the pot controllable vs a made flush. A half-pot continuation bet is the right balance — big enough to get value and charge draws, small enough to fold to a polarizing large raise. Bet 300 into 600. If he raises big on the flop I’ll give credit to a made flush and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

I have the Jack of Diamonds (Jd), which gives me a strong flush draw on a monotone board of 7d-Td-3d. Minni's donk bet of 300 into 600 is a common play on these boards with either a made flush, a single high diamond, or a pair trying to protect against a fourth diamond. With the Jd, I have good equity and positional advantage. Calling is the best option to see a turn and potentially improve my hand without overcommitting if Minni already has a strong made hand.

I hold Kc Th — the turn K♥ gives me two pair (Kings and Tens). The pot is 1,200 and Flash checked the turn after calling my 300 flop bet. His calling range is wide heads-up: worse Tens, single-pair Kx, one-card diamond draws, some sets/trips or the rare flopped flush. I’m ahead of a large portion of his range (Kx, Tx, medium pairs, one-gappers) and need to charge diamond draws and extract value from worse pairs. A half-pot bet (~600) accomplishes that: it prices in one-card diamond draws, gets value from Tx/Kx, and keeps the pot manageable against hands that beat me. If he check-raises big I’ll give credit to a flopped flush/strong set and fold rather than overcommit.

I have the Jack of Diamonds, which gives me a flush draw on a monotone board. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn with half-pot bets, suggesting she either has a made hand (like a pair of Kings or Tens) or a high diamond. While my direct pot odds (25%) are slightly higher than my equity to hit the flush (approximately 19.5%), the positional advantage and implied odds make a call acceptable. If a diamond falls on the river, I have a strong chance to win a larger pot, and if she is bluffing or has a weak one-pair hand, I still have some narrow showdown value or the opportunity to represent a hand on certain river cards.

I have KcTh — two pair (Kings and Tens) on the river. The board is 7♦ T♦ 3♦ K♥ 5♦: there are four diamonds on board, so any single diamond in Flash’s hand now makes a flush and beats my two pair. Flash opened wide pre and called my flop and turn bets, so his calling range is heavily represented by single-card diamond draws and some two-card diamond combos — the river 5♦ completes a lot of that range. Betting here risks getting raised or called by many hands that now beat me; checking controls the pot and lets me either win at showdown if he checks back or call a reasonable river stab from worse Kx/Tx/medium pairs without diamonds. I’ll check and re-evaluate to his action (call small river bets for value, fold to large river raises that represent completed diamonds).

I have a Jack-high flush on a board with four diamonds. Minni led out on the flop and turn but checked the river when the fourth diamond arrived. This check often indicates a hand that is afraid of a higher flush or a hand that was semi-bluffing or protecting a pair. Since I hold the Jack of diamonds, only the Ace and Queen of diamonds beat me. I should bet for value, as I expect to be called by any lower diamond (9d, 8d, etc.) or potentially a very stubborn pair of Kings or Tens. A half-pot bet is a standard size to elicit a call from weaker made hands.

I have two pair (KTs) but the board shows four diamonds (7d Td 3d Kh 5d) so any single diamond in Flash’s hand now beats me. Flash called my flop and turn bets, which is consistent with both one-card diamond draws (that complete on the river) and some made hands; his river sizing is small (1200 into a 3600 pot ~1/3 pot), which can be either a thin value bet or a bluff/blocked value bet. Calling 1200 to win ~6000 gives >20% pot equity required — my two pair has substantially better equity than that against a wide, partly non-diamond calling range (Kx, Tx, medium pairs, air). Raising would only build the pot when I’m often behind to completed diamonds and would have little fold equity versus flushes. Folding would be too nitty given the price. I’ll call 1200 and go to showdown.

Heads-up on the button with A♦4♥ — a hand I want to play aggressively in position. Opening here takes the initiative, steals the blinds, and gets value from worse aces/high cards while allowing me to play postflop last. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB) — a standard isolation size that gives fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with this marginal holding. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll be inclined to fold, but in position I can continue profitably when I hit an Ace or get favorable runouts.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Given the pot odds (calling 200 into a pot of 400), T8o is a standard hand to defend with in heads-up play. It has decent connectivity and high-card value to see a flop, and I have a comfortable stack lead that allows me to play post-flop without excessive risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h Td. The flop is Js Qc Qh. This board is quite dangerous as it is paired and contains high cards that likely connect with the pre-flop raiser's (Minni) range. My hand currently has no value, though I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 to complete 8-9-T-J-Q). In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first on post-flop streets. I will check to see how Minni proceeds. Betting into a pre-flop raiser on this board is risky, and checking allows me to potentially see a free turn card or evaluate the strength of my opponent's bet sizing.

Flop is J♠ Q♣ Q♥; I have A♦4♥ — just Ace-high with no real draw. Flash checked, and heads-up his calling range to my preflop raise is weighted toward hands that connect very well with this flop (Qx trips/full houses, Jx/sets, medium pairs and broadways). A continuation bet could fold some air, but given the paired board and my lack of pair or backdoor equity I have little showdown value and limited fold equity versus a competent opponent who can check-back strong hands or check-raise for value. I want to control the pot size in position, avoid getting check‑raised or bloated into a big pot with a marginal hand, and see the turn for free to re-evaluate. Plan: check, fold to large aggression on the turn, call smaller bets occasionally to realize equity, and look to bluff or take the pot later only if Flash shows clear weakness.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, an extremely high-card-heavy board (Js Qc Qh Ah). While this Ace generally favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, her check on the flop suggests she might not have a Queen or a very strong Jack. However, my Ten-Eight offsuit has no showdown value and very little equity on this board. I will check to see how Minni responds to the Ace. If she bets, I will likely fold, as I am not in a position to contest this pot with Ten-high against a range that includes many Aces and Broadway cards.

Turn paired my Ace — I now have two pair (Aces & Queens) in position. Heads‑up this is a very strong holding and worth betting for value: Flash’s calling range includes many worse A‑x, medium pocket pairs, and single‑pair hands that will pay a reasonably sized bet, while only a minority of hands (Qx trips/quads, sets, K10 straights, or AQ/full houses) beat me. Both checked flop and turn could be weakness or a trap; I don’t want to give a free river to worse hands, but I also don’t want to overcommit vs a likely check‑raise. I’ll make a firm value bet to extract from worse hands and deny equity to straight/runner draws, keeping the size small enough to fold to a very large check‑raise. Bet 1,200 (≈2× pot). If Flash jams or makes a strong turn raise I’ll put him on trips/straights/full house and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

The turn brings an Ace on an already dangerous board of Js-Qc-Qh. Minni has decided to overbet the pot, wagering 1,200 into 600. While her check on the flop might suggest she doesn't have a Queen, the Ace is a significant scare card that connects well with a pre-flop raiser's range. My Ten-high has no showdown value and my only real equity comes from a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). Calling a 2x pot bet with such low equity is mathematically unsound, especially since I am out of position and would still have to face potential action on the river. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy here is to raise to build the pot and maintain initiative in position. Minni has been playing a relatively standard aggressive style, so a 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and allows me to see how she reacts while retaining control of the hand.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300, K♦8♣ is a reasonable defend from the big blind. With deep stacks (~164bb) and being out of position postflop, I prefer to call and keep the pot controllable rather than make a small 3-bet that commits more against a wide range. Calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives ~33% break-even equity — K8o usually meets or exceeds that vs a wide button opening range. Plan: see the flop and continue aggressively with a king, eight, or strong draws; on dry misses I’ll check/fold to heavy pressure.

I have top pair (K) with a weak 8-kicker on K♥ J♥ 2♣ — heads-up vs the button’s wide opening range this is a hand I want to get value from and protect against Jx and heart draws. I was planning to continue with a king, so I’ll fire a value/protection bet. A half‑pot bet (~300 into 600) charges draws, gets called by worse kings, jacks and medium pairs, and keeps the pot controllable deep-stacked. If Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (he could have two pair/sets or a strong combo); but on the flop this is the right proactive line.

On this flop of Kc-Jh-2c, I have middle pair with Jack of Clubs and Nine of Clubs. Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' could represent a variety of hands, including a King, a draw (like hearts or a straight draw), or even a weaker pair. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (like a King or a better Jack) and might force out bluffs that I want to keep in the pot. Folding is too weak given that I have a solid pair and position. Therefore, calling is the most balanced play, allowing me to see a turn card and maintain pot control while seeing if Minni continues her aggression.

Turn analysis: I have top pair (K) with a weak 8 kicker (Kd8c). The turn (Js) paired the board, which substantially improves any holdings containing a Jack (trip J’s) and creates full‑house combos (e.g. KJ). Flash called my flop bet, so his range contains Jx, Kx and medium pairs/overcards. He checked the turn — that can be either a trap with trips or a pot‑control line with Kx/medium hands. With a vulnerable kicker and deep stacks, betting here mainly exposes me to a large check‑raise by trips/full house and commits chips when I’m behind; there are effectively no meaningful draws I need to charge. I prefer pot control: check back the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the river. If Flash bets the river I will call reasonable, small‑to‑medium sizing for value and fold to large, polarizing raises.

On the turn, the board pairs the Jack, giving me three of a kind. Minni led out on the flop but has now checked the turn. This check suggests she might be wary of the Jack or is potentially trapping with a King. However, with trips and a decent kicker in a heads-up scenario, I must bet for value to get called by a King, a lower pocket pair, or a heart draw. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot is a strong value-oriented sizing that prices in draws while still getting looked up by hands I currently beat.

Call 800. The turn bet (800 into a ~2000 pot) is a medium-sized probe that requires ~28.6% equity to justify a call. Flash called my flop lead and is now betting into a paired board — his range contains a lot of Jx (including trips), Kx and some medium pairs, but also some weaker Kx/overcards and occasional bluffs. With top pair (Kd) and a vulnerable 8‑kicker I don’t want to inflate the pot by raising into a range that contains many hands that currently crush me (JJ, Jx with good kickers, KJ) — a raise is thin and will either get me called by better or fold out worse hands I’d rather keep. Folding outright surrenders value from worse Kx and non‑J hands that I beat. Calling keeps the pot controllable, gets to a river decision, and preserves the ability to fold to large, polarized action on the river while collecting value from worse hands that continue. Plan: call the 800, re‑evaluate on the river — call small/medium bets for value, fold to large polarizing bets or raises.

Board: 2c Jh Kh Js Qd. I hold Kd8c — top pair with a weak kicker. Flash called my flop bet and bet the turn, so his range is heavy in Jx (trips), KJ (full houses), and Kx/Qx; the river Qd improves many hands (KQ -> two pair, Qx pairs). Betting now bloats the pot and either folds out worse kings I want value from or gets me raised by the few hands that beat me (trips/full house). With deep stacks and a checked river, pot control is the correct line: check to showdown and avoid committing more chips with a vulnerable kicker. If Flash barrels a small/medium bet I can call for value; I’ll fold to a large, polarized raise.

On the river, the board is Kc-Jh-2c-Js-Qd. I have three of a kind (Jacks) with a Nine kicker. While trips are generally a very strong hand in heads-up play, this board is now extremely coordinated. The Queen completes several possible straights for any Ten (AT, KT, QT, JT, T9). Additionally, hands like QJ or KJ now have me soundly beaten. Given that Minni called a significant bet on the turn and the river card is a 'scare card' that hits many of her potential drawing hands, betting for value is risky. A check-back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against a King or missed draws, while avoiding a potential check-raise from a straight or a superior Jack.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠10♦ (QTo) and deep stacks (~150bb). This is a strong, playable hand that wants to take the initiative preflop — calling 50 surrenders initiative and lets the BB see a cheap flop with wide range. I’ll raise to a standard 3x-ish open (to 300) to build the pot with position, get fold equity, and define Flash’s range. Postflop I can continuation-bet many favorable boards, control pot size when behind, and fold to large aggression if I miss and face heavy resistance.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six of Diamonds (Ad 6d). Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Ad 6d is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially being suited. I have a significant chip lead (nearly 2:1). While a 3-bet is a viable option for value and protection, calling allows me to see a flop and use my stack advantage to navigate post-flop play without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position. I'm getting 2:1 odds on a call, which is more than enough to proceed.

On a flop of 8d 2d 7c, I have the nut flush draw with my Ace of Diamonds and Six of Diamonds. In heads-up play, the standard move after calling a pre-flop raise from the Big Blind is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Minni acts on this texture. If she bets, I have a very strong semi-bluffing candidate or a hand that can comfortably call to realize equity. For now, checking is the most balanced and strategic play.

I raised to 300 preflop to take the initiative. Flop is 8♦-2♦-7♣ and I hold Q♦-T♠ — two overcards plus a backdoor diamond. Heads-up this is a good spot to continuation-bet: the board is fairly dry (no obvious straights) and many hands that cold-called preflop (broadways, Kx, weaker Ax) will fold to pressure. I want enough fold equity to pick up the pot while charging small pairs and draws, without bloating the pot with just Q-high. Betting ~350 into the 600 pot (~58% pot) balances those goals. If called I will re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; I’ll fold to a large raise unless I improve or pick up a real draw.

I have the nut flush draw on a board of 8d-2d-7c. After checking to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni made a continuation bet of 350 into a 600-chip pot. With over 12 outs (9 diamonds, 3 Aces) to likely have the best hand, and being out of position, this is a prime opportunity for a check-raise. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately if Minni has a marginal hand, and build a larger pot for when I hit my draw. Given my stack advantage, I want to put pressure on her. I will raise to 1100 total.

I c-bet this dry-ish flop (8♦-2♦-7♣) as planned after opening preflop with Q♦T♠ to take the initiative. Flash’s check-raise to 1,100 (I must call 750 more into a 2,050 pot) is large and polarizing — it represents a lot of value hands (sets: 22/77/88, mid pocket pairs turned aggressive) and also some strong diamond-eligible hands (A♦/K♦) or bluffs. I have no pair and only backdoor diamond outs (I’d need both turn+river diamonds for a flush) plus six outs to pair by the river (≈24% to pair by river). The immediate pot odds on a call are ~26.8% (750 into a 2,800 total), so my raw equity is borderline and I’m frequently crushed by the value portion of his range. Given the sizing, polarization of his line, and my earlier plan to fold to large aggression when I miss, folding preserves a healthy stack and avoids playing a bloated pot out of position with only overcards and a weak backdoor draw.
I have Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Facing a button (SB) open to 300 with 8s6c in the big blind. This is a marginal but playable heads-up hand — the BTN's opening range is wide and my immediate pot odds are decent (call 200 into a 400 pot → ~33% required). I prefer to call and see a flop rather than 3-bet bluff because 8-6 offsuit has limited blocking value and I'll be out of position postflop. Plan: proceed cautiously on the flop (fold to big C-bets without pair/draw, float or continue on favourable textures, value-bet when I improve).

I have 8♠6♣ on Q♥5♣4♥. I have no pair and only a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs → ~8.5% to hit the turn, ~16.5% by river). I cannot make a flush with only one club in hand. Flash opened from the button (wide range) and will c‑bet many flops, but my equity is too weak to call a standard continuation bet profitably. Pot is 600 — calling meaningful bets (≈half‑pot or larger) would require equity I don’t have. Plan: check and see how he plays it. I’ll fold to a normal/large c‑bet, but I can consider calling a very small probe or float versus an aggressive c‑better and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet if I improve, check‑fold to continued aggression if not).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Jack offsuit, which remains a strong holding despite not hitting the flop directly. The board (Q-5-4) is relatively dry. Since Minni checked to me, a standard continuation bet of 50% pot is appropriate here to maintain initiative and potentially fold out better hands or simply take down the pot if she missed entirely.

Flash c‑bet 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)=25%). With 8♠6♣ on Q♥5♣4♥ I only have a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs → ~16.5% to hit by the river) plus a small backdoor club possibility (~5%); combined realistic equity is ≈20% — still below the 25% needed. I’m out of position, have no pair, and very little fold equity to justify a call or a raise. Per my preflop plan I’ll fold to a standard c‑bet here to preserve chips and wait for a better, more profitable spot.

I’m on the button (SB) with Q♠4♣. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to play. Heads-up I should be opening a wide range to seize initiative and use positional advantage post‑flop. Q4o is marginal, but raising is standard — it gets folds from worse hands, gives me the lead, and keeps pots manageable. I’ll raise to 200 total (2x BB). If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c‑bet selectively on favorable flops and control the pot on wet boards.

In a heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind and facing a min-raise from Minni. My hand, 7-5 offsuit, is quite weak, but the pot odds are very favorable. I only need to call 100 chips to compete for a total pot of 400, meaning I need 25% equity to make the call profitable. Even a weak hand like 7-5 offsuit generally has enough equity against a standard button opening range to justify a call, despite being out of position. I will call to see the flop and proceed cautiously.

I have completely missed the flop with 7-5 offsuit on a coordinated board of Qd-Ts-8s. I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. Checking is the most appropriate action here to see how Minni responds, as this board likely connects better with her pre-flop raising range.

I raised preflop and have position; the flop Q♦ 8♠ T♥ gives me top pair (Q♠4♣) — not a monster but very strong heads‑up. Flash checked, so betting for value and protection is correct: we get called by worse Qx, Tx, 8x and small pocket pairs, and we deny odds to backdoor combinations. I size up to roughly 60% of the pot (≈250) to extract value while keeping control; if he check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (call smaller aggression, fold to an overbearing shove depending on reads). If he calls I plan to continue extracting on safe turns and pot‑control on dangerous cards.

I have completely missed the flop with 7-5 offsuit. The board of Qd-Ts-8s is highly coordinated and likely connects well with Minni's pre-flop raising range. Given that I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value, I cannot justify calling a bet of 250 into a pot of 650. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 62o, a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead (25,575 vs 14,275), this hand has very little equity post-flop and doesn't play well against the Big Blind's range. Minni has been active and has shown a willingness to play back at raises. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid wasting chips on a marginal starting hand.

Heads-up on the button with A♦3♦ — this is a profitable opening hand in position. I want to take the initiative, isolate the BB’s wide defending range, and build a pot I can play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — a size that pressures light defenders and reduces the frequency of cheap multi-way flops. If Flash 3-bets small I’ll call and play postflop in position; if he 3-bets very large or shoves I’ll fold or proceed cautiously since A3s is vulnerable vs a strong 3-bet range. On the flop I’ll C-bet favorable textures, pursue flush/straight equity, and control the pot when I miss or face heavy resistance.

In Hand #114, I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5c. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. 95o is a very weak hand that does not play well out of position. Given the stack sizes and the need to preserve my significant lead (25,475 vs 14,125), I should avoid defending with marginal hands that are easily dominated or hard to realize equity with post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding pocket eights on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand for heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, as is standard strategy for strong pairs in this position.

Heads-up vs a button opener to 300, A♣5♥ is a hand I want to play aggressively rather than passively out of position. The button’s open is likely wide — I have a top-pair type Ace that plays well three-betting for value and fold equity. I’ll 3-bet to apply pressure, isolate, and take the initiative preflop. Size to ~900 (≈3x the open) gives solid fold equity vs his wide opens but keeps pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, call smaller 4-bets, and if called proceed cautiously OOP (c-bet favorable A/high or dry boards, pot-control on coordinated boards). This line maximizes EV vs a wide SB opening while protecting my stack from being dominated.

I have pocket eights, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni's 3-bet to 900 shows strength, but against a single opponent, a pair of eights is well ahead of a wide range. My goal is to maximize value or realize my equity. Calling keeps the pot controlled while allowing me to see a flop where I can evaluate. However, 4-betting is also viable to put pressure on her stack. Given the stack depths (I have her covered) and the aggressive dynamic of the previous hands, calling is a solid play to maintain pot control and play a flop in position.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♣5♥ so my range is weighted to strong Aces and value hands. Flop J♦ 3♦ A♠ gives me top pair with a weak kicker on a board that’s otherwise fairly dry except for two diamonds — there are obvious 1‑card diamond draws. As the preflop aggressor I should continue for value and protection: I want to charge Kx/Qx/Jx and worse Aces, deny equity to diamond draws, and keep initiative. A size around 2/3 pot (~1200 into 1800) accomplishes that — it extracts value from worse hands while making draws pay to see a turn. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on dangerous turn cards; if he raises big I’ll respect a strong check‑raise/set range and re‑evaluate. Therefore I will bet 1200.

On a board of As-Jd-3d, my pocket eights are now a weak underpair. Minni's pre-flop 3-bet and her 1,200 lead into the 1,800 pot represent a very strong range that heavily includes Aces and Jacks. Given that I am behind almost all of her value range and even some of her semi-bluffs have significant equity against me, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my significant stack lead.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I have 2♥8♣ — a very weak offsuit hand. In heads-up play the button should open very wide to steal the BB and take the initiative; stacks are deep enough that a standard 3x BB open is inexpensive relative to stack and gives good fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (3 BB) to apply pressure and play the pot in position. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet with this hand; if called proceed cautiously — c-bet favorable flops, check dry/connected boards and re-evaluate to avoid getting trapped with a dominated holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc6c, and Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call for a 400 pot). While J6o is a relatively weak hand, we are heads-up and I have a significant chip lead. Defending my big blind against a standard button raise is generally correct to prevent the opponent from opening too wide profitably. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture and Minni's post-flop aggression.

On this dry, unconnected flop of 9h-3c-4s, my Jack-high hasn't connected, but it holds some back-door straight potential and two overcards to the 3 and 4. I am out of position against Minni. Checking is the standard play here to see how she reacts. Since there is no bet to call yet, checking allows me to potentially see a free turn or evaluate her bet sizing if she decides to continuation bet. Given my stack lead, I don't need to force a play on this specific board texture without a piece of it.

Heads‑up I opened wide preflop and the flop (9♥‑3♣‑4♠) is very dry — favorable for a continuation bet. My actual holding (2♥8♣) has no pair and only a few backdoor outs, so I don’t want to build a big pot but I do want to apply pressure to win the pot now and deny equity to overcards. A small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) gives meaningful fold equity while keeping my risk low; I can fold to a sizeable raise and re‑evaluate if he just calls. Bet 200 to continue the initiative and keep the pot manageable.

On this dry 9-3-4 rainbow board, my Jack-high hasn't hit, but I have two overcards to the board's lower cards. Minni has bet 200 into a 600-chip pot, giving me 4:1 pot odds to call. This small sizing is often used as a standard continuation bet with a wide range of hands, including air. Given the favorable odds and the fact that I'm heads-up with a significant stack lead, I will call the 200 to see a turn card. If I hit a Jack or potentially a Six, I may have the best hand, and if she checks the turn, I can evaluate a potential bluff or see a cheap showdown.

I am out of position on a board that has now seen a King. My hand, Jack-high, has not improved and has very little equity. Since I am first to act on the turn, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out into the raiser on a card that likely favors their range (the King) would be risky and likely unnecessary. I will check and see if Minni checks behind or if she continues her aggression.

Check. I opened wide preflop and c‑bet a small 200 on a very dry 9♥‑3♣‑4♠ flop and got called, so Flash’s calling range is wide but contains a lot of showdown hands (9x/4x/3x, small pairs, some floats and occasional Kx). The K♦ on the turn is an overcard to my 2♥8♣ and improves many of his likely calls. He checked to me — ambiguous, but given the flop call I should assume limited fold equity to a second barrel and a real risk of running into a K or a slow‑played pair. I have no pair and very little equity, and I want to keep the pot manageable with position. Checking preserves stack, lets me see the river cheaply, and keeps options open (steal a missed river or fold to aggression). If he bets the turn I’ll fold; if checked to on the river I’ll decide whether a small bluff or a value attempt makes sense based on the river card.

On a board of 9h-3c-4s-Kd-Qc, my Jack-high has no showdown value. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a King, but she could still hold an Ace-high, a Nine, or a small pair that she's looking to take to a cheap showdown. Since I have no real equity and there aren't many natural bluffs in my range besides low straight draws that missed, I will check. If Minni bets, I will fold, as I don't want to risk chips on a complete bluff in a relatively small pot given my significant stack lead.

I hold 2♥8♣ and missed the board (9♥-3♣-4♠-K♦-Q♣). I led small on the flop (200) and was called, so Flash’s range is wide but contains a lot of real showdown hands (9x/4x/3x, small pairs, some Kx/Ax and floats). The K on the turn reduced my fold equity and he checked back turn and river, which looks like pot control or a medium-strength showdown hand — not the type that will fold to a river bluff very often. The Q river is another overcard and doesn’t improve my hand or give a credible blocker to top pair. With essentially no showdown value and poor bluffing prospects versus his line, the +EV play is to check and preserve my stack. If he fires a meaningful bet I will fold.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand for this position and should be played aggressively. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my chip lead against Minni.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up with K♠6♦ in the big blind. This is a marginal but defendable hand in heads-up; calling 200 more (300 total) is cheap relative to the pot and keeps me in against what is likely a wide opening range. 3-betting with K6o is too loose here (poor blockers and OOP postflop) and folding is overly tight. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed if I hit top pair or a strong draw; otherwise be ready to fold to heavy aggression out of position.

I have K♠6♦ on T♥6♥T♠ — middle pair (6s) with a decent kicker but the board is paired with tens, so any Ten (or a higher pocket pair/overpair) crushes me. Flash opened wide preflop heads‑up and is likely to continuation‑bet a lot. Out of position with a vulnerable one‑pair hand, I want to control the pot and induce bluffs rather than build a big pot OOP. Plan: check and call small‑to‑medium c‑bets to extract value from worse Kx/9x hands and fold to large raises/shoves. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (look to check/call or take a free card).

I have flopped trips (tens) with a strong Queen kicker on a board of Th-6h-Ts. This is a monster hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has checked to me. I want to build a pot while the board is somewhat coordinated (the flush draw exists) and ensure I get value from pairs or draws. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to keep her range wide while extracting value.

I have K♠6♦ on T♥6♥T♠ — second pair with a decent kicker but very vulnerable to any Ten, overpairs or sets. Pot is 900 and Flash’s bet is 300 (~1/3 pot). Flash has been opening wide heads‑up and will c‑bet a lot, so this is a good spot to keep the pot controlled and extract value from worse Kx, smaller pairs and some bluffs rather than build a big pot OOP. Calling is cheap (300 to win ~1200) and keeps stronger hands and bluffs in; raising OOP with a marginal made hand is too risky. I will call and re‑evaluate on the turn, folding to heavy/aggressive action unless I improve or get a clear read otherwise.

Board is T♥ 6♥ T♠ 9♣; I hold K♠6♦ (second pair, decent kicker) and the pot is 1,200 with no bet to me on the turn. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — a range that includes lots of misses and medium strength hands. Checking back surrenders fold equity and lets a free river card potentially beat me; raising big is unnecessary and too risky with a marginal made hand. I want a small bite of the pot to accomplish three things: extract thin value from worse Kx and other one‑pair/weak hands, charge any turn/river draws, and keep the pot controllable if I run into a stronger holding. A small bet (~1/3 pot) is ideal — it can take the pot now and, if called, still allows a cheap river decision. If he raises I can fold and preserve my stack. Betting 400 achieves that balance.

I have trips with a Queen kicker. Minni's lead of 400 on the turn after check-calling the flop is often a sign of a marginal hand trying to set a price or a draw. I will raise here to extract value from her draws and any weaker made hands (like a 6 or a 9) while the pot is still growing. It also protects my hand against the various straight and flush draws available on this board.

I have K♠6♦ — second pair on T♥6♥T♠9♣. I sized the turn to 400 to take a small bite, but Flash’s raise to 1,200 is heavy and strongly polarized toward value: any Ten (trips), a 9 (two‑pair), sets/full houses and some very strong pocket pairs — with only occasional bluffs. The pot is 2,800 and it costs 800 to call (you need ~22% equity), and my hand’s equity vs that polarized raiser range is likely well below that. Calling risks committing more on the river to a hand that already beats me; raising OOP with a marginal second pair is too risky. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with K♦4♣. In heads-up play this is a hand I can (and should) open-raise rather than limp or just call — position and initiative matter. With deep stacks (~139BB) I want a standard-sized open that gets fold equity, defines my range, and keeps postflop play manageable if called. I’ll raise to 3×BB (300) to apply pressure to the BB, steal the blind often, and retain maneuverability postflop. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold K4o without committing a big portion of my stack.

I am holding Ace-four offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the strength of an Ace in heads-up play, this is a standard defend. I have a significant stack lead, so I can afford to see a flop and evaluate. 3-betting is an option to take down the pot immediately, but calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to see if I can pair my Ace or find a favorable board texture.

The flop is monotone (all hearts), and I hold Ace-high with no hearts. I have no pair and no flush draw. Since I am first to act post-flop in this heads-up structure, I will check to the raiser. My hand has very little equity on this texture, and I want to see how Minni reacts before committing more chips. With a significant stack lead, there is no need to play a high-variance line on a board that I haven't connected with at all.

I raised preflop and have the initiative heads-up; flop is J♥ 2♥ 6♥ and I hold K♦4♣ — an overcard but no heart. This board is somewhat monotone so there is a non-zero chance Flash has a made flush, but that probability is relatively small and Flash checked, which often indicates air or a marginal holding. I’ll make a standard continuation bet to press my range, deny free cards, and fold out weaker holdings (Qx, low pairs, missed broadways, many Axs). I size to ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) to retain fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if I’m behind. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check to pot-control versus aggressive action, continue-barrel small on safe turn cards, fold to a sizable check-raise which likely represents a flush or a set).

The flop is monotone (all hearts), and I hold the Ace of clubs and Four of spades, meaning I have no hearts and thus no flush draw. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600 pot on this texture is quite standard for a continuation bet, but without a pair or any heart in my hand, I have almost no equity to continue. I am in a strong chip lead position and there is no reason to risk chips on a board where I have completely missed. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In Heads-Up play, Queen-Jack offsuit is a very strong starting hand. I am on the Button/Small Blind and will put in a standard 3x open raise to 300. This builds the pot and exerts pressure on Minni, who has shown a willingness to fold to aggression pre-flop and post-flop in recent hands.

I’m in the big blind with 7♠6♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). Heads-up the opener’s range will be wide, and 7‑6o has decent playability and reasonable equity vs a wide raising range. Calling 200 to see a flop is cheap (pot odds require ≈25% equity), so I’ll defend rather than fold. I won’t 3‑bet with this offsuit, connected hand because out-of-position play with a marginal hand risks bloating the pot and facing 4‑bets; a shove/iso 3‑bet isn’t necessary with deep stacks and no strong read. Plan postflop: proceed aggressively if I hit a pair/straight draw or favourable texture, otherwise fold to big pressure on dry high-card boards.

Heads-up on a low, relatively dry flop (3♠-5♣-9♦). I have 7♠6♦ — no pair but a decent semi-bluff: a gutshot to a 4 and six pair-outs (3 sixes + 3 sevens) — roughly 10 clean outs to improve to at least a pair/straight by river (~38% to hit by river). The opener’s preflop range is wide, so a small continuation bet can take the pot now against many missed hands and charges broadway/overcard holdings. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a modest c-bet (~1/3 pot) to preserve fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. If he raises, I’ll fold to heavy pressure (hand not strong enough to continue); if he calls, I’ll continue aggressively when I hit a pair or the straight.

Minni has led into me for 200 chips on a very dry board of 3-5-9. This is a small bet (1/3 of the pot), and with my Queen-Jack offsuit, I have two overcards that likely provide six outs to the best hand. Given the excellent pot odds (4:1) and the fact that I am in position, calling is the most profitable play. It allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially realize my equity if I hit a pair, or take the pot away on a later street if Minni shows weakness.

Heads-up on the turn I’ve improved to a pair of sevens (7♠6♦ on 3♠-5♣-9♦-7♣). Flash called my small flop bet, so his range is wide (overcards, 9x, small pairs, and some 6/8 or 4/6 combos). My hand is often good but vulnerable to 9s, sets or completed straights. I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge single-card draws while keeping the pot controlled, so I make a medium value/protection bet (~½–⅔ pot). That size gets called by worse hands and floats but doesn’t bloat the pot if I’m behind. If Flash raises big I’ll give credit to a stronger hand/straight and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously (check back on dangerous rivers, value-bet small vs obvious callers).

On a board of 3-5-9-7, Minni has led out for 200 on the flop and followed up with a 600-chip bet on the turn (60% of the pot). With Queen-Jack high, I have no made hand and no draws other than overcards. While I have about 12% equity to hit a pair on the river, the pot odds of 2.6:1 (requiring 27% equity) do not justify a call. Minni's aggression on this specific runout suggests a made hand like 9x or a pocket pair, and I am unlikely to win the pot without improving or executing a high-risk bluff. Given my significant stack lead, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with 4♣4♠ (small blind). With position postflop and deep stacks this is a standard open-raise spot: I want initiative, fold equity, and to define the pot rather than limp and give the BB a free look. A 3xBB-sized open to 300 is consistent with the table, pressures Flash’s wide BB defending range, and still lets me play postflop in position. Plan: c-bet on dry flops to take the pot, slow-play or set-mine if I hit a 4, and fold to a large 3-bet (or proceed cautiously vs a small 3-bet depending on implied odds).

In a heads-up situation, King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand. Minni has been frequently raising from the button, and a 3-bet here allows me to take control of the pot and exert pressure given my significant stack lead. Raising to 900 is a standard 3-bet size that forces Minni to either commit more chips with a wide range or fold.

I opened to 300 on the button and Flash 3-bet to 900. With 4♥4♠ heads-up and very deep stacks (~144 BB), this is a standard set‑mining spot: the 3-bet size is modest relative to stacks so implied odds justify a call. A 4-bet shove is too large for a marginal hand and a small 4-bet is unnecessary — I want to keep the pot controllable and see a flop in position. I will call 600 more (900 total). Postflop plan: if I hit a set extract max value; on dry boards without a set play cautiously (check/fold to heavy aggression or float and try to take down the pot when appropriate).

On a paired board like 5h-7d-5d, the pre-flop 3-bettor generally maintains an advantage as the range consists of many high-card combinations and overpairs that the caller might lack. With King-Queen, I have two overcards to the 7. I will place a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (600 chips) to maintain initiative, fold out her complete misses, and potentially see a free turn or river if she calls with a draw or a marginal hand.

I have pocket 4s and missed a set on this dry, paired flop (5h‑7d‑5d). Flash 3‑bet preflop so his range is fairly wide heads‑up, but his flop bet is small (600 into the 1800 preflop pot; current pot 2400). Calling 600 gives me immediate pot odds of 5:1 (need ~16.7% equity), while I only have ~8.5% to hit a 4 by the river, so I’m not calling purely for set odds. However, the small c‑bet on this board frequently contains bluffs and missed overcards; in position I can float cheaply, control the pot, and look to take it away on the turn if he shows weakness. Raising as a bluff here would overcommit an underpair and is likely to run into 5s/7s/overpairs, and folding surrenders too much given the price and postflop playability. Plan: call (total 1500 committed), check the turn and re‑evaluate — fold to heavy turn aggression, try to bluff/put pressure if he checks or shows weakness, and extract max value if I hit a 4.

The turn 8h makes the board more connected, though it doesn't significantly help my perceived range as the pre-flop 3-bettor. Since Minni called the flop continuation bet, she likely has some part of this board, such as a 7, a small pocket pair, or a diamond draw. With only King-high, betting again is unlikely to fold out her made hands, and I could be building a pot against a 5. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see the river for free to realize my equity.

I opened and called Flash’s 3‑bet with pocket 4s intending to set‑mine. Flop (5h‑7d‑5d) was dry and he c‑bet small; I called to keep his bluffs/draws. Turn is 8h and Flash checks — a clear sign of weakness (missed overcards, floats, or pot‑control). With only an underpair I don’t want to allow a free river or be forced to call large river bets; at the same time I’m not committed without a set. I’ll seize the initiative and apply pressure with a half‑pot bet to fold out air and many one‑pair/overcard hands while keeping the sizing manageable if he check‑raises. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and fold to significant aggression since my showdown value is low.

I have King-high with no draws on a paired and connected board (5-7-5-8). While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of this board, such as a seven, an eight, or a diamond draw. My check on the turn signaled weakness, and her bet of 1500 (half-pot) puts significant pressure on my non-made hand. I have only six potential outs to overcards, which may not even be good if she has a five or a straight. Given my substantial chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal call with just King-high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Qd 3d. This is a suited hand with some playability heads-up. Minni has been quite aggressive lately, but as the button, I should be opening a wide range to maintain pressure and exploit the positional advantage. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to win the blinds or set up a post-flop play.

Td7c is a marginal hand but vs a button open I can defend fairly wide heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200, so calling gives 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). With deep stacks I can realize fold equity and play postflop; a 3-bet is a thin bluff out of position and folding wastes the BB. I'll call and see a flop, folding to heavy aggression if I miss.

I have top pair (T♦7♣) on a 2♦-5♦-T♣ board. Versus a heads-up button open the opponent's range is wide and includes many diamond draws and worse tens/pairs. Checking invites a c-bet and lets draws see cheap cards; betting both gets value from worse tens/some pairs and charges flush/straight draws. Pot is 600 so I size up to ~2/3 pot to deny equity to diamond combos while still keeping the bet small relative to our deep stacks. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I can continue evaluating on the turn; if he check-raises big (pot-sized or larger) I'll heavily discount my hand and likely fold to avoid bloating the pot with second-best top pair.

I have flopped a flush draw with an overcard (Queen) to the board. Minni has led out with a 2/3 pot bet on a 2d-5d-Tc board. While a raise is an option to exert pressure, calling allows me to realize my equity in position and potentially trap Minni if the flush completes on the turn. My hand has significant equity against her range, and keeping the pot manageable for now while maintaining position is a solid strategy.

I have T♦7♣ on 2♦-5♦-T♣-6♣ — top pair with a medium kicker. I bet the flop (~2/3 pot) and was called, so Flash’s range is wide: diamond draws, small pairs, weaker tens and some overcards. The 6♣ on the turn doesn’t complete diamonds and doesn’t change my relative strength much, but I need to deny diamond equity and get value from worse tens/small pairs. Pot is 1,400 — I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (900) to charge draws and extract value while keeping the pot manageable against deeper stacks. This sizing gives flush draws very poor odds to continue (calling 900 to win ~2,300 requires ~39% equity vs their ~20% actual), so it should fold many of their draws and price in worse hands. If Flash check-raises pot-sized or jams, I will heavily discount my hand and fold; if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the board is 2d-5d-Tc-6c. I have the Queen-high flush draw (Qd 3d). Minni has led out for 900 into a pot of 1400. I am getting roughly 2.5:1 on a call (900 to win a total pot of 3200), which requires about 28% equity. My nine outs to a flush give me approximately 20% equity for the river. However, being in position allows me to potentially realize more equity through implied odds if I hit, or by taking the pot away on the river if she checks. Since Minni has been aggressive, a call is the most prudent play to see if the draw completes without risking a large portion of my stack to a semi-bluff raise.

I will check. I have T♦7♣ — top pair with a weak kicker — but the river (2♣) is a nasty card: it pairs the board (giving any 2 in Flash’s hand trips/quads) and completes three clubs on board so any two-club holding now has a made flush. Flash has been sticky (called both flop and turn), so his continuing range contains exactly the hands that beat me (2x, two clubs, Tens with better kickers, and full-house/set combos). Betting here risks getting raised off a marginal one‑pair hand and bloating the pot when I’m often behind; a small bet might get called by worse but will also get value‑raised by better hands. Checking controls the pot and lets me see what Flash does — I can call a reasonable value bet (or a small bluff) but avoid committing to a big pot when behind. Plan: check to induce or get value-cheap showdown; call small/medium river bets; fold to pot-sized jams or large raises.

I missed my flush draw on the river as the board came out 2d-5d-Tc-6c-2c. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, especially considering Minni led out on both the flop and the turn. However, Minni's check on the river suggests she is either giving up on a bluff or has a marginal hand (like a weak 10, a 6, or a 5) that she wants to take to showdown. While I could attempt to bluff to represent the 2 or a slow-played 10, Minni has shown enough stickiness throughout the match that a bluff here is risky. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, checking back to minimize losses and see if my Queen-high can somehow win against a lower missed draw (like 8d-7d or 4-3) is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Q♠2♣. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call. Q2o is a marginal hand, but in heads-up position I should open a wide range and take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure, deny correct odds for easy calls, and play the rest of the hand in position. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on boards that heavily dominate my queen.

I have AKo in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Minni has been raising frequently from the Button/Small Blind. I want to build a pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or dominate her range. A 3-bet to 1,000 is a standard size to put pressure on her and extract value.

Flash's 3-bet to 1000 after my 300 open is a big shove of aggression. Pot is 1300 and it costs me 700 to call (I’d be trying to win 2000), so I need roughly 35% equity to justify a call. Q♠2♣ is heavily dominated by typical 3‑bet value ranges (Ax, better Qx, broadways, pairs) and has poor postflop playability despite position. I also planned to fold to a large 3‑bet. I could consider a rare 4‑bet bluff with stronger blockers, but Q2o isn’t that hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with T2o. This is a very weak hand. In a heads-up situation, while I should be playing a wide range from the button, T2o is near the bottom of my range. Minni has been quite active and has shown the ability to play aggressively post-flop. Given the stack depths and the upcoming hands, I can afford to let this one go and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with such a marginal holding.

Heads-up on the button with A♠8♠ — a very playable hand and I have position. Stacks are deep (~175bb), so I want initiative and to play postflop in position rather than limp. A standard 3x-ish open here both applies pressure and preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll raise to 300 total (3 BB) to isolate the big blind and take the lead. Postflop plan: continue-barrel many textures as IP, use the A-blocker and position to apply pressure; if Flash 3-bets big/shoves I’ll respect the strength and fold, but I’ll often call a small-to-medium 3-bet to play position postflop.

In the Big Blind with K4o, I face a 3x open (300 total) from Minni. The stack sizes are relatively deep (over 170BB effective), and while K4o is a marginal hand, defending the Big Blind against a button open is standard in a heads-up or late-stage tournament dynamic. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary with a hand that has some high-card value but poor playability in large pots. I will call the 200 to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

On a flop of Qd-7h-6d, I have missed completely with my K4o. Standard play in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor is to check and see how they react. The board texture favors a button opening range that contains many Broadway cards, and while I have some backdoor possibilities, there is no reason to lead into the raiser here.

I’m the preflop aggressor on the button with A♥8♠ and position. Flop Q♦-7♥-6♦ is a fairly dry, low-ish board that misses a big chunk of the BB’s calling range (lots of broadways, weaker aces, medium pairs, Kx, connectors). Flash checked, so I can take the initiative. I have an Ace-blocker and only Ace-high, so this is mainly a c-bet for fold equity and to deny equity to small pairs/draws. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (300 into the 600 pot): big enough to fold out a lot of unpaired hands and price some draws, but small enough not to overcommit with just Ace-high. Plan: fold to a large check-raise/shove, call a small raise to play postflop in position and re-evaluate on the turn (barrel many blanks, give up vs heavy aggression or if a diamond/Queen pairs the board).

On a flop of Qd-7h-6d, I have completely missed with King-high. Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot. While King-high can sometimes be the best hand in heads-up play, K4o lacks any significant backdoor potential or showdown value on this texture. Calling out of position against a persistent aggressor like Minni with zero equity or draws is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am holding 3h 2c, which is the weakest starting hand in poker. Given that I have a comfortable chip lead and we are approaching the final stages of the tournament (hand 125 of 150), there is no strategic reason to play the literal bottom of my range, even in position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to exploit my lead.

Heads-up on the button with Q7o — playable but a bit marginal. In heads-up play the button should open a wide range to exploit position and fold equity. Limping/passive call would give the aggressive BB too much control, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (total 300). This sizing is in-line with table norms and puts pressure on Flash to fold weaker hands. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold Q7o (often dominated); if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures (Q-high, dry boards) and play pot-control on wet boards. Versus a small 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies, but raise-to-300 is the correct proactive default here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises. I will put in a 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. A raise to 1,000 is a standard sizing here given her 300 opening raise.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q7o and Flash 3-bet to 1000. The pot is 1300 and calling 700 would make the pot 2000, so I’d need ~35% equity to justify a call. Q7o is frequently dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (Ax, broadways, medium/strong pairs) and likely has less equity than that. 4‑betting would be a thin bluff and would commit a lot more chips (min‑4bet to 1700 requires an extra 1400), which I don’t want to gamble with this hand. Consistent with my earlier plan to fold Q7o to a large 3‑bet, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q4o. Q4o is a weak hand, but in a heads-up situation, it's often worth a raise from the small blind to try and take the blinds or set up a post-flop play. However, Minni has been quite active and sometimes aggressive against my raises lately. Given the stack sizes and the hand's limited playability, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 3♦4♠. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; min-raise is to 200. In heads-up, opening very wide is standard and I want initiative and position postflop. 3-4 offsuit is marginal but playable heads-up and a small standard raise applies pressure, preserves fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (~176bb). Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (this hand has poor equity vs a strong 3-bet); if called, continuation-bet dry, play cautiously on coordinated/overcard boards and extract value when I connect. Raise to the minimum to take the lead and leverage position.

With Ace-four offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a min-raise from the Small Blind. A4o is well ahead of a typical button/SB opening range in heads-up play. Given the pot odds (3:1) and my position, calling is a standard play to see a flop and keep the pot manageable, especially since I have been 3-betting aggressively in recent hands.

On a low, connected board of 8-5-6, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out. Standard play in heads-up is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets a reasonable size, I may consider calling one street given my overcard and the backdoor straight possibilities, but for now, checking is the best way to keep the pot controlled.

Flop is 8c-5s-6c. I'm on the button with 3d4s — I have an open-ended straight draw (need a 2 or 7: 8 outs → ~34% to hit by river, ~17% to hit the turn). I raised preflop, so I still have initiative and Flash’s check suggests a wide, often missable range (overcards, weak pairs, some draws). I want to use fold equity and build a pot when I hit, but I don’t want to overcommit with just a draw. A moderate c-bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) pressures pure overcards and charges other draws while leaving good odds to see the turn if called. I’ll bet a small-to-medium sizing to maximize fold equity and keep the pot manageable — if I get raised big I can fold, if called I can realize my equity and proceed based on the turn.

On a board of 8c-5s-6c, Minni's small continuation bet of 150 into a 400 pot (offering 3.6:1 odds) is a common probe. While I only have Ace-high, I have one overcard and a backdoor straight draw (any 7 or 2/3). In heads-up play, folding Ace-high to such a small bet on this texture would be too weak, as Minni can easily be betting with air or low equity hands. I will call one street to see if the turn improves my hand or if the action slows down.

The Ten of Clubs is a highly dynamic turn card that completes potential flushes (any two clubs) and increases the connectivity of the board (straights like 7-9 were already possible, but now more two-pair and straight-draw combinations exist). My Ace-high has no club and very little equity against a range that calls or bets on this texture. I will check to see Minni's reaction; if she bets, I will likely fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as the board has become too dangerous to continue with just an overcard and a gutshot straight draw.

Board is 8♣-5♠-6♣-T♣, my hand 3♦4♠ — I have an open‑ended straight draw to a 2 or 7 (8 outs, ~17.4% to hit the river). Flash check‑called my small flop c‑bet, showing a wide range (weak pairs, overcards, some draws). The turn T♣ makes the board three clubs, creating flush possibilities and also brings in top‑pair hands (Tx) that will now beat me. Betting here is a semi‑bluff, but if called I have very poor equity and would be committing chips with only ~17% to improve. Pot control and realizing equity is preferable with deep stacks and a dangerous turn. I’ll check behind to see a free river and re‑evaluate based on any river action (call reasonable barrels when price is correct, fold to large river aggression unless I hit).

The river completes a four-flush board with the Ace of Clubs. While I have improved to top pair with the Ace of Spades, the board texture is highly coordinated, making any single club in my opponent's hand a flush. Given that I am out of position and the board contains four clubs, betting into Minni is unlikely to get called by hands I beat and would likely only receive action from flushes, straights, or two-pair hands. Checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a cheap showdown if Minni also checks with a marginal hand or a missed draw.

My open-ended straight draw (3♦4♠) missed — neither a 2 nor 7 came. The river A♣ is a very bad card for me: there are now four clubs on board, so any club in Flash’s hand is a made flush, and an Ace or Ten in his hand are legitimate top-pair/second-pair value hands. I hold no club blocker and have no pair or showdown value, while Flash’s line (check-call on the flop, check turn) is consistent with many hands that will call a river bluff (weak pairs, Tx, or club combos). Betting here has poor fold equity and would be asking to be called by many better hands, so the +EV play is to preserve chips and take the free showdown. Action: check and fold to any significant raise.

I am in the Small Blind with 93o, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my current stack lead and the fact that there are only 21 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to play such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to maintain my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range — position is my biggest edge. 2♠5♥ is a weak holding, but a small preflop raise (to the 2x BB standard here) maximizes fold equity and often wins the blinds outright. The raise to 200 is a low-risk way to take the initiative with a deep stack: if Flash 3-bets I’ll fold (this hand has poor playability), if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet on dry textures and control pot size on dangerous boards. Raising to 200 balances aggression and risk, so I’ll raise to steal the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six suited, which is a premium hand in a heads-up match. Minni has made a minimum raise to 200 from the Button. While calling is an option to keep the pot small and see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and extract value from her wider opening range. Given the tournament stage and my chip lead, applying pressure is effective, as Minni has folded to several of my 3-bets recently (Hands #122, #126). I will raise to 600 total to build the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop.

I opened wide from the button which is fine, but Flash's 3-bet to 600 changes the situation. Calling 400 into the current pot requires roughly ~33% equity to break even; 2♠5♥ is heavily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range and has very poor post‑flop playability, so it won't realize enough equity. A light 4‑bet here would be too thin and riskful with deep stacks and little fold equity, so this is a clear spot to preserve chips and wait for a better hand/positioned opportunity. Fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot with a hand that possesses significant high-card value. My standard opening size in this tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button in a heads-up spot. 5c7c suited has reasonable equity and post‑flop playability against a wide opening range, and the call is cheap relative to the pot (200 more to call into ~400). Stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds, so I prefer to call and evaluate the flop; proceed on clubs/connected boards, and fold to heavy aggression on dry misses.

I have middle pair (5c7c) on a fairly dry 3d-9d-5h flop. Flash opened a wide button range preflop so he will c-bet a lot, but he also has many 9x/3x combos and diamond draws that beat or have decent equity vs my hand. With deep stacks and a marginal made hand I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloating the pot out of position. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, gives me the chance to call small continuation bets, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn (or induce a bluff I can call/raise later). Plan: check here; call reasonable small-to-medium c-bets to see a turn, fold to heavy pressure unless I improve or get a very clear read to continue.

On a flop of 3-9-5, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many turn cards. Minni checked, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure, fold out her air, and protect my hand. If called, I have an overcard and some backdoor straight/flush potential to improve.

Flash opened a wide button range preflop and is c-betting the flop. I have middle pair (5c7c) on 3d-9d-5h — decent showdown value but no diamond draw and a mediocre kicker. Flash's 300 into the ~600 pot is a standard half‑pot c-bet against a wide range that contains many bluffs and worse pairs as well as value hands (9x/3x and diamond draws). With deep stacks I prefer to control the pot and realize equity rather than folding a made hand or inflating the pot out of position with a thin raise. Pot odds are attractive (call 300 to win ~1,200 — ~25% break‑even), so I will call and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to large turn aggression if I miss or face clear strength, but continue/raise only if I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a strong read to bluff.

Check. I have second pair (5s) with a weak 7 kicker on 3d‑9d‑5h‑2c. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains a lot of bluffs/overcards and diamond draws as well as value hands (9x, 3x, and some pocket pairs). The 2c on the turn is a relatively blank — it doesn’t complete any straight and, given the flop contained no clubs, a club flush is actually impossible by the river with only one club currently on board. With pot = 1,200 and deep stacks I want to control the size of the pot and realize showdown value rather than bloating the pot out of position with a thin raise. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets me call small-to-medium bets profitably, and lets me fold to clear strength if Flash turns up heavy. Plan: check; call a small/medium turn bet (roughly up to ~½ pot ≈ 600) because the break‑even equity is only ~25%, re‑evaluate on the river, and only raise here with two‑pair/trips or a very specific read to bluff. Fold to large turn aggression unless I improve.

On the turn, the board is 3d 9d 5h 2c. I have Ace-high and picked up a gutshot straight draw with the 2c. Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. While I could continue to barrel to push her off of marginal pairs (3x or 5x), her call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw (like diamonds). By checking back, I take a free card to try and hit my Ace or my straight draw, while also keeping the pot controlled for showdown in case my Ace-high is currently the best hand. This is a standard play to realize equity in position.

I have second pair (5♣5♥) with a weak 7 kicker on a fairly dry 3♦-9♦-5♥-2♣-T♠ board. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — his range is mixed between missed diamond draws/overcards and some value hands (9x/3x/pocket pairs). With the pot only 1,200 and deep stacks, this is a marginal showdown hand rather than a good thin‑value or bluff candidate. Betting here will often get called by hands that beat me (9s/Tx) and only fold the pure air I already beat, so I prefer to check and realize equity for free. Plan: check to show down; call small‑to‑medium river bets but fold to large river pressure unless I pick up a clear read or improvement.

On the river, the board is 3d 9d 5h 2c Ts. I have Ace-high. Minni has checked for the third street in a row (after calling my flop bet). This sequence of checks suggests she either has a weak made hand (like a 3 or 5) that she wants to take to showdown or a missed draw (like diamonds). Ace-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, especially when the opponent has shown little aggression. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I am unlikely to fold out a 9. By checking back, I can win against missed draws or lower Ace-highs without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button (also small blind) with J♣8♥ — a playable hand in position. The pot is 150 and calling 50 would be passive and allow the big blind to see a cheap flop with a wide range. Table standard and my own heads-up strategy is to open-raise to 2.5–3x BB; that size (300 total) gives good fold equity, isolates the BB, and keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks (~165BB). I’ll raise to 300 total to apply pressure while retaining room to fold to a large 3-bet; if Flash calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on middling textures.

J9o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind in a heads-up match against a 3x open. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the pot odds (200 to win 400) and the hand's potential to hit top pair or straight draws make calling the most standard and effective play.

In heads-up play, as the Big Blind, I act first on the flop. I have middle pair (Jacks) on a somewhat connected board (Qc-Jh-5c). In heads-up matches, middle pair is a relatively strong hand, but leading out here (donk betting) doesn't achieve much; it allows the opponent to play perfectly. Checking to the raiser is the standard play to see how they react to the board. I plan to check-call most standard-sized bets to evaluate the turn, as this board hits a Button's opening range quite well but my pair of Jacks is too strong to fold immediately.

Heads-up c-bet. I raised preflop and flop comes Qc Jh 5c; I hold Jc8h — top pair with a decent kicker and a backdoor club. Flash checked, showing weakness or a float; as the preflop aggressor in position I should pressure a wide checking range and get value from worse Jx, small pocket pairs, and hands with a club or backdoor equity. A bet of 350 (~58% pot) charges many of those hands, maintains fold equity, and keeps the pot controllable if I’m called. Plan: if called I’ll evaluate the turn card and proceed cautiously (barrel good cards or when I pick up additional equity); if raised strongly I’ll fold or proceed based on sizing/reads since top pair is good but not invulnerable on this texture.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Nine kicker on a board of Qc-Jh-5c. The opponent's bet of 350 into 600 is a standard continuation bet. In heads-up play, middle pair is a very strong defending hand. Folding would be much too tight, and raising would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws. Calling is the most appropriate action to maintain the pot size and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly coordinated with the addition of the Ten of Clubs (Tc). I currently hold middle pair (Jacks) and an open-ended straight draw (8s and Kings). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation, providing both showdown value and significant improvement potential.
