However, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor is rarely the optimal play here, as it doesn't allow them to continue with bluffs and could lead to a difficult situation if raised. Checking is the standard and most effective play, allowing me to see Minni's reaction to the turn card and likely setting up a check-call to realize my equity.

I have Jc8h on Qc Jh 5c Tc — top pair with a decent kicker. I was the preflop and flop aggressor; Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn. The turn club makes the board three clubs, which completes some club combinations for the opponent but does not give me a flush (I only have one club). Flash’s check on the turn looks like a medium-strength holding (pair, single jack/ten, missed draw) or a slow-played strong hand. I want to get value from worse Jx/Tx/Qx hands and charge straight/club draws, while keeping the pot controllable given the many hands that can beat top pair (two-pair/sets/straights/flushes). Betting about half the pot balances value and protection: 650 into the 1300 pot prices worse hands to call and forces straight/club draws to pay, but does not overcommit me. If Flash check-raises strongly I will give credit to two-pair/sets/flush/straights and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river based on the card and sizing.

On this turn, the Ten of Clubs (Tc) significantly changes the board texture, completing potential straights and flushes. My hand has improved to a middle pair (Jacks) with an open-ended straight draw (needing a King or an Eight). Given the high coordination of the board and the fact that I am heads-up, my hand has substantial equity. However, Minni's bet suggests she could have a made hand like a flush, a straight, or a superior two-pair. Calling is the most prudent action; it allows me to see the river and potentially hit my straight or improve my hand while keeping the pot manageable. Folding would be a mistake given the straight draw and pair, and raising would likely commit too many chips against a range that currently beats me.

On the river, the board has paired with a Five of Hearts, which is unlikely to have improved either player's hand given the previous action. However, the board remains extremely coordinated with many completed straights (K9, 98) and flushes possible. My hand, a pair of Jacks, has some showdown value in a heads-up context, but it is not strong enough to value bet against a range that likely consists of better made hands or air. By checking, I can see if Minni checks back to realize my showdown value or decide whether to call a final bet based on her sizing and my perceived odds.

I hold J♣8♥ on Q♣ J♥ 5♣ T♣ 5♥ — two pair (J+5). Flash checked the river after calling my flop and turn bets, so his calling line includes Qx, Jx, Tx, pocket fives, some medium pairs and missed draws. The river 5 pairs the board, so hands that now absolutely beat me (5x trips/quads) or have top two pair (Qx/T﻿x) are possible — those hands can comfortably call or check‑raise. Still, I’m ahead of a decent portion of his calling range (worse jacks, some underpairs and sticky single-pair hands) and can extract thin value. I’ll make a modest value bet (~45% pot) to charge worse Jx and get value from sticky hands while keeping the pot controllable against hands that dominate me. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll give credit to trips/full house/quads and fold.

On the river, the board is [Qc Jh 5c Tc 5h] and I hold [Js 9c] for a pair of Jacks. Minni has executed a triple barrel, betting into me on the flop, turn, and now the river with a sizing of nearly half the pot (1200 into 2600). The board texture is extremely coordinated; any King, any two clubs, or an 89 combination has completed a straight or flush. Furthermore, I am losing to any Queen or any Jack with a superior kicker. While my pair of Jacks has some showdown value in a heads-up context, it is effectively a bluff catcher on this specific runout. Given the action history and the number of improved hands in the opponent's range that would follow this betting line, I believe folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead. Calling would require me to be right more than 24% of the time, which is unlikely given the board's connectivity.

I am on the button with Jh 3c. In a heads-up situation, I typically look to open a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and steal the blinds. However, J3o is quite weak, even for heads-up play against an opponent like Minni who has been active and willing to call or re-raise. Given my significant chip lead (22,075 vs 17,775) and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 133 of 150), I can afford to play slightly tighter to protect my lead or choose a low-frequency open. Considering the hand's limited post-flop potential, I will simply fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Heads-up on the button with Jc9c — a strong, suited, connected hand that plays very well postflop and performs well heads-up. I want to take the initiative and build a pot rather than limp/call and give Flash cheap odds. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — that’s big enough to get value from worse Jx/9x and suited connectors, to deny free cards, and to discourage free floats/see-a-cheap-flop plays. I’ve already posted 50, so I’ll put another 250 in now for a total commitment of 300. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a polarized shove/very large 3-bet, or calling/sizing-dependent play if it’s a moderate 3-bet), but with deep stacks I prefer to take the lead and play postflop with position and a playable hand.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. Given the stack depths and the strength of my hand, I have two main options: call and see a flop or 3-bet for value. Looking at our recent history, Minni has been quite active and often folds to 3-bets (as seen in Hand #122, #126, #130). However, I want to keep their range wide and utilize my post-flop edge, or build a pot immediately. I'll choose to 3-bet to 1000 to take control of the hand and extract value from weaker kings, queens, and various suited connectors or smaller pairs in Minni's range.

Facing a 3-bet to 1,000 after my 3x open with Jc9c heads-up, I’ll call to play the hand in position. J9c is very playable heads-up — decent equity vs a reasonably wide 3-bet range plus backdoor club/straight potential — and calling 700 more to win ~2,000 (~35% break-even) is cheap relative to our deep stacks. A light 4-bet here would polarize my range and commit chips without a strong blocker; I prefer to see a flop and use position to control the pot. Plan: continue aggressively on favorable/club-heavy flops, exercise caution on A/K-high boards and fold to strong action if necessary.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting to 1000. The flop is 9h-9d-Td, which is a paired board that provides me with two overcards (K, Q) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). While this board can definitely hit a 3-bet calling range (containing hands like JTs, T9s, or pocket pairs), I need to maintain the initiative. A continuation bet here allows me to represent a high overpair (JJ+) or an Ace-high that is still ahead of her missed connectors. If Minni has a Ten or a draw, she likely calls, but if she has lower pocket pairs or total air, she may fold. I'll bet roughly one-third of the pot to keep the price controlled while applying pressure.

I have trips (9c) on a 9h–9d–Td flop — a very strong hand versus most of Flash’s range, but vulnerable to full houses (TT, T9). Flash’s flop stab is small (650 into ~2650), which looks like a probe/thin-value sizing from a wide 3‑bet range rather than a confident value shove. I want to extract value from worse hands (Tx, K9, J9, A9, KQ, AJ, single-pair hands and draws) and charge any backdoor draws while using position to control later streets. I’ll raise to a sizable number to maximize value and deny correct odds to draws; 3000 is a clear value sizing (well above the min-raise) that prices many worse hands in and forces opponents to pay to continue. If Flash jams big over this raise I will re-evaluate tightly (he’s polarized to very strong hands or bluffs); against a polarized shove I’ll fold only if the shove size/read strongly indicates TT/T9 full house frequency is very high, otherwise I can call smaller/standard 4-bets. Plan: raise now to 3000, take it to the turn if called and re-assess on showdown cards.

I am in a difficult spot after 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting on a 9-9-T board. Minni's raise to 3000 is significant, representing about 4.6x my bet size and a large portion of her remaining stack. This board texture (paired with a high card) is often good for the player who called the 3-bet, as their range contains more 9x and mid-pocket pairs. While I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (Jack), I am currently holding only King-high. Against such a large raise, my equity is likely insufficient to call, especially out of position, as Minni is indicating she is willing to play for her entire stack. I will fold and preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with K8o. This is a solid hand to open in heads-up play. Historically, Minni has been quite active and has won several recent pots, so I need to maintain aggression to protect my stack and not let her dictate the rhythm. Raising to 3x is standard here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I have T♣3♠ in the big blind — a marginal, non‑waterproof hand but playable heads‑up. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed) is the right defensive play: Flash opens very wide often, and the call gives me ~33% pot odds to see a flop and realize equity without bloating the pot out of position. I won't escalate with a thin 3‑bet here (hand has poor blockers and little postflop playability), and I will fold to a large 4‑bet preflop. Postflop plan: continue only if I pair the Ten or pick up a meaningful draw; otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy pressure.

I check. I defended the big blind to Flash's wide button open, but the flop Qh‑5c‑6s completely misses my T♣3♠ — no pair and no real draw (only weak backdoor outs). The board is fairly dry and my hand has poor blockers and little post‑flop playability, so bluffing or raising here is unattractive. With stacks deep relative to the pot I want to control the size of the pot and realize equity cheaply: check and fold to standard continuation bets. I would only continue if I pair the Ten or pick up a meaningful draw; I might call a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) to float against obvious over‑aggression, but otherwise give this one up to pressure.

On a dry, disconnected board of Qh-5c-6s, I have King-high with some backdoor potential. As the pre-flop aggressor, I am in a good position to continuation bet. This board favors my range, which contains more strong Queens and overpairs than Minni's flatting range. A standard bet of 325 (slightly over half-pot) is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on her to have a piece of the board to continue. If she raises, I can comfortably fold, but if she calls, I can check back most turns to see a cheap river or try to improve to top pair.

Fold. On Qh‑5c‑6s holding T♣3♠ I completely missed the flop and have no meaningful draws (only remote backdoor possibilities). Flash's c‑bet of 325 into the pot leaves me facing a 325 call to win 1250 (about 26% equity required). Versus a typical button continuation range (Qx, pairs, overcards and some spade combos) my hand's showdown equity is well below that threshold and I don't block important hands. My preflop plan was to defend the BB but fold dry flops with no pair/draw — with deep stacks and plenty of hands left I should conserve chips for better spots. Fold.

7♦3♣ is one of the weakest hands, but the price to see a flop is tiny (call 50 into a 150 pot) and I have postflop position (button/SB acts last after the flop). Raising to 200 is an option in heads‑up, but with 7‑3o it’s marginal — it buys little fold equity and just bloats the pot when I’m often dominated or have poor equity. Calling keeps the pot small, preserves stack, and lets me use positional advantage to fold to heavy action or bluff selectively on favorable runouts. I’ll call the 50 (total committed = 100) and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Minni has limped in from the Small Blind. K5o is a decent hand to see a flop with in a heads-up situation, especially when there's no additional cost to check. Since I have position post-flop in some contexts (though technically she acts first post-flop as the SB), and the pot is small, checking is the most standard and low-risk play here to see a flop.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a 10-6-5 board. In a limped pot, there's no need to build a large pot with a marginal hand. Since I am first to act post-flop in this heads-up structure, I will check to keep the pot small and see how Minni responds.

I have 7♦3♣ on 6♥ 10♣ 5♦ — no pair and only weak backdoor/paired outs. Heads‑up the flop is pretty dry and Flash checked, so a small continuation bet will often take this down versus missed overcards. I want to apply pressure while keeping risk limited: make a half‑pot c‑bet to fold out better unpaired hands but be able to fold to significant resistance. Bet 100 into the 200 pot; if Flash raises I’ll fold (very poor equity), if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously and re‑evaluate on the turn.

In this limped pot, I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a 10-6-5 rainbow board. Minni's bet of 100 into a 200-chip pot is small, and in a heads-up situation, bottom pair is frequently the best hand or has sufficient equity to continue. I will call the bet to see a turn and evaluate the strength of my hand relative to the board's texture as the hand progresses, keeping the pot manageable with a marginal made hand.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second Ten, giving me two pair (Tens and Fives) with a King kicker. Since this was a limped pot and the board has paired a high card, there is a risk that Minni holds a Ten for trips. However, my pair of Fives is still a decent hand in a heads-up limped pot. I will check to keep the pot small and see how Minni reacts to the paired board. This allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand while potentially enticing a bluff or seeing a free river card.

Check. The turn (Td) paired the board and I still have nothing with 7♦3♣ — no pair and very weak backdoor outs. Flash already called my small flop bet, so his range is wide but contains many hands (top pair, tens, overpairs, floats) that now gained or retained equity when the Ten paired. Betting into a 400 pot with air would be thin: my fold equity is low after he called the flop and I’d be forced to fold to a raise or spew chips when called. Checking controls the pot, preserves my stack (19075 vs his 20525), and lets me see a river for a cheap bluff or fold to aggression. If he checks back I can choose a disciplined river bluff on a favorable card; if he bets I can fold to larger bets and avoid costly mistakes.

On the river, the board is 6-10-5-10-A. While I have two pair (Tens and Fives), this is quite weak in the context of this board. Any Six (Tens and Sixes), any Ace (Aces and Tens), or any Ten (Trips) beats me. Minni's line of limping pre-flop and betting the flop could indicate a marginal hand like a Six or an Ace that she's playing cautiously. Since I have very little value in betting and the Ace is a scare card that likely improved her range or reinforces her lead if she had a Six, I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown.

Check. Heads‑up on 6♥ Tc 5♦ Td Ad I have 7♦3♣ — no pair and virtually no showdown value. Flash called my small flop c‑bet and then check‑checked turn and river; that line is consistent with tens, one‑pair hands (6/5), occasional Ace floats, or other weak calls. The river Ace actually improves a lot of hands in his calling range and reduces my fold equity, so betting into a 400 pot with pure air would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or raised. Checking controls the pot, preserves my stack, and lets me fold comfortably to any meaningful bet. I’ll check and concede the pot to stronger holdings or call only a very small probe (but fold to larger river aggression).

I am on the button with T8s, a strong speculative hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy here is to raise and take control of the pot early. Minni has been active, but this hand has good playability and equity against a wide range.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with 2♣5♦ — essentially the worst heads-up hand. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 (total commit to 300) which requires ~33% equity to break even. Versus a typical heads-up opening range, 2-5o has well under that equity and will be dominated postflop; I also have no meaningful draws and I'm out of position. 3-betting as a light bluff is an option in heads-up play, but with deep stacks and many hands left I prefer to conserve chips for better spots. Fold and wait for a stronger opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Ad8d — a top-tier heads-up hand (ace with a suited 8) where I want initiative. Limping/calling lets the BB see a cheap flop and play back at me; raising isolates and takes the lead postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3x BB / consistent with table sizing) to build the pot and apply pressure. Plan: if called, continuation-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll reassess (fold or pot-control depending on sizing and read) rather than getting committed with a marginal kicker.

94o is a very weak hand to defend in the big blind, even in a heads-up situation. Although the pot odds are 2:1, the hand's playability post-flop is poor, and I'm out of position against an aggressive opponent. With only 13 hands remaining and a slight chip lead, I'll wait for better opportunities to commit my chips.

I am on the button with Qd-7h. This is a mediocre hand in a heads-up situation, but as the small blind, I have a wide range of hands I can raise with to apply pressure, especially given our stack sizes. Minni has been active and occasionally aggressive. However, Q7o is slightly above average for heads-up play and worth a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and win the blinds.

Heads-up, I’m in the BB facing a 3x SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (to 300 total) — that requires ~33% equity to break even on a call. My hand 9c3h is extremely weak and poorly coordinated, and will be dominated by most of the SB’s opening range. I’d also be out of position postflop (SB/button acts last), making postflop play difficult. A small 3-bet to 500 would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and a large investment for little fold equity. Given the pot odds, hand quality, and positional disadvantage, folding preserves stack to pick better spots later.

Heads-up on the button with A♣Q♠ is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Pot is 150 and I can just call 50, but I want to take the initiative, isolate the BB and build value with a premium holding while keeping postflop flexibility. A standard 3xBB to 300 achieves that: it pressures weaker hands, keeps worse calls in, and still leaves deep stack play. If he calls I'll c-bet favorable textures; if he 3-bets I will re-evaluate based on sizing and pot odds.

I am in the big blind with 7d 4c, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3 levels (300). In a heads-up scenario, while defense ranges are wider, 74o is at the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, there's no need to defend such a weak holding against a standard raise.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand. With only 10 hands left in the tournament and a slight chip lead, I want to maintain pressure. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 — their opening range is very wide. I have 9♥7♣ offsuit: a playable hand in heads-up that can make two‑pair, straights, or good top pair – and with stacks ~19–20k calling 200 to see a flop is low risk. Pot is 400; calling 200 gives correct pot odds and preserves flexibility post‑flop. A min‑raise to 500 is just a thin play with a marginal hand and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. I’ll call and evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

I have second pair (9♥ with a 7 kicker) on J♣-9♠-5♠. There are two spades on board and a Jack overcard, so my hand is strong vs a very wide opening range but vulnerable to Jx, Ax/Kx broadways, sets, and spade draws. I’m out of position heads‑up with deep stacks — I want pot control and information. Checking lets Flash c‑bet a wide range so I can call down vs bluffs and medium bets, avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal kicker, and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: check-call reasonable bets; if he barrels very large or check‑raises strongly I’ll fold; if he checks back I’ll consider a small turn probe to protect/extract.

On a flop of Jc 9s 5s, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. This board favors my pre-flop raising range more than Minni's calling range, though it is somewhat coordinated. Since Minni checked, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take the pot down now. If she calls, I still have two overcards to the 9 and 5 and a backdoor spade draw to potentially improve on the turn.

Heads-up, Flash opened wide and his small 300 bet into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot) represents a very wide range (bluffs, small pairs, Jx, spade draws). I have second pair (9♥) with a weak 7 kicker — good showdown value vs a wide c-bet but vulnerable to Jx/strong draws. Calling 300 to win ~1200 gives me ~25% pot‑odds, which is well within the equity my hand has and keeps the pot controlled out of position. Raising OOP with a marginal kicker would bloat the pot and commit stacks unnecessarily; folding would be too tight against such a small probe. Plan: call, see the turn, and re-evaluate — call down vs small/medium barrels, fold to large turn check‑raise or big polarizing shove, and if he checks back consider a small protection/extraction bet on the turn.

I have second pair (9♥) with a weak 7 kicker on J♣-9♠-5♠-4♦. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop with a very wide range (bluffs, Jx, spade draws, small pairs). Pot is 1,200 and there is no bet to call on the turn, so checking gives him a free river card and lets spade/draw hands realize equity cheaply. I want a small protection/extraction probe: a bet ~50% pot will charge spade draws, get called by worse pairs (5x, worse 9x, some Jx and floats) and fold out pure air, while still being small enough that I won’t overcommit OOP with a marginal kicker. If he raises big or jams I will treat it as a polarized action and fold (preserving my stack); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously. Bet size: 600 (≈50% pot).

Minni's decision to lead into me on the turn after check-calling the flop is often a sign of a marginal made hand (like a nine or a five) or a strong draw. The board of Jack-Nine-Five-Four is quite coordinated, and my Ace-high has very little equity if I am behind, likely only having three to six outs. Given that I have a chip lead and there are only nine hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to avoid high-variance spots with non-made hands. Folding here allows me to protect my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠T♣ — a strong, playable opener. With deep stacks (~196bb) and only one opponent in the big blind, I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I'm ahead of the BB's calling range, and apply fold equity. A standard 3x open to 300 accomplishes those goals: it isolates, discourages loose callers, and gives me position postflop. If called I'll c-bet favorable, dry flops and play cautiously on heavy A/K boards; if faced with a large 3-bet I'll fold QTo preflop (or consider a very size-dependent call). Amount is the total committed preflop (includes my 50 SB).

I am in the Big Blind with 93o. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. 93o is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially against Minni who has been aggressive. With only a few hands left in the tournament and nearly even stacks, there is no reason to defend such a weak range. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

K4s is a strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top tier of hands to open from the button. I have been consistently raising to 300 on the button to maintain initiative and pressure the big blind. With only 8 hands left in the tournament and a slight stack lead, I want to keep playing aggressively with my playable hands to protect my position and potentially extend the lead.

Heads-up in the big blind with Jc9h facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to defend (total 300). J9o is a defendable hand in heads‑up; I’d rather call and see a flop than 3‑bet out of position with a marginal hand. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves stack depth and lets me evaluate the flop (continue on favorable J/9 cards or with good draws, fold/defend more narrowly to heavy aggression or a large 4‑bet).

Heads‑up on a three‑heart flop (Qh 7h 6h) with J♣9♥. I don’t have a made hand but I have a very strong flush draw (9 outs) and some backdoor straight possibilities — roughly ~35% to make by the river. The button checked, showing some weakness, so this is a prime semi‑bluff spot to take the pot down now or build it when I hit. I’ll size about half the pot to get folds from many unpaired hands while keeping good equity if called. Plan: if Flash check‑raises large I’ll fold (I’m only a draw); if called I’ll continue/capture equity on the turn (value/raise when I complete the flush, exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on dry turns).

I have flopped the nut flush with the King-high heart. Minni has led with a 'donk bet' of 300 into the 600 pot. This often indicates a piece of the board, a smaller flush, or an attempt to take control of the hand. Since I have the absolute nuts on this board, my goal is to maximize value. Raising now helps build the pot immediately and protects against the action potentially dying if a fourth heart or a board-pairing card appears on the turn. Given our deep stacks and the late stage of the tournament, I want to start inflating the pot now. I will raise to 900 to keep her range wide while still getting value from Queens, sets, or lower flushes.

Board Qh 7h 6h, I hold Jc9h — I have a one-card flush draw (9 outs) and ~34–35% to improve to the river. I bet modestly (300) as a semi‑bluff and Flash check‑raised to 900. Calling 600 into a 1800 pot gives me ~4:1 pot odds (need ≈20% equity), and my draw has substantially more equity than that. Villain’s check‑raise range will include a lot of hearts (made flushes) and strong pairs, but also bluffs — folding here would be too tight given the math, and re‑raising is unnecessary since I’m drawing. I’ll call to realize equity and see the turn: if I complete the flush or pair the jack I can extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I’ll fold to avoid committing too much of my stack.

Heads‑up on the turn: I have J♣9♥ on Q♥7♥6♥ / Q♦. After calling Flash’s flop check‑raise I’m now looking at a four‑heart (one card to come) — 9 outs to a heart on the river = 9/46 ≈ 19.6% to improve. The flop check‑raise polarised Flash’s range toward very strong hands (made flushes, sets, strong Qx) and he checked the turn, which can be a trap or a sign he’s pot‑controlling. Betting as a semi‑bluff here would need a lot of fold equity to be +EV: a half‑pot bet (~1,200) requires >33% folds to breakeven (1200/(2400+1200)), and even a small bet (~600) needs ~20% folds. Given the prior check‑raise, his fold frequency is likely lower than that. With only ~19.6% to hit on the river and a paired board that improves his range, I don’t want to inflate the pot or bluff into a range that calls or raises me off my equity. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity for free. Contingencies: if Flash checks behind we get a free river card; if he bets a small amount on the turn (roughly ≤~600) I’ll consider calling because pot odds justify it; if he fires a larger bet I’ll fold — we don’t have the equity to chase against a calling/raising range.

The turn has paired the Queen. I currently hold the King-high flush, which is the nut flush as the Ace of Hearts is not on the board. While the board pairing introduces the possibility of a full house (QQ, 77, 66, Q7, or Q6), I must continue to bet for value against Minni's likely range of a lower flush or a Queen. Her check on the turn after calling my flop raise could be a trap with a full house, but more often it represents a hand that has become cautious or is looking to check-call. I will bet 1,500 into the 2,400 pot to extract value from her trips or smaller flushes while the board is still relatively safe.

I checked the turn to realize equity with J♣9♥ after calling Flash’s flop check‑raise. Now Flash bets 1500 into a 3900 pot. Math and range reasoning: I have a one‑card heart flush draw (9 outs = 9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit on the river). There are ~5 additional non‑heart outs to pair (J or 9) (~10.9%), so the raw chance to improve ≈30.4% — but many of those improvements (pairs) will still lose to a made flush or strong Qx/sets. Pot odds: calling 1500 to potentially win a ~6900 pot requires ≈21.7% equity to breakeven. My pure flush equity (≈19.6%) is below that, and once you discount losing pair outcomes vs Flash’s polarized check‑raise/c-bet line, my true winning equity is likely lower than breakeven. A reraise would be a thin bluff into a very strong line and would commit too much. With stacks deep and late in the tournament, preserving chips and avoiding a negative‑EV call is correct. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦8♦ — a playable, suited queen with good postflop playability and the positional advantage. With deep stacks and only 50 to call into a 150 pot, I want to take the initiative rather than limp; a standard 2xBB open (to 200 total) is a compact, effective size that gives fold equity, keeps weaker hands in (so I can extract value postflop), and keeps the pot controllable. If Flash 3-bets big I can release and preserve my stack; if he calls I’ll play the flop last and leverage position. Raising also prevents giving cheap equity to random hands and sets the pace of the hand.

I hold Pocket Tens, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has opened for a minimum raise from the button. I want to build a pot and capitalize on the strength of my hand while also maintaining my chip lead as the tournament reaches its final hands. A 3-bet to 600 is a standard value raise in this spot to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I opened to 200 on the button with Q♦8♦ and Flash 3‑bet to 600. Heads‑up Q8s has good postflop playability and with ~18k effective stacks calling is the prudent line — the extra 400 is small relative to the pot and stacks and gives me position and the ability to realize equity (backdoor flush/straight) and outplay on later streets. Pot odds are roughly 400 into an 800 pot (≈33%), so it’s cheap to see a flop. A 4‑bet to 1000 would bloat the pot and should be reserved for stronger hands or a polarized bluff range; Q8s is better played by calling and evaluating the flop. I will call and use position to proceed postflop.

I hold an overpair to the second and third cards on the board, although there is an overcard (the Queen). Having 3-bet pre-flop, I need to continue my aggression to extract value from weaker hands like 9x, mid-pocket pairs (55-88), or draws (spades, JT). A bet of approximately half the pot is standard here to define where I am in the hand and protect my equity.

Board Qs 9c 4s; I hold Qd8d — top pair with a medium kicker and position. Flash 3‑bet preflop, then c‑bet 650 into a 1200 pot (pot now 1850). Calling is the best line: my hand is strong enough to continue (I beat bluffs and worse Qx, and I can realize equity and use position), and the price is attractive — I must call 650 to win a final pot of ~2500 (≈26% pot odds). A raise here would be thin and often dominated by the value heavy 3‑bet range (AA/KK/QQ/AK etc.) and could get me 4‑bet or committed out of position on later streets. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a turn to re‑evaluate; if he checks or I improve I can extract value, if he barrels a big turn into me on a scary card I can fold. Call 650.

The turn brings the Seven of Spades, which is a significant scare card. There are now three spades on the board, and any spade in Minni's range just completed a flush. Additionally, I am still behind any Queen. While I have an overpair to the 9, 7, and 4, the board is becoming increasingly dangerous. Since I do not hold a spade (Tc Td), I have no redraw potential to a flush. At this stage of the tournament, protecting my stack is a priority. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Minni responds. This allows me to see a river cheaper or evaluate her aggression on this wet texture.

Turn completed a third spade (4s Qs 9c 7s) and Flash — who 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop — now checks. I have top pair (Q♦8♦) with a medium kicker but I am vulnerable to made flushes (any two‑spade combos), sets/two‑pair, and better Qx. Betting now risks getting check‑raised by a flush or bloating the pot when I am behind; a small probe could extract from worse Qx but will also turn marginal spots into big commitments. With deep stacks and position, the safest + most +EV line is to control the pot and see the river: check behind, evaluate any river bet by sizing and frequency, call small/medium value bets and fold to large, polarizing aggression.

The river King of Spades is a terrible card for my pocket Tens. There are now four spades on the board, and I do not hold a spade myself, meaning any single spade in Minni's hand completes a flush. Additionally, the King is another overcard to my pair. Since Minni checked back the turn, she could be pot-controlling a Queen, a King, or a small spade. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands. Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can get to a cheap showdown, although I expect to fold to most bets given the board texture.

I check. I hold Q♦8♦ on 4s‑9c‑Qs‑7s‑Ks — top pair with a medium kicker but the turn already completed spade draws and the river put another spade on board. Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and river: his line contains a lot of very strong hands (AA/KK/QQ/AK) and many spade combinations that now beat me. Betting risks getting raised by a completed flush or bloating the pot when I’m often behind; a small probe only gets value from a few worse Qx while exposing me to large losses. With deep stacks and a relatively small pot, pot‑control and taking showdown is the +EV line, so I check behind.

King-Ten offsuit is a premium hand in heads-up play, well within the top 20% of the range. I am on the button and should exert pressure by raising to take initiative and build the pot. A standard 3x open to 300 chips is consistent with my previous pre-flop strategy and puts the decision on Minni.

Facing a 300 open from the button in heads-up with 2♦3♣ in the big blind. The pot is 400 and calling 200 more (total 300) gives about 2:1 pot odds (~33% break-even equity). In heads-up the defender should defend fairly wide; 2-3o has enough equity vs a typical button opening range to justify a call. I won’t bloat the pot out of position with a speculative 3-bet — I’ll call to see a flop and plan to play very cautiously OOP: check-fold to large C-bets on dry boards, but continue with any pair, two pair/sets, or strong draws.

I have bottom pair (2♦) with a very weak 3 kicker on 9♥ J♥ 2♠ and I’m out of position against a wide button opener. There’s been no bet yet, so I’ll check for pot control and to induce a c-bet. Bottom pair has decent heads-up showdown value and calling a small-to-medium continuation bet (roughly ≤~1/2 pot ≈ 300) is fine, but I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or turn over into a check-raise spot. If Flash fires a large bet or raises, I’ll fold unless the sizing is small enough to justify a call; if I improve or the turn is very safe I can start leading then.

On a board of 9h-Jh-2s, I have two overcards and an inside straight draw (to the Queen). As the pre-flop raiser, I have the range advantage on this board. Minni's check is expected, and a continuation bet is standard here to take down the pot immediately or build it if I improve on the turn. A bet of roughly half the pot (325) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and applies pressure on her marginal hands.

Heads-up, my 2♦3♣ is bottom pair (pair of 2s) on J♥9♥2♠ with a very weak kicker and no real draw. Flash’s flop bet (325) is a relatively small c-bet into the pot, so I’m getting good price to see the next card: calling 325 into a 925 pot gives me about 26% required equity (pot ~1250 after my call). Versus a wide button opening/c-betting range this hand has decent showdown value — many of his bluffs/overcards I beat. Raising OOP with a vulnerable bottom pair and weak kicker would bloat the pot unnecessarily and fold out weaker hands I want to keep. Plan: call to keep the pot under control and realize equity; fold to strong turn aggression unless I improve to trips or pick up clear additional equity, otherwise evaluate on the turn for a possible lead or fold.

Check. I have only bottom pair (2s) with a 3-kicker on J♥9♥2♠6♦ and I’m out of position. The turn is a blank for my hand — betting here would bloat the pot and mostly fold out the worse hands I beat while getting called by better hands, so pot control is the priority. By checking I keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs from missed overcards/hearts, and preserve the option to call a small-to-medium barrel for showdown value or fold to large aggression. If Flash fires a small/half-pot bet I’ll call; I’ll fold to a very large bet/raise unless I improve on the river.

On the turn, the 6d is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I have two overcards and an inside straight draw (Queen), giving me roughly 10 outs to likely improve to the best hand. Minni's check-call on the flop indicates she has a piece of the board or a draw. By checking back here, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can potentially induce a bluff on the river or catch one of my outs to value bet. King-high also has some marginal showdown value against her missed draws if the river goes check-check.

Board is 9♥ J♥ 2♠ 6♦ Q♣, my hand 2♦3♣ — only bottom pair with a weak kicker. Preflop Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop (small sizing) then checked the turn and checked the river. That line is consistent with a lot of marginal/medium-value hands and missed bluffs, but it also contains many hands that beat me (Jx, Qx, 9x, pocket pairs). With the pot at 1,250 and both players deep, a river bet here would be a thin bluff: a lot of better hands will call and a lot of worse hands I might fold out (A/K highs) are only a small portion of his range. Betting risks building a pot I’m almost always behind in when called. The low-variance, +EV line is to check and take my showdown equity — preserve stack and avoid turning a marginal pair into a costly bluff. I’ll check to showdown.

On the river, the Queen of Clubs completes a straight for my hand (9-T-J-Q-K). This is the current nut straight as there is no possibility of a flush or a full house. Since Minni checked to me after the turn went check-check, I have a clear value bet. I want to choose a sizing that can get called by a Jack, a Queen, or any mid-pocket pair she might be stubborn with. A bet of 850 into a 1250 pot (roughly 68% of the pot) is large enough to extract significant value while still looking potentially like a missed heart draw or a bluff, encouraging a call from her marginal made hands.

The river bet is 850 into a 1250 pot (pot now 2100); calling 850 to win 2950 requires ~29% equity. I hold 2♦3♣ — bottom pair with a terrible kicker and I'm out of position. Flash opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop (small sizing), checked the turn, and now fires a sizeable river bet. That line is heavily consistent with value (Qx, Jx, 9x, and pocket pairs/sets pot‑controlling the turn and extracting value on the river) with only a minority of pure bluffs (missed heart draws, some K/A highs). My hand only beats pure air and a few missed draws — not enough to clear the ~29% requirement versus his value‑weighted range. Raising would be an expensive bluff with poor blockers; calling is a high‑variance, likely negative‑EV play without a specific read that Flash bluffs here frequently. I’ll fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with A♣7♥ — this is a clear open-shove candidate for value/initiative in heads-up play. I already have the small blind posted (50) and will use position postflop. Raising to the minimum allowed (200 total) is a standard, efficient play: it takes the lead, denies the BB a free flop, preserves stack depth against a 3-bet, and extracts value from worse Ax and many broadway/medium hands. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (fold vs a big isolated 3-bet; potentially call vs a small, non-committal 3-bet), but the default is to open-raise here to seize initiative and keep the pot manageable with a medium kicker.

I am currently in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Trey of Diamonds. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. Although the price to call is only 100 chips and the pot odds are 3:1, T3o is a very weak hand to play out of position. We are on hand 146 of 150, and I currently hold a slight chip lead. My priority in these final hands is to avoid playing marginalized pots with low-equity hands and instead focus on protecting my stack and looking for better spots to utilize my position. Folding here preserves my lead and prevents Minni from potentially outplaying me on a board where I am unlikely to connect strongly.

In a heads-up situation with only a few hands remaining in the tournament, J7o is a reasonable hand to open from the button. I currently hold a slight lead over Minni and have been successfully winning pots with standard opening raises. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the hand with position. If Minni calls, I can evaluate the flop, and if she folds, I increase my lead without much risk.

Facing a button open to 300 with 9♣7♥ in the big blind. The pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 more (300 total) — about 3:1 pot odds. Heads-up the opener’s range is wide, and 9-7o has enough playability to defend cheaply. A min 3-bet to 500 would be a thin, out-of-position bluff that I don’t want to escalate with this hand (and it may invite a 4-bet). Folding would be too tight given the price. I’ll defend and see a flop, keeping my stack flexible for postflop play.

Heads-up on a very dry 7♠-4♥-3♦ flop I have top pair (7♥9♣) with a decent kicker. Flash opened preflop and checked the flop, which leaves a wide range including many missed overcards and small pairs. Betting for value and protection is the best play — I want to get called by worse pairs (3s/4s, smaller pocket pairs, weak 7x) and fold a lot of pure air/overcards, while denying the rare 5-6 straight combos a free turn. I’ll make a ~60% pot c-bet (350 into 600). If Flash check-raises big I’ll heavily discount my hand and re-evaluate (likely folding to a large shove; call a small raise to see the turn), but otherwise proceed to extract value and control the hand.

I have top pair on a very dry board (7-4-3 rainbow). Minni's lead bet of 350 into a 600 pot (a 'donk' bet) could represent a variety of hands, including a smaller pair, a straight draw like 56, or a complete bluff. Given that I have top pair with a Jack kicker, I have a very strong hand in this heads-up context. Calling is the best play here to keep Minni's range wide and avoid folding out her bluffs, while also keeping the pot size controlled in case she has hit a set or two-pair.

I have top pair with a decent kicker (9♣7♥) on a very dry board 7♠-4♥-3♦-2♥. Pot is 1,300 and Flash (button opener) called my flop c-bet — his range is wide (overcards, small pairs, floats, some 7x/4x/3x). The 2♥ is a blank relative to that range: it doesn’t give many new hands that beat me (mostly sets/two-pair) and there are essentially no realistic made straights or flushes yet. Betting again extracts value from worse pairs and overcards that will call, and denies equity to the few turn/river-completing hands. I want a firm but not bloated size — roughly 50–65% pot — to charge calls while keeping the pot controllable with stacks this deep. I’ll bet 800 into 1,300. If Flash check-raises large I’ll heavily discount my kicker and fold to extreme aggression; I’ll call modest action and re-evaluate on the river if called.

I have top pair (Sevens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 7-4-3-2. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn. While the 2h on the turn brings some straight possibilities (56 is now a straight, and A5 has a gutshot), it doesn't change the fact that top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni could be betting with a range of hands, including smaller pairs, straight draws, or air. Raising here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (sets or straights) and fold out her bluffs. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, maintain the value of my hand, and can evaluate the river. I have the chip lead and only 4 hands left, so playing a solid, cautious game to protect that lead is preferred over a high-variance raise.

Heads-up on a dry 7♠-4♥-3♦-2♥-8♠ board with top pair (7♥9♣). Pot is 2,900 and Flash (preflop opener) has been calling my flop and turn bets — a fairly wide, sticky calling range that includes a lot of worse hands (weaker pairs, floats and underpairs) plus a limited number of hands that now beat me (sets/two-pair, A8/88, or 7x with a better kicker). The river 8 does improve some villain combos, but those combos are a minority of his calling range. Betting a small amount (~1/3 pot) is the most +EV line: it extracts value from worse hands that will call, keeps the pot controllable vs the occasional better hand, and is consistent with the line I’ve shown. I’ll bet 1,000 to get called by weaker pairs/floaters and fold if Flash check-raises very large (I don’t want to bloat the pot with just a 9-kicker). If he makes a modest raise I’ll re-evaluate but will generally fold to heavy river aggression.

I have top pair (Sevens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 7-4-3-2-8. Minni has led out on all three streets (triple barrel), which often indicates strength, but her river bet sizing of 1000 into a 2900 pot (roughly 34% pot) is small and offers me excellent pot odds of approximately 4-to-1. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand, and while a straight (56 or A5) or two-pair is possible, folding for this price is too tight. Raising would only likely result in being called by better hands and folding out her bluffs. Since I am the chip leader with only four hands left in the tournament, calling is the most balanced move to maximize my expected value while managing risk.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should open very wide — 9♠3♥ is garbage in multiway play but acceptable as a heads-up steal. I’ll raise to the minimum (200 total) to take the initiative and use positional advantage post‑flop; with deep stacks I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or play a single-opponent pot. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. This is a very favorable price (3:1 odds). Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 148 of 150) and I have a significant chip lead, I should defend my big blind against a small open from Minni, although the hand is weak. Folding is also an option, but the price is too good to pass up against her likely wide opening range.

I have bottom pair (5s) on an A-high board. Generally, the pre-flop aggressor has an advantage on this board texture. I will check to Minni and see how she responds. If she bets, my pair is usually strong enough to call a small or medium-sized bet, but I want to keep the pot controlled.

I missed this flop (9♠3♥ on 5♦ 8♣ A♠) — no pair, no real draws, and the Ace heavily favors the caller’s range. Although I opened wide from the button and can c-bet many textures, this particular board gives me little fold equity versus Flash (who has shown a tendency to defend/call). Betting would commit chips with near‑zero equity and likely be called; I prefer pot control with two hands left in the tournament. I’ll check and see his action: if he checks back I take the pot; if he leads I’ll fold to significant pressure (or consider a very light call only to the smallest stab sizes), preserving my stack for better spots.

I have improved to two pair (Queens and Fives) on the turn. Minni's check on the flop strongly suggests she doesn't have an Ace. I am likely holding the best hand and should bet for value, targeting her 8x hands, lower pairs, or high-card hands that might call one street. I will bet 300 into the 400 pot.

Fold. I checked the turn to control pot with no pair and no meaningful draw; Flash’s lead of 300 into 700 is small but represents hands (A‑x, Q‑x, pocket pairs) that dominate my 9♠3♥. Calling 300 to win ~1000 requires ~23% equity; I only have ~6/46 (~13%) to pair on the river and even then I’ll often be behind. A raise is a pure bluff with poor blockers and unnecessary risk with two hands left in the tournament. Preserve my 16.65k stack and fold to this turn pressure.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament and a significant lead of over 6,500 chips, my primary objective is to minimize variance and prevent my opponent from doubling up. JACK-FOUR offsuit is a weak hand, and while calling the small blind is inexpensive, folding allows me to preserve my stack and ensures I enter the final hand with a commanding lead. There is no strategic reason to risk chips with a marginal hand at this stage.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Tc7c — a playable suited connector in position. Pot is only 150 and calling costs 50 more, but opening is standard heads-up: wide range, take initiative, and build a pot I can play postflop with position. I’ll open to a standard size (300 total) to get fold equity, define Flash’s range, and keep the investment small relative to my 16650 stack. If I face a big 3-bet I can fold or reassess; if called I’ll use position to probe the flop.

This is the final hand of the tournament, and I currently hold a significant lead of 6,800 chips over Minni. My hand, 8h 3s, is extremely weak and unlikely to win at showdown or play well post-flop. Since my primary goal is to maximize my stack and secure my position at the end of the 150 hands, there is no strategic reason to risk any additional chips with a sub-optimal hand. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to protect my lead and conclude the tournament.
